Not eligible for CR/NCR option. Restricted to students in the Drama Specialist and Major. An application and a proposal are required in order to enroll in this course. Please consult the CDTPS Independent Study Policy for further details.
prerequisites: 14.0 credits, including DRM220Y1 with a minimum of 70%, Specialist or Major in Drama, Independent Study Application Form and description of project/syllabus signed by the student and the faculty supervisor, approval of the Centre for Drama, Theatre and Performance Studies (CDTPS). Application deadlines: August 10th for September start date; November 10th for January start date.
difficulty_reasoning: Independent 400-level research project requires sustained self-directed scholarship, disciplinary maturity, and faculty collaboration throughout semester.

name: Introduction to Diaspora and Transnational Studies
description: What is the relationship between place and belonging, between territory and memory? How have the experiences of migration and dislocation challenged the modern assumption that the nation-state should be the limit of identification? What effect has the emergence of new media of communication had upon the coherence of cultural and political boundaries? All of these questions and many more form part of the subject matter of Diaspora and Transnational Studies. This introductory course ex-amines the historical and contemporary movements of peoples and the complex issues of identity and experience to which these processes give rise as well as the creative possibilities that flow from movement and being moved. The area of study is comparative and interdisciplinary, drawing from the social sciences, history, the arts and humanities. Accordingly, this course provides the background to the subject area from diverse perspectives and introduces students to a range of key debates in the field, with particular attention to questions of history, globalization, cultural production and the creative imagination.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory survey course requiring no prerequisites. Conceptual frameworks are accessible to first-year students encountering diaspora studies for the first time.

name: Qualitative and Quantitative Reasoning
description: Focuses on research design and training in methods from history, geography, anthropology, literary and cultural studies, and other disciplines appropriate to Diaspora and Transnational Studies. Prepares students to undertake primary research required in senior seminars.
difficulty_reasoning: Interdisciplinary methods course with accessible frameworks, but demands sustained engagement with diverse research approaches and primary source work.

name: Special Topics in Diaspora and Transnational Studies
description: An upper level course. Topics of study vary from year to year.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level humanities course with variable content; requires sustained reading and analytical writing but no specialist background assumed.

name: Transnational Toronto
description: Toronto is a city increasingly configured through transnational connections and practices. It is a city defined by the scale at which its residents live their lives; a scale that is no longer (if it ever was) parochial, but extends across time and space to connect people and practice across a multitude of locales. Contemporary understandings of Toronto can only be reached through adopting a transnational lens. This course will examine the processes that have produced Toronto as a transnational city over time, including the dynamics of immigration and mobility, experiences of alienation, the global extension of capitalism, and the (re)formation of communities grounded in the complex dynamics of identities produced in a space that is both ‘home’ and away’. We will also explore the specific practices, and connections that produce “Toronto” as a space that transcends its physical geographic boundaries and is continually reproduced in and through the flows of people, capital, objects, ideas, - and the many forces that reproduce and reconfigure these flows.
difficulty_reasoning: Social science humanities course requiring essay writing and critical reading but no specialized prior knowledge or technical skills.

name: Exile
description: Historically used to describe both voluntary departure and enforced banishment from a city, today, "Exile" is perhaps most famously associated with both the Jewish and the Palestinian condition. But exile is also a state of being, one that is creative, critical, and full of meaning-making. This class posits “Exile” as a conceptual framework through which to think through the 21st-century condition of migration and diaspora. Through foundational texts and historic case studies, particularly drawn from East Africa, South Asia, and the Middle East, we will consider how exile has been deployed in order to bring together diverse situations under a unifying theory of both individual and collective experience.
difficulty_reasoning: Conceptual humanities course with no prerequisites, manageable weekly readings, and essay-based assessment typical of 300-level seminars.

name: Citizenship and Multiculturalism
description: This course examines approaches to belonging and distinction that accompany different models of citizenship. What are some historical and recent trends in the intersections of place, custom, and rights? How have governments related social diversity to social justice in theory and in practice? Areas of emphasis will vary, but may include topics such as authenticity and assimilation; ethno-nationalism; immigration and naturalization policy; indigeneity; insurgency; legacies of colonialism; mass media and popular culture; policing and surveillance; racial stratification; transnational markets; and xenophobia.
difficulty_reasoning: Humanities survey requiring close reading and critical thinking, but no specialized background or technical skills needed.

name: Marriage and Family across Nations, Diasporas, and Boundaries
description: How do migration and resettlement affect marriage and family life? This course will explore marital, familial, sexual, and other relational aspects of diaspora and migration, examining how marriage and family become tools for maintaining cultural traditions and gender hierarchies but are also areas of life where negotiation, creativity, and rebellion occur. Transnational in its scope, the course will investigate how marriage and family involve choices but also constraints imposed by communities, cultures, religions, and nation-states. A primary objective of this course will be to interrogate how marriage and family relations consist of choices and constraints that are experienced, celebrated, lamented, and resisted. The class will draw on interdisciplinary approaches and present case studies from a variety of diasporic communities in Canada and other migration contexts.
difficulty_reasoning: Interdisciplinary humanities course requiring reading and critical thinking but no technical background; standard essay assignments.

name: Filth: Dirt and Dehumanization in Transnational Perpective
description: This class will draw on research from anthropology, religious studies, environmental studies, and other fields to examine transnational, cross-cultural, and diasporic perspectives on filth and dirt and how these concepts are deployed to create and reinforce social hierarchies in a multitude of global contexts. Discussions will address such topics as colonial encounters and otherization through ideas of cleanliness; the weaponization of filth in transnational practices of violence; the commercialization and exportation of trash from wealthy nations to the global south; migrants, cleaning/cleanliness, and dehumanization; and impurity, gender, and sexuality in diaspora communities.
difficulty_reasoning: Interdisciplinary humanities course requiring close reading and critical thinking, but no specialized prior knowledge or technical skills needed.

name: Independent Study
description: A scholarly project chosen by the student, approved by the Department, and supervised by one of its instructors. Consult with the Diaspora and Transnational Studies Program Office for more information. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Independent study requires self-direction, sustained research, and regular supervisor meetings; no structured course scaffold.

name: Independent Study
description: A scholarly project chosen by the student, approved by the Department, and supervised by one of its instructors. Consult with the Diaspora and Transnational Studies Program Office for more information. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Self-directed research project requires sustained independent work, topic mastery, and regular supervisor feedback over full semester.

name: Advanced Topics in Diaspora and Transnationalism
description: An in-depth investigation of topics in Diaspora and Transnationalism. Content in any given year depends on instructor. Refer to the Centre for Diaspora and Transnational Studies website (https://www.cdts.utoronto.ca/undergraduate/undergraduate-program/current-undergraduate-courses) for more information.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requiring strong writing and sustained engagement with unfamiliar theoretical frameworks in diaspora studies.

name: Advanced Topics in Diaspora and Transnationalism
description: An in-depth investigation of topics in Diaspora and Transnationalism. Content in any given year depends on instructor. Refer to the Centre for Diaspora and Transnational Studies website (https://www.cdts.utoronto.ca/undergraduate/undergraduate-program/current-undergraduate-courses) for more information.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requiring sustained engagement with unfamiliar theoretical frameworks, but no specialized prerequisites beyond introductory diaspora studies.

name: Advanced Topics in Diaspora and Transnationalism
description: An in-depth investigation of topics in Diaspora and Transnationalism. Content in any given year depends on instructor. Refer to the Centre for Diaspora and Transnational Studies website (https://www.cdts.utoronto.ca/undergraduate/undergraduate-program/current-undergraduate-courses) for more information.
prerequisites: 14.0 credits including DTS200H1/ DTS200Y1/ CJS200H1/ CJS201H1
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar on specialized topics demands prior DTS foundation and sustained critical engagement with complex transnational concepts.

name: Diasporic Foodways
description: Food links people across space and time. As it spirals outward from parochial sites of origin to articulate with new sites, actors and scales, it assumes new substance and meaning in new locales. This movement of food gives rise to new ‘foodways’ to help us to understand the past in terms of temporally connected sites of intense interaction. Food also plays a strong role in shaping translocal identities. As peoples have moved in the world, food has played a central role in (re)defining who they are, reproducing myth and ritual, and bounding diasporic communities. This course seeks to address questions surrounding the dynamics of the food ‘we’ eat, the ways in which ‘we’ eat, the meaning ‘we’ give to eating, and the effect of eating in a transnational world. Recognizing that culinary culture is central to diasporic identifications, the focus is on the place of food in the enduring habits, rituals, and everyday practices that are collectively used to produce and sustain a shared sense of diasporic cultural identity.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level humanities course requiring sustained reading and essay writing; no specialist prerequisites beyond prior DTS coursework.

name: Global Sexualities
description: Sexuality is a complex interplay of desires, attractions, interests, and modes of behavior and has diverse meanings in different societies and cultures. In this course, we will examine the notion of sexuality as well as gender identity and expression from an interdisciplinary perspective that is rooted in ethnography. A cross-cultural study of sexuality and gender identity within global and transnational contexts will provide students with an understanding of how the intersections of culture, community, as well as social and political factors affect individuals’ sexual choices and understandings of gender. A particular focus in this course will be experiences of sexuality and gender within diasporic communities.
difficulty_reasoning: Ethnographic and interdisciplinary perspectives are accessible but require sustained engagement with unfamiliar theoretical frameworks and diverse cultural contexts.

name: Modern Standard Chinese I
description: Intended for students with no background in Mandarin or any Chinese dialect, this course provides an introduction to modern standard Chinese as a foreign/second language. The course consists of mandatory lectures and tutorials. Students study a minimum of 450 Chinese characters. Students must go through screening process conducted by the Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/chinese for details.
prerequisites: Students must go through screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/chinese for details.
exclusions: EAS101Y1, LGGA60H3, LGGA61H3, LGGB60H3, LGGB61H3, CHI101H5
difficulty_reasoning: Daily character memorization and listening drills demand consistent practice; no prior language background makes acquisition slower than content-based courses.

name: Modern Chinese I for Students with Prior Background
description: This course is designed for students who understand elementary Mandarin or any Chinese dialect. The course consists of mandatory lectures and tutorials. Students will learn 600 characters. Students must go through placement process conducted by the Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/chinese for details.

name: Premodern East Asia
description: Examines how various histories of East Asia can be written by focusing on specific themes in the histories of China, Japan, and Korea to roughly 1600. Required of EAS specialists, majors, and minors.
difficulty_reasoning: Survey course with no prerequisites covering unfamiliar history through lectures and essays, manageable with consistent reading.

name: Modern East Asia
description: Examines how various histories of East Asia can be written by focusing on specific themes in the histories of China, Japan, and Korea from roughly 1600 to the Cold War. Required of EAS specialists, majors, and minors.
difficulty_reasoning: Thematic survey of unfamiliar but accessible history requires consistent reading and essay writing without specialist background assumed.

name: Modern Standard Korean I
description: This course is designed to help students build communication skills in the Korean language. Through an integration of listening, speaking, reading and writing, it aims to provide a solid foundation in beginning-level Korean. This course assumes that students do not have any prior knowledge of Korean. Students must go through screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
prerequisites: Students must go through screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
difficulty_reasoning: Language acquisition requires sustained daily practice and memorization of vocabulary, grammar, and writing systems from scratch.

name: Modern Standard Japanese I-a
description: This course is for students with no or a very limited background in Japanese. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Students must go through a screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/japanese for details.
exclusions: EAS120Y1, EAS121H1, EAS122Y0, LGGA80H3, LGGA81H3, LGGA82Y3
difficulty_reasoning: Language learning requires daily memorization and practice; structured drills and homework dominate time outside class.

name: Modern Standard Japanese I-b
description: This course, which is a continuation of EAS151H1, is for students with some background in the Japanese language. All students must go through a screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/japanese for details. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: EAS151H1 (minimum of 60%) or equivalent as determined by the placement process. Students who did not take the prerequisite in the same academic year must take the placement test.
difficulty_reasoning: Continuation course demands sustained vocabulary, grammar, and writing practice; weekly assignments and regular speaking assessments create consistent workload.

name: Consumption, Taste and Culture in East Asia
description: This course explores the roles that consumption and taste play in personal and public lives in East Asia. Course focus may include the cultural histories of food, fashion, tourism, sports, or forms of audio and visual media. (No prior knowledge of East Asian languages or cultures is necessary.) Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Survey-style humanities course requiring no specialist knowledge, with cultural analysis built on observation and discussion rather than technical foundations.

name: Media Worlds and East Asia
description: The term "world-making” is often used nowadays to refer to transmedia storytelling, or the creation of story-worlds across serial narratives in a range of entertainment media, such as novels, games, film series/franchises, television shows, comics, and webtoons. This course looks at the ways that media producers and fans, alike, engage with media worlds. More importantly, the course situates these media worlds within a broader conception of "world-making," namely, the geopolitical and economic configuration of modern East Asia. (No prior knowledge of East Asian languages or cultures is necessary.) Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Survey-style humanities course requiring media analysis and reading, but no specialized background or technical skills needed.

name: Modern Standard Chinese II
description: This course is a continuation of EAS100Y1. Students learn major grammatical structures which are not introduced in the first year and expand their vocabulary. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through a placement process conducted by the Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/chinese for details.
prerequisites: EAS100Y1 (minimum grade 67%)/equivalent as determined by placement process.
exclusions: EAS101Y1, EAS201H1, LGGB60H3, LGGB61H3, LGGB62H3, LGGB63H3, CHI203H5, CHI204H5
difficulty_reasoning: Continuation course builds systematically on EAS100Y1 foundation; grammar and vocabulary expansion require consistent practice and memorization but follow predictable patterns.

name: Modern Chinese II for Students with Prior Background
description: This course is a continuation of EAS101Y1. It focuses on development of students’ listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills at an intermediate low level. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by the Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/chinese for details.
prerequisites: EAS101Y1 (minimum 67%)/equivalent as determined by placement process.
exclusions: EAS200Y1, LGGB60H3, LGGB61H3, LGGB62H3, LGGB63H3, CHI203H5, CHI204H5
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate language course requiring consistent daily practice and weekly assignments; manageable for students with EAS101 foundation.

name: Approaches to East Asia
description: Required of EAS specialists and majors, this course introduces various approaches, theories, and methodologies for the study of East Asian societies and cultures. As the course serves as a foundation for upper-level EAS courses, it should be taken as early as possible, preferably in the second year.
difficulty_reasoning: Foundation course requiring sustained reading and conceptual engagement but no prior specialist knowledge or technical skills demanded.

name: Modern Standard Korean II
description: As a continuation of EAS110Y1, this course is designed to help students improve their skills in the Korean language. Students in this course are expected to perform basic communicative functions, read and write paragraph-level texts, and conjugate verbs/adjectives accurately. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
prerequisites: EAS110Y1 (63% minimum)/ equivalent as determined by the Korean placement interview. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
difficulty_reasoning: Second-year language course demands consistent daily practice, weekly assignments, and cumulative skill-building from prerequisite foundation.

name: Accelerated Modern Standard Korean I & II
description: Covering both EAS110Y1 and EAS210Y1, the course is ideal for self-motivated students who enjoy fast-paced language learning, students with a passion for Korean films and television, students with some listening and speaking skills from prior study or family background, and students taking the full four-year course series. Successful completion of the course fulfils the prerequisite for EAS310Y1. Students must go through screening process conducted by the Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
prerequisites: Students must go through screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
difficulty_reasoning: Accelerated pace compresses two full courses into one year, demanding daily study, memorization, and speaking practice without prior Korean background.

name: Accelerated Modern Standard Korean II
description: As a continuation of EAS110Y1, this course is designed to help students increase their Korean communication skills at beginner high to intermediate low levels. It is ideal for self-motivated students who enjoy fast-paced language learning, and students who have built basic language skills from prior study. Successful completion of the course fulfils the prerequisite for EAS310Y1. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by the Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
prerequisites: EAS110Y1 (70% minimum)/Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by the Department.
difficulty_reasoning: Accelerated language course demands daily practice, frequent speaking assessments, and rapid vocabulary acquisition without specialist background.

name: Reading in Korean Across Genres
description: This course aims to strengthen Korean language learners’ literacy skills by developing reading fluency, comprehension, and critical analysis across diverse genres. Students will engage with authentic texts and practice reflective reading to expand vocabulary, deepen cultural understanding, and sharpen higher-order skills such as interpreting, summarizing, and evaluating. In addition, extensive reading activities, with books chosen by students according to their interests, proficiency levels, and instructor guidance, will foster confidence and independence as readers. By balancing cultural insight with critical engagement, the course supports advanced communication skills and complements oral communication classes. Lectures will be conducted in a mix of Korean and English to facilitate both acquisition and comprehension.
prerequisites: EAS110Y1/ EAS211Y1/ LGGA10H3 and LGGA12H3/ a placement interview
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced language course requiring sustained reading comprehension and cultural analysis; assumes prior Korean study foundation from prerequisites.

name: Cultural History of Food in East Asia
description: This course introduces historical, literary, and anthropological issues related to the consumption of food and/or drink in East Asia. Through a wide variety of reading materials, it focuses on the relationships between food/drink consumption and commerce, religion, social regulation, medicine, and cultural identity, among others.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites required; accessible readings on food history engage curiosity without demanding specialist background or prior knowledge.

name: Chinese Science Fiction
description: Pivoting on the recent and growing global prominence of Chinese science fiction, this course takes the history of science fiction in China as an entry point into studying the literary history of a global genre. The course surveys the Euro-American origin of science fiction, introduces students to the earliest Chinese sci-fi and fantasy literatures, and guides students through major science fiction works produced by modern and contemporary Chinese authors, while cultivating critical skills of close reading and distant reading. The course aims to rekindle the joy of reading and thinking in the literature classroom while demonstrating how science fiction offers fresh perspectives on technology, culture and the future.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites needed; close reading is a standard literary skill taught in high school. Weekly readings and essays are typical humanities assignments.

name: Perceptions of China in Japanese Literature
description: Lectures on Japanese literary negotiations with China, the Chinese and Chineseness, ranging from celebration of shared cultural practices and aestheticization of China to nativist resistance to Chinese cultural hegemony. Required readings are available in English translation, including Tale of Genji, Tale of Middle-Councillor Hamamatsu (medieval romance), Haku Rakuten (No Play), Battles of Coxinga (Kabuki play), Three-Cornered World (by Soseki), and Wild Goose (by Ogai).
difficulty_reasoning: Literature in translation is accessible to newcomers; moderate weekly readings and essay assignments typical for 300-level humanities courses.

name: Introduction to Chinese Philosophy
description: A historical introduction to Chinese philosophy, covering selected figures and texts from the Warring States period through the Qīng dynasty. Schools of thought covered include Confucianism, Mohism, Daoism, Legalism, “Profound Learning,” Neo-Confucianism, and “Evidential Learning.” Texts and thinkers include the Confucian Analects, Mòzǐ, Mèngzǐ, Xúnzǐ, Dàodéjīng, Zhuāngzǐ, Hán Fēi, Guō Xiàng, Zhū Xī, Wáng Yángmíng, and Dài Zhèn.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; unfamiliar texts and philosophical concepts demand engaged reading but remain accessible with consistent study effort.

name: Early Modern Japanese History
description: A survey of the history of Japan from about 1600 until the disintegration of the Tokugawa regime in the mid-19th century. Uses a wide range of translated primary Japanese texts to illuminate the emergence of cultural forms and their conjunction with social, economic, religious, and political trends.
difficulty_reasoning: Survey course with primary texts; no prerequisites assumed, but consistent reading and essay writing required throughout semester.

name: Sinophone Cinemas: Revolution of the Sensible World
description: This course is an introduction to the cinematic avant-garde movements that follow the end of the first Cold War across the Sinophone world, specifically China, Hong Kong, and Taiwan. Taking up the legacies of the Cold War, we examine specific film histories in each region and situate the New Wave movements of the past four decades in a vast terrain that is shaped by the narratives of war, nationalism, colonialism, economic integration, urbanization, among others.
difficulty_reasoning: Film analysis and history are accessible to newcomers; consistent weekly viewing and essay writing create moderate steady workload.

name: Aesthetics and Politics in 20th Century Korea
description: This lecture course examines key questions and texts in the history of literature from the Korean peninsula during the twentieth century, exploring how aesthetic form refracted the experiences of colonialism, division, and the formation of opposing nation-states.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; literary analysis is teachable but demands close reading and essay writing discipline throughout term.

name: Modern Standard Japanese II-a
description: This course is an advanced beginners' level Japanese language course, which is designed for those who have completed EAS152H1 or EAS120Y1 or EAS121H1 with a minimum grade of 67%. All students must go through a screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/japanese for details.
prerequisites: EAS152H1 or EAS120Y1 or EAS121H1 (minimum of 67%), or equivalent as determined by the placement process. Students who did not take the prerequisite in the preceding academic year must take the placement test.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced beginner language course demands daily practice, memorization, and speaking proficiency. Most undeclared students lack sustained language study background.

name: Modern Standard Japanese II-b
description: This course, which is a continuation of EAS252H1, is for students who have a good foundation of lower beginners' grammar, vocabulary and kanji knowledge and have not yet acquired the proficiency required to take EAS350Y1 (previously EAS320Y1). All students must go through a screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/japanese for details.
prerequisites: EAS252H1 (minimum of 60%) or equivalent as determined by the placement process. Students who did not take the prerequisite in the same academic year must take the placement test.
difficulty_reasoning: Language continuation requires daily practice, active production, and cumulative vocabulary mastery with no shortcuts.

name: Chinese Literature (Pre-Qin to Tang)
description: A survey of major works in premodern Chinese literature, including poetry, essays, and short narratives from the pre-Qin through Tang eras (11th century BCE - 10th century CE).
difficulty_reasoning: Survey course with no prerequisites introduces unfamiliar historical texts through guided readings and essays; manageable with consistent engagement.

name: Classical Japanese Literature
description: This course examines canonical literary works written in classical and sinitic Japanese (bungo and kanbun), including poetry, narrative, and theatre, produced from the Nara Period to the Edo Period (roughly 8th to 19th Centuries), with an emphasis on rhetoric, media, performance, reception history, canon formation and gender relations. Themes include: orality and literacy; masculine and feminine discourses; image and text; poetry and narrative; (auto)biography and fiction; intertextuality and authorship; the supernatural and realistic; establishment and resistance; and the centre and periphery. All readings will be in English.
difficulty_reasoning: Literary analysis of unfamiliar texts requires engagement but all readings provided in English translation removes language barrier.

name: Narratives of 19th-Century Korea
description: This course examines the tumultuous events of 19th-century Korea – from peasant uprisings to gunboat diplomacy and from the decline of slavery to the economy’s integration into global capitalism – together with the ways these events have been subjected to the changing and contentious interpretations of historians.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites; accessible historical narrative. Weekly readings and essays require engagement but manageable without specialist background.

name: Popular Culture in East Asia
description: Through various forms of popular culture in the 20th century, this course explores the cultural contexts and social trajectories of China, Japan and Korea. Forms of popular culture studied include art, visual culture, consumer habits, foodways, advertising, music, and fashion.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites; engaging multimedia content requires only curiosity and willingness to read/watch; standard essays manageable.

name: Revolutions in Modern China
description: This course offers an interdisciplinary treatment of the Chinese people’s protracted struggle to transform the world's oldest empire and to create a revolutionary society, with a special focus on the myriad revolutions, uprisings, and protests that had radically remade the country’s social fabric in the tumultuous twentieth century. First-person accounts, classic and revisionist scholarly works, and literary and visual materials will be used to examine China’s multifaceted transformations both chronologically and thematically, in their socioeconomic, cultural, political, and global contexts.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites needed; history survey format with primary sources is accessible. Moderate weekly reading and periodic essays.

name: Environment and East Asia
description: This course introduces environmental issues that are important to East Asia. Or better put, it examines the role East Asia plays in the global environmental crisis. We engage both the factual and humanities' dimensions of Climate Change, biodiversity loss, and other urgent environmental crises.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites required; introductory environmental and humanities content accessible to first-year students without specialist background.

name: Topics in East Asian Studies
description: A study of Chinese, Japanese or Korean culture, history and/or literature. Content depends on the instructor. When offered, the course will have a subtitle that describes its content.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites required. Content-driven humanities course emphasizing reading, discussion, and essays over technical skills or specialized background.

name: Modern Standard Chinese III
description: This high intermediate-level course is a continuation of EAS200Y1. Students learn to read short texts which deal with cultural and social topics, compose paragraph-length essays, and converse in social situations which require exchanges of basic information and generate interpretations. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by the Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/chinese for details.
prerequisites: EAS200Y1 (minimum 70%)/ equivalent as determined by placement process.
exclusions: LGGC60H3, LGGC61H3, LGGB62H3, LGGB63H3, LGGC62H3, LGGC63H3, CHI303H5, CHI304H5
difficulty_reasoning: High-intermediate language study demands consistent practice and cultural engagement, manageable with disciplined weekly effort from students meeting prerequisites.

name: Modern Standard Chinese III for Students with Prior Background
description: This course is the equivalent of EAS300Y1 with an accelerated learning pace. It is for students who have either completed successfully EAS201H1 or reached the intermediate-mid level in speaking, listening, reading and writing by prior study.
prerequisites: EAS201H1 (minimum 70%)/ equivalent as determined by placement process.
exclusions: EAS300Y1, LGGC60H3, LGGC61H3, LGGB62H3, LGGB63H3, CHI303H5, CHI304H5
difficulty_reasoning: Accelerated pace through intermediate Chinese demands sustained daily practice, vocabulary memorization, and regular written/oral assignments.

name: Modern Standard Korean III
description: As a continuation of EAS210Y1 and EAS211Y1, this course is designed to help students improve their Korean proficiency at the intermediate level. The class focuses not only on oral fluency and grammar but also on reading comprehension. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
prerequisites: EAS210Y1 (67%)/EAS211Y1 (67%)/EAS212H1 (67%)/equivalent as determined by placement process
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate language study requires sustained practice in speaking, grammar, reading; manageable for students with EAS210/211 foundation.

name: A History of Japanese Monsters
description: This course examines the historical development of Japanese monsters, from roughly the 7th - 8th centuries to modern times. We focus on how the changing understanding of monsters in society has embodied certain fissures in Japanese culture, especially with regard to gender and class.
difficulty_reasoning: Humanities survey requiring only prior EAS course; weekly readings and periodic essays are standard upper-year humanities workload.

name: Precarious Island: Taiwan in the World
description: This course is a thematic survey of twentieth- and twenty-first-century Taiwanese literature and culture, set in archipelagoes and oceans caught in the global history of colonialism and imperialism, the twentieth century wars, the unfinished Cold War, environmental crisis, and AI chip war.
difficulty_reasoning: Literature and cultural history survey accessible to beginners; consistent reading and essay assignments typical of 300-level humanities.

name: The "Yellow Peril": Past & Present
description: Beginning with the Chinese Exclusion Acts, the Asian presence in North America has often been considered a serious social menace. This course explores the Asian/North American response to the past and present "Yellow Peril" constituted as a gendered, sexualized, classed, and racialized epistemological and affective structure of knowledge.
difficulty_reasoning: Theoretical humanities course requiring close reading and critical engagement with unfamiliar historiographical and cultural concepts.

name: Korean Language in Media
description: This course examines contemporary Korean language through diverse media, including pop culture, news, drama, and film. Students analyze authentic dialogues, expressions, and cultural nuances to strengthen linguistic and pragmatic competence. Instruction emphasizes real-world intercultural communication in modern Korean media contexts.
prerequisites: EAS210Y1/ EAS211Y1/ EAS212H1. If you do not meet the prerequisites, a placement interview is required.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires solid Korean foundation from prerequisites; authentic media analysis and cultural pragmatics demand sustained engagement.

name: Japanese Fiction and the Nation
description: Explores modern Japanese literature, with special attention given to literature's relation to the nation. Students explore how literature transforms throughout Japanese modernity and how its meaning and effects function to simultaneously solidify and fracture national identity.
prerequisites: EAS105H1 and at least one (0.5 credit) course in literature
difficulty_reasoning: Close reading and essay writing on unfamiliar texts require sustained effort but no specialized disciplinary background assumed.

name: Science, Technology and Society in Modern China
description: Learn to understand modern China from an understudied yet important perspective: the development of science and technology since the establishment of People’s Republic in 1949. Science and technology have played crucial parts in China’s political, economical, social, and cultural transformations. Drawing from anthropological, social, and historical studies of science, we examine, among other topics, science and nation-building, biopolitics, technocracy, and scientists’ self-fashioning at the junction of Communist reign and global capitalism. Students also learn key concepts of science studies.
difficulty_reasoning: Humanities focus with unfamiliar China context requires engaged reading; no quantitative or technical skills needed.

name: Cross-Cultural History of Scents and Aromatics
description: This course surveys the importance of smell as a form of knowledge and source of history, and explores the use of aromatics in medicine, food, ritual, and personal fragrance, along with their role as a global commodity in East Asia and beyond. Students will read a variety of medieval and early modern texts about aromatics as well as more recent studies on olfaction.
difficulty_reasoning: Novel subject requires close reading of primary texts, but no prior discipline expertise needed and concepts are intrinsically engaging.

name: Gift, Plunder, and Exchange: Japan and World History
description: This course critically re-evaluates the history and historiography of Japanese capitalism, imperialism/colonialism, and world-empire through the lens of three, distinct “modes of exchange”: gift, plunder, and commodity exchange. Inspired by Kojin Karatani’s The Structure of World History: From Modes of Production to Modes of Exchange (2014), this course explores the emancipatory politics inherent in the critical analysis of modes of exchange, and takes up historical cases from Japan, Hokkaido, Okinawa, Taiwan, Korea, China, and the “Greater East Asian Co-Prosperity Sphere.”
difficulty_reasoning: Theoretical framework and historiography are dense; heavy reading load typical for upper-level humanities, but no specialized prior knowledge strictly required.

name: Modern Standard Japanese III
description: This is a lower intermediate level course for those who have completed EAS253H1 or EAS220Y1 or EAS221H1 with a minimum grade of 70%. All students must go through a screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/japanese for details.
prerequisites: EAS253H1 and EAS220Y1 or EAS221H1 (minimum of 70%), or equivalent as determined by the placement process. Students who did not take the prerequisite in the preceding academic year must take the placement test.
difficulty_reasoning: Lower intermediate Japanese requires consistent daily practice, weekly assignments, and cumulative character/grammar mastery beyond typical 300-level courses.

name: The Art and Politics of Video in Japan
description: This course will study the history of Japanese video art, beginning in the 1960s (when the Sony Corporation released the first portable video cameras) leading to the contemporary moment in which recording devices (phones, surveillance cameras, computers) and new distribution models (the Internet, public projections) abound. Video art is neither cinema nor television, and its early history is marked by some of the most radical artistic and political experiments in the history of modern Japan. This course will focus on the aesthetics and politics of experimental video with an eye on its global flows and Japan’s central role in its development.
difficulty_reasoning: Art history survey with accessible visual primary sources and manageable weekly readings; no technical or specialist background required.

name: China's Cultural Revolution: History and Memory
description: No understanding of contemporary China is possible without understanding the ramifications of the Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution (1966-1976). This course considers this tumultuous episode as a field of historical research and conceptual inquiry. This course critically examines a wide variety of sources, including scholarly accounts, official documents, personal memoirs, oral histories, and literary works.
difficulty_reasoning: Heavy reading load and essay writing demand consistent engagement, but no specialized prior knowledge beyond EAS105H1 is required.

name: Culture of Nature in China
description: The course examines the cultural practice of nature in China’s past and present, focusing on literary, artistic, spiritual, ethical, political, and scientific aspects of human-nature relation. Through scholarly works and primary sources, the course inquires into the cultural politics of human-nature entanglements.
difficulty_reasoning: Literature and art-focused humanities course with manageable primary sources; no technical prerequisites beyond EAS105H1 foundation.

name: Transnational East Asian Cinema
description: This course investigates cinema's border-crossing modes of production, reception, and circulation, to uncover the ways in which the study of cinema enriches current theories and approaches to the transnational. Films and sites to be explored may include Asian co-productions (documentaries, feature films, shorts), transnational genre adaptations (e.g., The Ring/Ringu), and film festivals.
difficulty_reasoning: Film analysis and theory are accessible to newcomers; consistent weekly readings and essays require steady engagement but no specialist background.

name: Special Topics in East Asian Studies
description: A study of Chinese, Japanese or Korean culture, history and/or literature. Content depends on the instructor. When offered, the course will have a subtitle that describes its content.
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on EAS105 foundation; essay-heavy humanities work requires careful reading and writing but no specialized technical skills.

name: Modern Standard Chinese IVa
description: This course aims to develop students’ abilities at an advanced level, with a focus on reading of fiction and non-fiction. Learners develop skills to better express emotions, describe complex scenes, and narrate events and stories. Students will improve their reading comprehension, strengthen writing skills, and advance speaking and listening skills through class discussions and oral presentations. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by the Department. See www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/chinese for details.
prerequisites: EAS300Y1/ EAS301H1 (minimum 70%)/ equivalent as determined by placement process
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced language fluency requires sustained intensive practice in reading, writing, speaking, and listening across complex materials.

name: Chinese Language in Media
description: Chinese for users whose proficiency level is at or beyond the highest level that is currently offered. Students develop linguistic skills and cultural competence to understand and research contemporary Chinese mass media, social media, and public discourse. The language of instruction is mostly Chinese – Mandarin.
prerequisites: EAS401H1/ EAS402H1/ EAS404H1 or self-identified advanced and near-native user of Chinese.
difficulty_reasoning: Instruction in Chinese plus advanced media analysis demands strong prior language proficiency and sustained engagement.

name: Business Chinese
description: This course aims to assist students with upgrading their language proficiency for specialized uses. It focuses on 1) business terms and expressions and 2) formal or semiformal discourse in business. The language of instruction is mostly Chinese – Mandarin.
prerequisites: EAS401H1/ EAS402H1/ EAS403H1 or self-identified advanced and near-native user of Chinese
difficulty_reasoning: Assumes advanced Chinese fluency already; business terminology and formal discourse demand specialized vocabulary mastery and cultural nuance.

name: Korean Language in the Workplace: Academic Internship
description: In this course, students apply their Korean language skills in supervised academic internship placements across education, nonprofit, business, and cultural sectors. Through structured opportunities to use Korean in real-world workplace settings, students develop practical and sector-specific vocabulary, enhance intercultural communication skills, and build professional networks. Students complete 35-40 hours of placement work with a partnering organization, such as local high schools, community organizations, or Korean businesses, in addition to in-class seminars, readings, and assignments. They engage in workplace communication scenarios such as outreach, project coordination, teaching support, or cultural programming. Seminars emphasize goal-setting, professional skills, networking, teamwork, and critical reflection. Students produce a final capstone portfolio and presentation. This course integrates linguistic, professional, and intercultural competencies, culminating in a meaningful, high-impact experiential learning experience. Not eligible for CR/NCR.
prerequisites: Completion of a 300-level Korean course with a minimum grade of A- OR any 400-level Korean language course. Short application and interview required.
difficulty_reasoning: 35-40 hour internship plus seminar assignments and capstone portfolio create sustained output demands typical of upper-year experiential courses.

name: Academic and Professional Korean
description: This course aims to assist students to upgrade their language proficiency for academic and professional purposes. It is ideal for students who seek career opportunities in Korea-related business or in higher education in Korean studies. Emphasis is on comprehension and composition of academic and business texts and preparation for Korean proficiency tests. Students who do not meet the prerequisite must go through placement process conducted by the Department. See www.utoronto.ca/languages/korean for details.
prerequisites: EAS310Y1 (70% minimum)/equivalent as determined by placement process.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced language course requiring fluency in academic Korean and professional composition skills from prerequisite foundation.

name: Border as Method: Rethinking Frontiers in Asia
description: This course rethinks the meaning of borders through literature, film, satellite imagery, museum, and other multimedia materials. Specifically, the course explores media representations of the histories and technologies of borders and maritime frontiers that shape our geospatial understanding of modern East and Southeast Asia. From mountains and rivers to thermal imaging, drone surveillance, and smart walls, borders are both physical and virtual, external and internalized. We will connect the DMZ in Korea, Jeju island, military bases in Okinawa, Taiwan’s offshore islands, Subic Bay in the Philippines, and the artificial islands in the South China Sea.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires EAS209H1 foundation and sustained engagement with unfamiliar geopolitical contexts through diverse media formats.

name: Korean Cultural Studies Seminar
description: This seminar provides an opportunity for in-depth reading and research into a specific topic in the cultural and intellectual history of Korea. Topics will vary each semester but may include colonial period print culture, the New Woman, the history of photography, and modernism.
difficulty_reasoning: Seminar format demands substantive weekly reading and original research essay; no technical barrier but sustained intellectual engagement required.

name: Chinese Cultural Studies Seminar: Land
description: Land as part of the Earth is repeatedly reconfigured in China throughout the 20th century, first by the Reformists and pioneers of the New Culture, then by geoscientists and writers, as well as by economic planners and social thinkers of recent decades. This seminar provides a critical review of this multi-dimensional cultural and conceptual practice of land, as well as its possible role in the era of climate change.
difficulty_reasoning: Seminar format with thematic readings across history, geography, and culture requires sustained engagement but no specialist background assumed.

name: Independent Studies
description: A scholarly project chosen by the student, approved by the Department, and supervised by one of its instructors. Consult the website (eas.utoronto.ca) for more information. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research project requires sustained scholarly output and self-direction without structured course support or scaffolding.

name: Independent Studies
description: A scholarly project chosen by the student, approved by the Department, and supervised by one of its instructors. Consult the website (eas.utoronto.ca) for more information. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Self-directed research project requires sustained independent work, sustained writing, and faculty feedback cycles throughout semester.

name: Independent Studies
description: A scholarly project chosen by the student, approved by the Department, and supervised by one of its instructors. Consult the website (eas.utoronto.ca) for more information. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Self-directed research project requires sustained independent work, writing, and faculty feedback iteration over entire semester.

name: The Global Bildungsroman: Narratives of Development, Time, and Colonialism
description: This course studies Bildungsroman, the story of an individual's coming of age, in the context of twentieth-century political, cultural, and social developments of imperialism, anti-colonialism, human rights discourse, and globalization. Our focus will be novels from the (post)colonial world and theoretical essays on the Bildungsroman form. The course aims to provide a model for rethinking literary history and genres within a global context. Authors may include Yi Kwangsu, Wu Zhouliu, Pramoedya Ananta Toer, Kang Younghill, Tsitsi Dangarembga, Camara Laye, amongst others.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level literary analysis with unfamiliar non-Western authors and postcolonial theory requires sustained engagement but remains accessible.

name: Fascism and Japan
description: This seminar analyzes fascism in modern Japan: its ideology, theories, and practices. We define fascism as a symptom of imperialism, and also explore the critique of fascism in Japan by Japanese thinkers on the Left, such as Tosaka Jun, whose Japanese Ideology represents one of the most trenchant critiques of fascism.
difficulty_reasoning: Seminar demands close reading of challenging political theory and historiography without assuming prior East Asian or fascism expertise.

name: Modern Standard Japanese IV-a
description: This is an upper intermediate level Japanese course that focuses on reading and writing skills for those who have completed EAS350Y1 (previously EAS320Y1) with a minimum grade of 70%. Native or near-native speakers are not permitted to take this course. All students must go through a screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/japanese for details.
prerequisites: EAS350Y1 or EAS320Y1 (minimum of 67%), or equivalent as determined by the placement process. Students who did not take the prerequisite in the preceding academic year must take the placement test.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-intermediate language requires sustained daily practice, active production skills, and cumulative mastery of complex grammar and kanji.

name: Modern Standard Japanese IV-b
description: This is an upper intermediate level Japanese course that focuses on oral/aural communication for those who have completed EAS350Y1 (previously EAS320Y1) with a minimum grade of 70%. Native or near-native speakers are not permitted to take this course. All students must go through a screening process conducted by the Department. See https://www.eas.utoronto.ca/languages/japanese for details.
prerequisites: EAS350Y1 or EAS320Y1 (minimum of 67%), or equivalent as determined by the placement process. Students who did not take the prerequisite in the preceding academic year must take the placement test.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-intermediate language course demands sustained daily practice, conversation fluency, and cultural competence beyond introductory foundations.

name: Post-Fukushima Literature and Culture
description: The course examines literature, film, and other cultural products that emerged in the aftermath of the earthquake and tsunami that hit Northeast Japan on March 11, 2011, as well as in the ensuing nuclear crisis at the Fukushima Tokyo Daiichi powerplant. We will explore the distinctive aesthetic forms and imaginaries that responded to such natural and human-made disasters.
prerequisites: EAS209H1, EAS320Y1 (or its equivalent Japanese language proficiency and higher), an additional 1.0 credit in EAS courses at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Requires strong Japanese language proficiency and cultural literacy built through prerequisites; content assumes disciplinary maturity.

name: Advanced Topics in East Asian Studies
description: An in-depth study of Chinese, Japanese or Korean culture, history and/or literature. Content depends on the instructor. When offered, the course will have a subtitle that describes its content.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar demands strong EAS209 foundation, close textual analysis, and sustained research essay without specialist background.

name: First-Year Learning Community
department: ASDN: Arts and Science, Office of the Dean
difficulty_reasoning: Learning community format emphasizes peer support and adjustment to university life rather than demanding subject matter.

name: Principles of Microeconomics
description: An introduction to economic analysis and its applications: price determination, market structure, decision making by individuals and firms, public policy. NOTE: extensive use of graphical and quantitative analysis.
exclusions: ECO105Y1, ECO100Y5, ECO101H5, MGEA01H3, MGEA02H3, PPG201H1
recommended_preparation: MCV4U (Calculus & Vectors) and MHF4U (Advanced Functions), or equivalent secondary school mathematics credits
difficulty_reasoning: Graphical and quantitative analysis requires sustained engagement; weekly problem sets demand consistent effort but remain accessible without prior economics background.

name: Principles of Macroeconomics
description: An introduction to economic analysis and its applications from a macroeconomic (economy-wide) perspective. Topics covered include international trade and finance, role of money and the banking system, monetary and fiscal policy. Note: graphical and quantitative analysis are used extensively.
recommended_preparation: MCV4U (Calculus & Vectors) and MHF4U (Advanced Functions), or equivalent secondary school mathematics credits
difficulty_reasoning: Graphical analysis and quantitative problem-sets demand consistent effort; concepts are foundational but require active engagement.

name: Principles of Economics for Non-Specialists
description: Fundamentals for consumers, businesses, citizens. Microeconomics focuses on cost/benefit analysis: gains from trade, price coordination, competition/monopoly, efficiency/equity tradeoffs, government/market failures, environmental policies, income/wealth distributions. Macroeconomics focuses on: GDP growth, unemployment, inflation, monetary/fiscal policies, business cycles, exchange rates, government deficits/debt, globalization. Emphasizes economic literacy, fewer mathematical tools than ECO101H1, ECO102H1.
exclusions: ECO101H1, ECO102H1, ECO100Y5, ECO101H5, ECO102H5, MGEA01H3, MGEA02H3, MGEA05H3, MGEA06H3
difficulty_reasoning: Non-specialist course with minimal math; conceptual foundations approachable for any curious first-year student without prior economics background.

name: Microeconomic Theory
description: Theory of markets and prices. Determination of prices through the interaction of the basic economic units, the household as consumer and as supplier of inputs and the business firm as producer and as employer of inputs. The pricing system as the mechanism by which social decisions and allocation of goods are made in a market economy.
exclusions: ECO204Y1, ECO206Y1, ECO200Y5, ECO204Y5, ECO205Y5, ECO206Y5, MGEB01H3, MGEB02H3, MGEC02H3
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate math-based theory course with regular problem sets; builds on first-year econ and calculus but remains accessible with consistent effort.

name: Macroeconomic Theory and Policy
description: Theory of output, employment and the price level; techniques for achieving economic stability; central banking and Canadian financial institutions and markets; foreign exchange markets and the exchange rate. This course is not intended for students enrolled in the BCom program; please see ECO209Y1.
exclusions: ECO208Y1, ECO209Y1, ECO202Y5, ECO208Y5, ECO209Y5, MGEB05H3, MGEB06H3, MGEC06H3
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate theory and math; builds on intro econ prerequisites; requires consistent problem-solving effort but remains accessible.

name: Microeconomic Theory and Applications (for Commerce)
description: The use of microeconomics to analyze a variety of issues from marketing and finance to organizational structure. Consumer preferences and behaviour; demand, cost analysis and estimation; allocation of inputs, pricing and firm behaviour under perfect and imperfect competition; game theory and public policy, including competition policy. Business cases are used to connect theory and practice and to highlight differences and similarities between economics and accounting, marketing and finance. This course is restricted to students in the Commerce programs.
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate theory with math foundations required; problem sets and business case analysis demand consistent engagement but remain accessible.

name: Microeconomic Theory
description: This course deals more rigorously and more mathematically with the topics included in ECO200Y1 and is intended primarily for students in certain Economics Specialist programs.
exclusions: ECO200Y1, ECO204Y1, ECO200Y5, ECO204Y5, ECO205Y5, ECO206Y5, MGEB01H3, MGEB02H3, MGEC02H3
difficulty_reasoning: Mathematical rigor and optimization theory demand strong calculus foundations most undeclared students lack without specialist preparation.

name: Macroeconomic Theory
description: This course deals more rigorously and more mathematically with the topics included in ECO202Y1 and is intended primarily for students in certain Economics Specialist programs.
exclusions: ECO202Y1, ECO209Y1, ECO202Y5, ECO208Y5, ECO209Y5, MGEB05H3, MGEB06H3, MGEC06H3
difficulty_reasoning: Mathematical rigor and cumulative theory demand sustained engagement; builds heavily on prior calculus and economics foundation.

name: Macroeconomic Theory and Policy (for Commerce)
description: Macroeconomic issues relevant for commerce students. Analytical tools are used to examine policy issues: Canadian government budgets, Bank of Canada monetary policy, exchange rate policy, foreign trade policy and government regulation of financial intermediaries. This course is restricted to students in the Commerce programs.
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on intro econ foundation with mathematical rigor and applied policy analysis; requires sustained effort but remains accessible.

name: Introduction to Data Analysis and Applied Econometrics
description: Numerical and graphical data description; data collection and sampling; probability; sampling distributions; statistical inference; hypothesis testing and estimation; simple and multiple regression analysis (extensive coverage). Learn how to analyze data and how to correctly interpret and explain results. Use Excel to analyze a wide variety of data and replicate tables and figures in economics research papers.
exclusions: GGR270H1, IRW220H1, PSY201H1, PSY202H1, SOC202H1, SOC252H1, STA220H1, STA221H1, STA248H1, ECO220Y5, MGEB11H3, MGEB12H3
difficulty_reasoning: Regression and hypothesis testing build logically from high school math; Excel reduces computational burden significantly.

name: Big-Data Tools for Economists
description: An introduction to the big-data tools—including Machine-Learning techniques—economists increasingly use in applied research. The course explores unstructured data sources such as text files, webpages, social media posts, satellite imagery, and weather data. It offers a practical introduction to creating datasets from these sources (e.g., web scrapping), linking data, and managing and visualizing them (e.g., geospatial visualization).
exclusions: CSC311H1, ECO250Y0 (Special Topics Abroad in Economics: Big Data Tools and Machine Learning for Economists) taken in Summer 2023, ESC190H1, JSC270H1, STA314H1, ECO225H5.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires programming and statistics foundation; practical coding assignments and data projects demand sustained effort weekly.

name: Foundations of Econometrics
description: A rigorous introduction to probability and mathematical statistics intended for students in Economics Specialist programs. Probability and estimation theory, sampling distributions, hypotheses testing, multiple regression analysis. Students will learn the tools used in economics and finance to model and address randomness and uncertainty.
corequisites: Recommended: MAT223H1/ MAT240H1, MAT235Y1/ MAT237Y1/ ECO210H1
exclusions: STA237H1, STA238H1, STA247H1, STA248H1, STA255H1, STA257H1, STA261H1, ECO227Y5
difficulty_reasoning: Rigorous probability and statistics with regression demands sustained mathematical engagement and problem-solving from first encounter.

name: Economics of Global Trade
description: This course is intended primarily for students in the International Relations program. This course offers an introduction to the economic causes and consequences of international trade in goods, services, labour, innovation and capital. Attention will be devoted to the economic impact of policies that affect these flows, such as protectionism and outsourcing.
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on first-year econ prerequisites with intuitive policy examples; moderate weekly readings and problem sets typical of 200-level economics.

name: Global Macroeconomics and Policies
description: This course is intended primarily for students in the International Relations program. The course serves as an introduction to some of the causes of differences in economic performance across countries and the impact of a variety of government policies, such as monetary and fiscal policy as well as exchange rate regimes, and how each of the three interact in response to changes in development, sovereign debt, monetary unions and financial crises.
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on first-year econ fundamentals with policy applications; requires sustained engagement but no specialist math or background beyond prerequisites.

name: Special Topics in Economics
description: Courses may be offered in one or more subjects each year. Students must meet the prerequisites announced by the Department.
prerequisites: See the Department of Economics website for details.
difficulty_reasoning: Variable by topic; typical special topics requires intermediate economics background and moderate weekly engagement with readings and problem sets.

name: Independent Study
description: This is an independent study course for students enrolled in an Economics program under the direction of a Department of Economics faculty member. It is an opportunity to explore a topic of interest through readings, discussion, written assignments, experiential learning, etc. This course is open only when a faculty member is available and willing to supervise on an individual basis. Applications will be jointly reviewed by the faculty member and Associate Chair, Undergraduate Studies. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
corequisites: ECO200Y1/ ECO204Y1/ ECO206Y1, ECO202Y1/ ECO208Y1/ ECO209Y1, ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on chosen topic and supervisor expectations; workload scales with self-directed depth and scope.

name: Research Opportunity Program
description: Credit course for supervised participation in faculty research project. Details at https://www.artsci.utoronto.ca/current/academics/research-opportunities/research-opportunities-program. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on research topic and supervisor expectations, which vary widely; workload scales with project scope and feedback frequency.

name: Empirical Industrial Organization
description: The quantitative analysis of firms' strategies in real-world industries, using tools from applied microeconomics and statistics. Topics include: studies of monopoly, oligopoly, imperfect competition, and the estimation of demand and cost functions that underpin these markets. Note: hands-on analysis of real-world data using computer programs is a course requirement. The course is open to all students who meet the prerequisites, and necessary support for applicable software is provided.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ ECO204Y1/ ECO206Y1; ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Empirical work with real data and econometric estimation is technically demanding for first-time exposure to applied microeconomics.

name: Environmental Economics and Policies
description: This course demonstrates how a rigorous application of microeconomic techniques can inform our responses to various environmental problems. Topics may include: air and water pollution and renewable resource management.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ ECO204Y1/ ECO206Y1; ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Requires solid microeconomic foundation and quantitative comfort, but applications are concrete and policy-driven, making concepts graspable.

name: Energy and the Environment
description: This course surveys important features of energy markets and related environmental challenges. One of the central objectives is to provide an understanding of the key economic tools needed to analyse these markets. A related objective is the development of a framework for understanding the public discourse on energy and the environment. Topics include: the hydrocarbon economy (oil, natural gas and coal), electricity markets, global warming and other externalities, renewable energy, conservation, carbon taxes and ‘cap-and-trade’.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ECO204Y1/ECO206Y1; ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Applies introductory economics tools to real-world energy systems; requires comfort with graphs and basic quantitative reasoning but no advanced math.

name: Applied Game Theory
description: Focuses on the core ideas and concepts of game theory and on applications of them in economics and other social sciences. Topics may include: oligopoly, electoral competition, the theory of public goods, voting theory, the free rider problem, repeated interaction, bargaining, evolutionary equilibrium, matching and auctions. Note: This course cannot be taken as a substitute in programs that require ECO326H1.
prerequisites: MAT133Y1/ MAT136H1/ MAT137Y1/ MAT157Y1, ECO200Y1/ ECO204Y1/ ECO206Y1/ ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ STA237H1/ STA247H1/ STA257H1/ PHL246H1/ PSY201H1(70%)/ POL232H1(70%)/ SOC202H1(70%)
difficulty_reasoning: Game theory requires abstract logical reasoning and comfort with mathematical formalism, but no calculus or programming; conceptual depth rather than technical machinery dominates.

name: Concepts of Fairness in Economics
description: How can we make collective decisions fairly? What does it mean to properly balance conflicting interests? How can we combine the well-being of individuals into a concept of societal well-being? We explore these and related ethical questions from the perspective of economic theory. A central tool is the axiomatic approach, which calls for decisions to be consistent, in precise senses, across related situations. Possible topics include: rationing problems, the Shapley value, fair division, discrimination, voting theory, foundations of utilitarianism and egalitarianism, measurement of inequality, population ethics, intergenerational equity, and concepts of equal opportunity.
exclusions: ECO351H1 (Special Topics in Economics: Principles of Fair Decisions) taken Fall 2021, Winter 2022, Fall 2022, and Winter 2023
recommended_preparation: Familiarity and comfort with mathematical notation and formal definitions.
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract axiomatic reasoning and unfamiliar ethical frameworks require sustained engagement; manageable with prior eco foundation but demanding intellectual work.

name: Economic Analysis of Law
description: The practical application of microeconomic theory to common legal problems: torts, contracts, property and crime, and the limitations of economic analysis. No previous familiarity with the law is assumed. (This is an economic analysis of legal issues, not a course in law).
difficulty_reasoning: Applies familiar microeconomic theory to unfamiliar legal contexts, requiring synthesis but not new mathematical tools or deep legal expertise.

name: Canadian Economic History, 1850-1960
description: Canadian economic history between 1850 and 1960, with a focus on the debate over the ability of the market mechanism to optimize economic development. Topics covered include: tariff policies, Confederation, the transcontinental railroad, opening the West, the Great Depression, monetary policy and the Bank of Canada, regional growth and dominion-provincial relations.
difficulty_reasoning: History-focused course requiring economics foundation; narrative-driven content and standard essays manageable with consistent reading effort.

name: Economic Development
description: This course critically analyzes issues related to economic development and the associated policy responses. Tools from micro and macroeconomic theory are employed, as well as the critical assessment of empirical evidence. Topics may include: education, health, credit markets, inequality, and the role of foreign aid.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ECO204Y1/ECO206Y1; ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Requires solid micro/macro foundation and statistical literacy; empirical analysis and policy application demand sustained engagement.

name: Advanced Economic Theory - Macro
description: A development of the microeconomic foundations of macroeconomic theory to expand students' analytic skills by constructing and solving macroeconomic models. Topics may include: dynamic choice, neoclassical growth theory, uncertainty and rational expectations, business cycles, as well as fiscal and monetary policy.
difficulty_reasoning: Mathematical rigor and dynamic modeling require sustained engagement; undeclared students lack accumulated econ and calculus maturity.

name: Advanced Microeconomics - Game Theory
description: Game theory and applications. Topics include: strategic and extensive games, with applications to economics.
difficulty_reasoning: Game theory requires abstract mathematical reasoning and economic intuition simultaneously. Most undeclared students lack both backgrounds.

name: Behavioural and Experimental Economics
description: Ample empirical and experimental evidence suggests significant departures from classical assumptions of economic behaviour. For example, humans are neither always perfectly rational nor always self interested. This course describes systematic ways in which behaviour deviates from neoclassical assumptions, generating new, and hopefully more realistic behavioural assumptions that have broad empirical, theoretical and policy implications.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ECO204Y1/ECO206Y1; ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on introductory economics and statistics foundations; conceptual rather than mathematically intense, but requires sustained engagement with research papers.

name: Urban Economics
description: Spatial economic theory and urban public policy: firms and individuals in partial and general equilibrium, land development and land-use controls, urban transportation, efficiency and equity in spending and taxing.
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate theory with math modeling and policy analysis builds on eco prerequisites but remains manageable with consistent effort.

name: The Political Economy of Media
description: We develop tools to analyze voters and the role of information in democracies. Theories of voter information are evaluated using empirical literature on media its political economy. We explore the effects of information-technology innovation, evaluating how the empirical results square with the theory. We focus on empirical methods used to identify the effects of media—from newspapers, radio and television to the internet and social media—on voters. Note: hands-on analysis of real-world data using computer programs is a course requirement. The course is open to all students who meet the prerequisites and necessary support for applicable software is provided.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ ECO204Y1/ ECO206Y1, ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Empirical methods and data analysis required; assumes comfort with statistics and coding, both non-trivial for undeclared students.

name: Public Economics
description: Theory of taxation and public goods, and quantitative methods for program evaluation. Additional topics include: taxation and income distribution; environmental policy; and the political economy of government policy.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ECO204Y1/ECO206Y1; ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Quantitative methods and economic theory require strong math foundation; problem sets and policy analysis are time-intensive.

name: Economics of Careers
description: The economic analysis of careers from the perspectives of both workers and employers. How do people decide what to study, what careers to pursue, and when to change jobs? How do these decisions interact with the structure of firms? The impact of specialization and the division of labour on the evolution of careers is considered, as are the role of cognitive and communication skills in the labour market.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ECO204Y1/ECO206Y1; ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Applied microeconomics with accessible real-world topics; requires strong prerequisite foundation but concepts are concrete and relatable once foundations are solid.

name: Labour Economics: Employment, Wages and Public Policy
description: Using tools from microeconomic theory and statistics, this course introduces students to the study of labour markets, focusing on employment and wage determination, and the application of labour economics to public policy. Topics may include: labour supply, labour demand, estimating the impact of welfare programs, minimum wages, and other labour market interventions. Note: hands-on analysis of real-world data using computer programs is a course requirement. The course is open to all students who meet the prerequisites and necessary support for applicable software is provided.
prerequisites: ECO200Y1/ ECO204Y1/ ECO206Y1; ECO220Y1/ ECO227Y1/ (STA237H1, STA238H1)/ (STA247H1, STA248H1)/ (STA257H1, STA261H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Microeconomic theory and statistics foundation required; hands-on data analysis using unfamiliar software adds significant technical demand.

name: Twentieth Century Economic History: Institutions, Growth and Inequality
description: Tailored to advanced students in Economics, Commerce, International Relations and History. The focus is on institutions, growth and inequality in countries across the world.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced economics foundation required; heavy reading on institutions and growth theory demands sustained engagement.

name: Money, Banking and Financial Markets
description: This course studies the interaction of the monetary and banking sectors with financial markets and the broader economy.
