difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level toxicology course requiring PCL482 foundation and specialized knowledge of risk assessment and regulatory frameworks.

name: Advanced Topics in Pharmacology and Toxicology
description: An opportunity to expand on innovative and unique topics in Pharmacology and Toxicology that are not already extensively addressed. The course will reveal the dynamic nature of the field and the diverse interests of our faculty. Students will be introduced to leading edge topics and research within the discipline of Pharmacology and Toxicology.
prerequisites: PCL302H1, one of STA288H1/ STA220H1/ PSY201H1, completion of 14.0 credits. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the Department.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced upper-year course requiring PCL302 foundation and research-level topic engagement; demands sustained disciplinary maturity.

name: Advanced Research in Pharmacology or Biomedical Toxicology
description: Following experience from PCL472Y1 or PCL474Y1, this course provides students an opportunity to continue to expand and develop their research skills and knowledge within a research laboratory or applied pharmacology setting. Students will develop advanced collaboration skills, good lab practices, scientific literacy, critical thinking and evaluation skills while applying their knowledge and trouble shooting skills to practical research questions. Written and oral communication skills as well as laboratory skills will comprise course assessments. Students are required to participate in active research for the equivalent of a minimum of 60 hours of laboratory time. NOTE: As this is an opportunity to continue their experience within the same PCL472Y1/PCL474Y1 research laboratory/supervisor. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Continuation course requiring 60+ hours lab work, advanced research independence, and scientific writing with minimal structured guidance.

name: Advanced Research in Pharmacology or Biomedical Toxicology
description: Following experience from (PCL297H1, PCL298H1), PCL472Y1 or PCL474Y1, this course provides students an opportunity to continue to expand and develop their research skills and knowledge within a research laboratory or applied pharmacology setting. Students will develop advanced collaboration skills, good lab practices, scientific literacy, critical thinking and evaluation skills while applying their knowledge and trouble shooting skills to practical research questions. Written and oral communication skills as well as laboratory skills will comprise course assessments. Students are required to participate in active research and related activities for the equivalent of a minimum of 200 hours of laboratory time. NOTE: As this is an opportunity to continue their research, students are required to continue within the same (PCL297H1, PCL298H1)/ PCL472Y1/ PCL474Y1 research laboratory/supervisor. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: 200+ mandatory lab hours plus advanced research, written/oral assessments, and troubleshooting demands sustained effort.

name: Arts & Science Internship Program - Maximizing Your Experience
description: This course is a mandatory component of the Arts & Science Internship Program (ASIP) for students admitted to the ASIP stream of their program for Year 2, and students admitted for Year 2 must successfully complete this course and receive a passing grade to gain access to the ASIP job board. The course will provide an opportunity for students to prepare for and maximize their learning and personal development through ASIP. Topics may include: self-assessment – skills, values, interests; building a career and co-op plan; skills articulation – how to identify, articulate and apply skills gained through courses and other life experiences; personal marketing documents; guided self-reflection on barriers to employment; resources and toolkits to support access for all; and co-op student panels. This course does not carry credit weight and is evaluated as Credit/No Credit. No tuition fee is associated, however an ancillary fee will be assessed. Refer to the ASIP Fees & Financial Aid website for further details. Students who enrolled in the ASIP stream of their program in Year 3 should enrol in PDC230H1.
difficulty_reasoning: Career-skills workshop with no prerequisite knowledge required; self-reflection and practical tools need minimal outside preparation.

name: Arts & Science Internship Program - Essential Skills for ASIP Success
description: This course is a mandatory component of the Arts & Science Internship Program (ASIP) for students admitted to the ASIP stream of their program for Year 2, and students admitted for Year 2 must successfully complete this course and receive a passing grade before starting their first work term. The course will help students prepare for their first work term and optimize their chances of success in the first recruitment cycle. Topics may include: ASIP guidelines and requirements, resume and cover letter tailoring, professionalism essentials, job search essentials, interview preparation, workplace rights, health and wellbeing while in ASIP, and accessibility and EDI considerations. This course does not carry credit weight and is evaluated as Credit/No Credit. No tuition fee is associated, however an ancillary fee will be assessed. Refer to the ASIP Fees & Financial Aid website for further details. Students who enrolled in the ASIP stream of their program in Year 3 should enrol in PDC231H1.
difficulty_reasoning: Professional skills workshop with no technical content, focused on practical resume and interview preparation requiring minimal prior knowledge.

name: Arts & Science Internship Program - Professional Skill Development I
description: This course is a mandatory component of the Arts & Science Internship Program (ASIP) for students admitted to the ASIP stream of their program for Year 2. Successful completion of this course is required for students to graduate from the ASIP stream of their program of study. The course supports students in developing transferable skills essential for success in professional environments and the evolving world of work. Delivered through an asynchronous modular format, students complete a combination of required and elective skill development modules. Required modules include: 1) Workplace Communication 2) Collaboration & Team Dynamics. Students further tailor their learning through elective modules that may include project management, critical thinking and problem solving, leadership and influence, application of workplace communication, data visualization, design thinking, universal design, and sustainable development. In addition to asynchronous skill development modules, students participate in bi-weekly synchronous lecture sections focused on recruitment cycle support. These sessions provide structured support for the job search process, including job search strategy, careers, and workplace readiness. This course does not carry credit weight and is evaluated as Credit/No Credit. No tuition fee is associated, however an ancillary fee will be assessed. Refer to the ASIP Fees & Financial Aid website for further details. Students who enrolled in the ASIP stream of their program in Year 3 should enrol in PDC330H1.
difficulty_reasoning: Asynchronous skill modules and bi-weekly job search sessions require minimal prior knowledge and no specialist background.

name: Arts & Science Internship Program - Professional Skill Development II
description: This course is a mandatory component of the Arts & Science Internship Program (ASIP) for students admitted to the ASIP stream of their program for Year 2. Successful completion is required for students to graduate from the ASIP stream of their program of study. Building on Professional Skill Development I, this course deepens students’ application of transferable skills in professional contexts. The course is delivered through a modular format in which students complete required and elective skill development modules. difficulty_reasoning: Asynchronous skill modules and bi-weekly job search sessions require minimal prerequisites and self-paced engagement only.

name: Arts & Science Internship Program - Professional Skill Development II
description: This course is a mandatory component of the Arts & Science Internship Program (ASIP) for students admitted to the ASIP stream of their program for Year 3. Successful completion of this course is required for students to graduate from the ASIP stream of their program of study. Building on Professional Skill Development I, this course deepens students’ application of transferable skills in professional contexts. The course is delivered through a modular format in which students complete required and elective skill development modules. Required modules include: 1) Project Management Essentials 2) Critical Thinking & Problem Solving Students further tailor their learning through elective modules that may include advanced project management, leadership and influence, workplace communication, data visualization, design thinking, universal design, and sustainable development. In addition to asynchronous skill development modules, students participate in bi-weekly synchronous lecture sections focused on recruitment cycle support. These sessions provide structured support for the job search process, including , job search strategy, careers, and workplace readiness. This course does not carry credit weight and is evaluated as Credit/No Credit. No tuition fee is associated, however an ancillary fee will be assessed. Refer to the ASIP Fees & Financial Aid website for further details. Students who enrolled in the ASIP stream of their program in Year 2 should enrol in PDC321H1.
difficulty_reasoning: Asynchronous skill modules and bi-weekly job search sessions require minimal prior knowledge and low weekly time commitment.

name: Pharmaceutics 1
description: This course introduces the key concepts in the design, manufacture, and use of efficacious dosage forms. The characteristics of different delivery routes are discussed. The course focuses on the role of pharmaceutics in drug development, and the role of anatomy and physiology in the conception and design of effective dosage forms. Topics covered include biopharmaceutical considerations, pharmaceutical solutions, sterilization theories and techniques, complexation and solubility enhancement, pre-formulation, colloids and dispersions, powders and micromeritics, and solid-state properties. Students will gain an appreciation of how the properties of drug components (such as pH, salt concentration, co-solvents, sterility, colloids, dispersions, powders, and solids) determine the stability, solubility, and efficacy of drug products.
difficulty_reasoning: Quantitative chemistry and pharmaceutical principles require sustained problem-solving; no assumed pharmacy background makes content unfamiliar and dense.

name: Molecular Pharmacology 1
description: Structural and mechanistic determinants of drug action at the molecular level. Topics include the physical-chemical properties of drugs per se as they relate to therapeutic intervention and the biophysical and biochemical properties of enzymes and nucleic acids that underlie and are affected by their interactions with drugs.
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on organic chemistry and biochemistry; requires visualizing molecular interactions and mechanisms unfamiliar to most undeclared students.

name: Molecular Pharmacology 2
description: Structural and mechanistic determinants of drug action via macromolecules that effect cellular signalling. Neurohumoral receptors and other signalling proteins are grouped according to their structure and mechanism of action. Native properties and their modulation by drugs are discussed in the context of basic principles of molecular pharmacology.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires solid organic chemistry and biochemistry foundation; molecular mechanisms and receptor signalling demand sustained conceptual integration.

name: Chemistry and Technologies in Drug Discovery
description: This course provides interdisciplinary learning related to the chemistry of drugs and technologies of drug discovery as they are related to small molecules and biologics, their mechanisms of action, and the latest trends in the field of pharmaceuticals. Various important concepts such as structure-activity relationships, drug-like properties, computer modelling, combinatorial chemistry, small molecule drugs, biologics and related aspects in relation to new drugs will be covered. Novel concepts such as artificial intelligence in drug discovery will be introduced. After completing this course, students may practice entry-level medicinal chemistry with sound synthetic chemistry acquired from other chemistry courses, or take advanced courses in medicinal chemistry, drug discovery, and specialized courses in drug development. Students will also have a deeper understanding of drug discovery and development.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires strong organic chemistry foundation and introduces computational/modelling concepts unfamiliar to most undeclared students.

name: Pharmaceutics 2
description: This course provides an understanding of advanced topics in pharmaceutics. It involves a detailed examination of the material properties of pharmaceuticals and the role of cellular processes in the delivery of a drug to its site of action. Selected topics include thermodynamic characterization of drug solubility, diffusion measurement and its use in pharmaceutics, liposomal drug delivery, excipient consideration and selection for liquid dosage forms, polymeric materials in pharmaceutical applications, rheology and its applicability to liquid drug vehicles, bioequivalence determination of dermatological dosage forms, and an introduction on the impact of physiochemical properties on dermatological dosage forms.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced chemistry and biology concepts require solid foundation; quantitative problem-solving and specialized pharmaceutical knowledge demand sustained engagement.

name: Establishing the Bioequivalence of Pharmaceutical Products
description: This course will introduce the practical and regulatory design elements and consideration of human clinical trials for the purposes of demonstrating bioequivalence between drug products. The principles discussed are currently used by generic and innovator drug companies in many countries. Students will learn about the regulations, methods, techniques, pharmacokinetics, and biostatistics involved in creating bioequivalence studies. The course has a heavy mathematical emphasis, with a large component dedicated to mathematical modeling (non-compartmental and compartmental pharmacokinetic modeling, and biostatistics). Also discussed are clinical trial sample size determination, bioequivalence study design elements, clinical study protocol and final report writing, disaster recovery and outlier analysis, and design of steady-state studies. A module is also included on developing and improving interview skills. After taking this course, students will understand the steps required to set up single-dose and steady-state pilot and pivotal bioequivalence trials using parallel, crossover, and semi-replicate designs, and be well-poised for entry-level scientific positions in industry.
prerequisites: BCH210H1, CHM247H1/ CHM249H1, (MAT130H1/ MAT135H1, MAT136H1)/ (MAT148H1, MAT149H1/ MAT137Y1), PHC230H1, PHC330H1
difficulty_reasoning: Heavy mathematical modeling and biostatistics with regulatory complexity demands sustained disciplinary engagement beyond typical 300-level courses.

name: Pharmaceutical Chemistry Laboratory
description: This course will introduce students to theoretical and applied concepts in pharmaceutical chemistry, in research, development, and manufacturing contexts. The course is designed with lectures and laboratories covering major areas in pharmaceutical chemistry, which have been carefully scheduled together to link concepts discussed during lectures to practical hands-on activities. The course covers selected topics pertinent to drug development such as acid/base equilibria, buffer preparation, drug solubility, drug polymorphs and salt selection, pKa, and co-solvency. The course also examines characterizing properties of common liquid, semi-solid and solid drug vehicles, such as viscosity, degradation kinetics/stability, diffusivity, tonicity, particle size distribution, and powder flowability. Emphasis is placed on the process of drug manufacture. Laboratory equipment used is commonly found in formulation labs, pharmacies, pharmaceutical research laboratories, and pharmaceutical manufacturing facilities. A lecture module on ethics and academic integrity will discuss cutting-edge applications of ethics in research, and issues arising when complex scientific solutions are proposed to regulatory bodies and to the public eye.
difficulty_reasoning: Lab-intensive course with demanding chemistry prerequisites and hands-on experimental design; requires strong foundational chemistry mastery.

name: Drug Transport across Biological Membranes
description: The goal of this course is to provide students with knowledge of the molecular processes involved in drug transport across biological cell membranes with emphasis on their physiological and clinical significance. This course is restricted to Pharmaceutical Chemistry Specialist program students.
prerequisites: PHC301H1, and enrolled as a Year 4 student in the Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program.
difficulty_reasoning: Year 4 specialist course building on three years of chemistry and pharmacology foundation; requires mastery of membrane biophysics and drug kinetics.

name: Application of Medicinal Chemistry to Pharmacology
description: This online course will build upon basic pharmacology and medicinal chemistry to make links between the basic sciences and demonstrate how basic principles can be used to improve clinical therapy. It will also include a critical evaluation of evidence for specific mechanisms and therapies. Topics covered include the effect of metabolism on drug response and factors that influence drug metabolism and kinetics; interactions of drugs with receptors, genetic differences in response, and mechanisms of tolerance; antimicrobials and antineoplastics; autocoids, antihistamines, vasopressin, anticoagulants, iron, estrogens, androgens, and the use of drugs in the perinatal period; antithyroid drugs, drugs to treat diabetes, and corticosteroids; eicosanoids, antihyperlipidemic drugs, and vitamins; drugs affecting bone metabolism, cholinergic agonists and antagonists; adrenoreceptor agonists and antagonists, agents affecting muscles and movement; opioids; CNS organization; anticonvulsants, anesthetics, and anxiolytics; antipsychotics, antidepressants, inotropic agents, antiarrhythmic drugs, diuretics; anti-inflammatory drugs, respiratory drugs, immune system organization; drugs to treat acid-peptic disorders, intestinal motility disorders, and inflammatory bowel disease; and adverse drug reactions and poisoning. An emphasis is placed on developing critical reasoning and evaluation skills to discern between standard of care and complementary therapies that have been developed in the past showing poor safety and/or efficacy, with poor regard to the scientific process. This course is restricted to Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program students.
prerequisites: PHC301H1, PHC320H1, and enrolled as a Year 4 student in the Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program.
difficulty_reasoning: Fourth-year specialist course covering 20+ drug classes requiring deep integration of chemistry, pharmacology, and clinical reasoning.

name: Latest Developments in Drug Discovery
description: In-depth discussion of implementation of pharmaceutical sciences in drug development strategies. Students will apply fundamental principles of pharmaceutics and drug delivery to current problems in the pharmaceutical industry. This course is restricted to Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program students.
prerequisites: Enrolled as a Year 4 student in the Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program.
difficulty_reasoning: Year 4 pharmaceutical chemistry capstone applying specialist knowledge to industry problems requires sustained mastery.

name: Nanomedicines in Oncology
description: This course covers a range of topics that pertain to the development and application of nanomedicines in oncology. Students will gain an understanding of the biological barriers to drug delivery in oncology as well as the tremendous heterogeneity in cancer and the challenge this presents for treatment. The concepts of passive and active targeting of nanomedicines will be covered with critical assessment of the enhanced permeability and retention effect. A detailed overview of the most advanced nanotechnology-platforms for drug delivery (i.e., liposomes, block copolymer micelles and polymer-drug conjugates) will be provided with additional discussion of new emerging platforms. The integration of imaging in drug development and development of theranostics and therapeutic-diagnostic pairs will also be discussed. Special emphasis on critical evaluation of scientific literature and pre-clinical/clinical studies will be made throughout the course. *Note* This course is offered in alternating years.
prerequisites: PHC230H1, PHC330H1 and enrolled as a Year 4 student in the Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program.
difficulty_reasoning: Highly specialized upper-year pharmaceutical chemistry course requiring mastery of prior courses and unfamiliar nanotechnology concepts.

name: Pharmaceutical Data Acquisition and Analysis
description: This course introduces students to theoretical and applied concepts in designing and implementing electronic circuitry, for the purpose of collecting and analyzing experimental data in pharmaceutics and other contexts. The course discusses introductory circuit design, with an emphasis on how common components work in scientific and pharmaceutical manufacturing instrumentation. Practical and mathematical aspects of circuit design are discussed (e.g., Ohm’s Law, voltage dividers, analog vs. digital signals). There is a heavy emphasis on programming in C++ in the Arduino IDE which will complement learning activities. Specific topics include power calculations, logic gates, capacitance, microprocessor pin functionality, switching strategies for higher powder devices (relays, transistors, MOSFETs), process control (on/off and PID controllers), and operational amplifiers. Data acquisition, smoothing, filtering, and logging strategies are also discussed. Each section has planned practical activities where circuits are built, programmed, and tested. After taking this course, students will be better equipped to build their own control circuits for undergraduate research projects and other applications.
prerequisites: BCH210H1, CHM247H1/CHM249H1, (MAT130H1/ MAT135H1, MAT136H1)/ (MAT148H1, MAT149H1/ MAT137Y1), PHC340Y1 and enrolled as a Year 4 student in the Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program.
difficulty_reasoning: Heavy C++ programming combined with unfamiliar circuit theory and hands-on lab builds creates sustained cognitive and time demand.

name: Global Pharmaceutical Policy
description: This course is designed for students who are interested in the critical analysis of global health policy and the interrelationship between global and domestic policy issues. The course will introduce students generally to the basic concepts and issues in global health with a particular focus on pharmaceutical policy. We will address key issues in health and discuss core institutional and transnational actors, how governments in different jurisdictions manage public health responsibilities, the tension between economic imperatives and health objectives, global obligations, and pressure from special interest groups. More narrowly, we will analyze a breadth of complex policies questions. Examples include: the research and development global divide, policial issues influences on pharmaceutical policy, how global commitments, such as membership in the World Trade Organization, conflict with or correspond to domestic policy directions and national sovereignty. Guest speakers will lead some sessions. This course will consist of lectures, guest discussions, case studies and student-led presentations. This is restricted to students in the Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program.
prerequisites: Enrolled as a Year 4 student in the Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program.
difficulty_reasoning: Fourth-year policy course requires critical synthesis of complex global health systems and institutional actors without deep technical prerequisites.

name: Pharmaceutical Chemistry Research
description: This course will provide research experience under the supervision of a Departmental staff member. The goal is to deepen the student’s understanding of the scientific basis and practical techniques relevant to modern pharmaceutical development. This course is restricted to Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Permission from the Course Coordinator and enrolled as a Year 4 student in the Pharmaceutical Chemistry specialist program.
difficulty_reasoning: Year 4 research course demands deep disciplinary mastery, independent investigation, and sustained full-semester output.

name: Ancient Wisdom, Modern Insights: A Historical Introduction to Philosophy
description: An introduction to the central branches of philosophy, such as logic, theory of knowledge, metaphysics, ethics, and political philosophy. Writings from the central figures in the history of Western and non-Western philosophy, as well as contemporary philosophers, may be considered. Note: PHL100Y1 and PHL101Y1 are both broad introductions to philosophy, and each is appropriate for students with no prior experience with the subject.
exclusions: PHL101Y1, PHL201H1, PHLA10H3, PHLA11H3, PHL101H5, PHL102H5, PHL105Y5
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites; concepts introduced from scratch with accessible primary texts; requires engagement but not specialist background.

name: What Should I Believe? An Introduction to Philosophical Issues
description: An introduction to some of the central problems of philosophy. Examples of questions that may be considered include: What is sound reasoning? What can we know? What is ultimately real? Is morality rational? Do humans have free will? Is there a God? What is consciousness? Should we fear death? What is justice? Note: PHL100Y1 and PHL101Y1 are both broad introductions to philosophy, and each is appropriate for students with no prior experience with the subject.
exclusions: PHL100Y1, PHL201H1, PHLA10H3, PHLA11H3, PHL101H5, PHL102H5, PHL105Y5
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; concepts introduced from first principles. Weekly readings and occasional essays manageable with consistent engagement.

name: Philosophy, Film, and Social Criticism
description: This course will critically examine the role of cinema in relation to selected topics and themes in social and political philosophy; these will vary from year to year, but may include race, ethnic and cultural 'other'-ness, class, social conflict, citizenship, cosmopolitanism, and human rights. Films will be screened in class and discussed against the background of focused critical and philosophical readings. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: No prior philosophy needed; film viewing is engaging; philosophical texts demand careful reading but introduction-level argument analysis.

name: Introduction to Philosophical Anthropology
description: Philosophical anthropology is the subject that poses the most fundamental questions about human nature and the human condition. Taking as its point of departure the most up-to-date scientific understanding of human nature, from anthropology broadly conceived, it goes on to inquire, in a disciplined fashion, about the implications of these views for perennial philosophical questions about human rationality, morality, the possibility of progress, the existence of god and the meaning of life. This course will examine one or more topics in this domain, as a more general introduction to the discipline. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory philosophy with no prerequisites, but abstract concepts and essay writing demand genuine engagement and effort.

name: Philosophy of Time
description: The passage of time is a fundamental aspect of human experience: we are born, we grow older, and eventually we pass away. During our lives our experience of the past, present, and future are distinct. We can influence the world in the present and the future, but it does not seem that we can influence the past. We have hopes about the future, memories of the past, and experiences of the present. In this seminar we will explore insights from contemporary philosophy and physics concerning the nature of the passage of time. Questions to be considered may include the following: What does it mean to say that time passes? Does time really pass at all? How do we experience time? Why can we influence the future but not the past? Is it possible to travel backward in time? Is time even real? What is time? Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Accessible conceptual inquiry with no math or specialist prerequisites; seminar format emphasizes discussion over technical mastery.

name: Ethics and Fiction
description: The goal of this seminar is to investigate selected ethical questions via works of fiction, considering how, and with what effect, fiction functions as an ethical medium. We will reflect on what fiction can teach us about pressing challenges of the human condition; themes to be discussed will vary from year to year, but may include choice and responsibility, freedom, friendship, empathy, integrity, and our relation to the divine and to death. Genres studied may range over novels, short stories, plays, and narrative poetry. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites; reading and discussion-based; writing demands are moderate and build gradually through the semester.

name: Ancient Philosophy
description: Central texts of the pre-Socratics, Plato, Aristotle, and post-Aristotelian philosophy.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense primary texts require slow, careful reading; no math or coding, but philosophical argumentation is new for most undeclared students.

name: Introductory Philosophy
description: An introduction to philosophy focusing on the connections among its main branches: logic, theory of knowledge, metaphysics, and ethics. This course is intended for those with little or no philosophy background but who have completed Year 1 in any area of study.
exclusions: PHL100Y1, PHL101Y1, or more than 1.0 PHL course
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory survey with self-contained concepts and no prerequisites; regular readings and essays are manageable for most students.

name: Early Medieval Philosophy
description: A study of issues such as the relations of reason and faith, the being and the nature of God, and the problem of universals, in the writings of such philosophers as Augustine, Boethius, Anselm and Abelard.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense primary texts and abstract concepts require sustained engagement, but no prior philosophy background assumed or needed.

name: Later Medieval Philosophy
description: A study of issues such as the relations of reason and faith, the being and the nature of God, and the structure of the universe, in the writings of such philosophers as Aquinas and Ockham.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical texts and abstract medieval concepts demand close reading and sustained engagement, but no prior philosophy background required.

name: 17th-and 18th-Century Philosophy
description: Central texts of such philosophers as Hobbes, Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz, Locke, Berkeley, Hume, and Kant.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical texts require careful reading and interpretation, but no prerequisites assumed and lectures scaffold unfamiliar ideas.

name: Introduction to Continental Philosophy
description: An introduction to some of the post-Hegelian thinkers who inspired the various philosophical movements broadly referred to as continental, such as phenomenology, existentialism, deconstruction, and post-modernism. Questions include the will, faith, death, existence, history and politics, rationality and its limits, encountering an other. Authors studied may include: Kierkegaard, Schopenhauer, Nietzsche, Marx, Freud, Heidegger, Sartre.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical texts with unfamiliar concepts require sustained close reading and interpretation despite no prerequisites assumed.

name: Knowledge and Reality
description: An introduction to issues in the fundamental branches of philosophy: metaphysics, which considers the overall framework of reality; epistemology, or the theory of knowledge; and related problems in the philosophy of science. Topics in metaphysics may include: mind and body, causality, space and time, God, freedom and determinism; topics in epistemology may include perception, evidence, belief, truth, skepticism.
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract concepts require careful thought, but no prior knowledge or technical skills assumed; readings and essays are standard philosophy workload.

name: Philosophy for Scientists
description: An introduction to philosophy tailored for students with backgrounds in mathematics and science. Topics include causation, explanation, the relation between scientific and mathematical theories and reality, the role of mathematics in scientific theories, the relevance of scientific and mathematical discoveries to ‘big’ traditional philosophical questions such as the nature of consciousness, whether we have free will, and the meaning of life.
corequisites: 1.0 credit in Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Engineering, or Computer Science
recommended_preparation: Background in science and/or mathematics will be presupposed.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory philosophy course requiring only curiosity and reading comprehension, not specialist science background.

name: Introduction to Chinese Philosophy
description: A historical introduction to Chinese philosophy, covering selected figures and texts from the Warring States period through the Qīng dynasty. Schools of thought covered include Confucianism, Mohism, Daoism, Legalism, "Profound Learning," Neo-Confucianism, and "Evidential Learning." Texts and thinkers include the Confucian Analects, Mòzǐ, Mèngzǐ, Xúnzǐ, Dàodéjīng, Zhuāngzǐ, Hán Fēi, Guō Xiàng, Zhū Xī, Wáng Yángmíng, and Dài Zhèn.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; dense historical texts require careful reading but concepts are intrinsically interesting and self-contained.

name: Introduction to South Asian Philosophy
description: An introduction to the main schools, thinkers, and topics in the philosophical thought of the Sanskrit world, covering various topics, from epistemology to philosophy of religion. Students will get acquainted with one of the world's richest philosophical traditions and its challenges.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical texts and unfamiliar conceptual frameworks require sustained engagement, but no prior background is assumed or needed.

name: Persons, Minds and Bodies
description: Consciousness and its relation to the body; personal identity and survival; knowledge of other minds; psychological events and behaviour.
difficulty_reasoning: Philosophy of mind requires careful conceptual thinking but no specialist background. Regular readings and essays are standard.

name: Philosophy of Human Sexuality
description: Philosophical issues about sex and sexual identity in the light of biological, psychological and ethical theories of sex and gender; the concept of gender; male and female sex roles; perverse sex; sexual liberation; love and sexuality.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; philosophy of sex engages everyday experience. Weekly readings and essays demand consistent engagement but remain accessible.

name: Human Nature
description: Aspects of human nature, e.g., emotion, instincts, motivation. Theories of human nature, e.g., behaviourism, psychoanalysis.
difficulty_reasoning: Philosophy of human nature is conceptually accessible without prerequisites; weekly readings and essays require steady engagement but remain manageable.

name: Modern Symbolic Logic
description: An introduction to formal deductive logic. Semantics, symbolization, and techniques of natural deduction in sentential logic. Symbolization, natural deduction, and models in monadic predicate logic. Symbolization and natural deduction with polyadic predicates. Introduction to advanced concepts in first-order logic, such as operations, identity, and models.
difficulty_reasoning: Formal logic demands sustained abstract thinking and symbol manipulation unfamiliar to most undeclared students, but no prior knowledge is required.

name: Probability and Inductive Logic
description: The elements of axiomatic probability theory and its main interpretations (frequency, logical, and subjective). Reasoning with probabilities in decision-making and science.
difficulty_reasoning: Probability requires careful logic and symbolic reasoning, but no calculus; weekly problem sets demand sustained engagement.

name: Philosophy of Science
description: This course is an introduction to contemporary topics in the philosophy of science which serves as preparation for more advanced study in general philosophy of science and philosophy of the particular sciences. Topics may include the rise and fall of logical positivism, the problem of demarcation, falsificationism, confirmation, the connection between theory and observation, the value free ideal, and the epistemic authority of scientists.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites required; concepts introduced from scratch but demand sustained analytical thinking and essay writing.

name: Philosophy of the Internet
description: The internet and digital technology have had a transformative impact on the economy, society and politics, art and culture, and everyday life. This course explores the fascinating, often urgent, new philosophical questions raised by these changes as well as the way they invite a rethinking of many older philosophical questions. Topics to be addressed may include ethical problems relating to artificial intelligence and algorithms; identity through social media; digital ownership and privacy; and collective/distributed knowledge in its relation to information, among others.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites; contemporary topics require only critical thinking and engagement with accessible philosophical frameworks and current examples.

name: Introduction to Political Philosophy
description: An introduction to central issues in political philosophy, e.g., political and social justice, liberty and the criteria of good government. The writings of contemporary political philosophers, as well as major figures in the history of philosophy, may be considered.
difficulty_reasoning: Heavy reading and essay writing are demanding but no specialized background required; philosophical thinking is learnable with consistent engagement.

name: Philosophy of Race
description: What is the meaning of race? How does it affect political philosophy? Is there an ethics of race? These are some of the questions that will be covered in this course on the critical philosophy of race. Students will be introduced to problems concerning the metaphysics of race, race and political injustice, ethics and recognition, race and aesthetic critique, and others.
difficulty_reasoning: Philosophy of race requires close reading and abstract thinking but assumes no prior background, making it intermediate with steady engagement.

name: Law and Morality
description: Justifications for the legal enforcement of morality; particular ethical issues arising out of the intersection of law and morality, such as punishment, freedom of expression and censorship, autonomy and paternalism, constitutional protection of human rights.
difficulty_reasoning: Philosophy of law is conceptually demanding but introductory, with regular readings and essays typical of mid-level humanities courses.

name: Environmental Ethics
description: A study of environmental issues raising questions of concern to moral and political philosophers, such as property rights, responsibility for future generations, and the interaction of human beings with the rest of nature. Typical issues: sustainable development, alternative energy, the preservation of wilderness areas, animal rights.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; conceptual philosophy is approachable through readings and discussion. Moderate weekly engagement required.

name: Introduction to Ethics
description: An introduction to central issues in ethics or moral philosophy, such as the objectivity of values, the nature of moral judgements, rights and duties, the virtues, and consequentialism. Readings may be drawn from a variety of contemporary and historical sources.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites; clear readings and essay structure make it manageable despite abstract philosophical concepts being new.

name: Bioethics
description: An introduction to the study of moral and legal problems in medical practice and in biomedical research; the development of health policy. Topics include: concepts of health and disease, patient rights, informed consent, allocation of scarce resources, euthanasia, abortion, genetic and reproductive technologies, human research, and mental health.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites; ethics concepts are intuitive. Weekly readings and discussion-based format require steady engagement but minimal technical background.

name: Aesthetics
description: An historical and systematic introduction to the main questions in the philosophy of art and beauty from Plato to the present. These include the relation between art and beauty, the nature of aesthetic experience, definitions and theories of art, the criteria of excellence in the arts, and the function of art criticism.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; core concepts are readable but require sustained philosophical thinking and essay writing.

name: Business Ethics
description: Philosophical issues in ethics, social theory, and theories of human nature insofar as they bear on contemporary conduct of business. Issues include: Does business have moral responsibilities? Can social costs and benefits be calculated? Does modern business life determine human nature or the other way around? Do political ideas and institutions such as democracy have a role within business?
difficulty_reasoning: Philosophy course requires careful reading and argument analysis, but no technical background assumed. Standard essay-based assessment.

name: Early Greek Philosophy
description: A study of selected Greek philosophers before Plato. Topics may include the Pre-Socratic natural philosophers, Parmenides and the Eleatics, and the so-called sophistic movement.
prerequisites: PHL200Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Ancient Greek philosophy requires careful textual reading and abstract conceptual thinking, but PHL200Y1 prerequisite provides foundational context.

name: Ancient Philosophy After Aristotle
description: A study of selected themes in post-Aristotelian philosophy. Topics may include Stoicism, Epicureanism, Neoplatonism, and various forms of scepticism.
prerequisites: PHL200Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Post-Aristotelian philosophy requires sustained close reading and textual analysis, manageable with prior PHL200 foundation.

name: Plato
description: Selected metaphysical, epistemological, and ethical themes in Plato’s dialogues.
prerequisites: PHL200Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Close reading of ancient texts demands interpretive skill; sustained philosophical argument requires patient engagement but no specialist background assumed.

name: Aristotle
description: Selected anthropological, ethical and metaphysical themes in the works of Aristotle.
prerequisites: PHL200Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Ancient philosophy requires sustained conceptual engagement with unfamiliar dense texts and careful argumentative reasoning throughout.

name: Classical Islamic Philosophy
description: An introduction to the major thinkers in classical Islamic philosophy, with emphasis placed on developing a properly philosophical understanding of the issues and arguments. Topics include the existence of God; creation and causality; human nature and knowledge; the nature of ethical obligations; and the constitution of the ideal political state.
prerequisites: PHL200Y1, 7.5 credits with at least 1.5 credits in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical texts and unfamiliar medieval Islamic arguments require significant engagement without typical introductory scaffolding.

name: Post-Classical Islamic Philosophy
description: An advanced introduction to philosophical movements in the Islamic world in the wake of Avicenna. Topics include developments in metaphysics, epistemology, philosophy of mind, value theory, and philosophical methodology from the 12th to the 17th century that arose in response to new social, political, religious, and scientific currents.
prerequisites: 7.5 credits with at least 1.5 credits in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced historical philosophy text with minimal scaffolding demands disciplinary maturity most undeclared students lack.

name: Augustine
description: Central themes in St. Augustine's Christian philosophy, such as the problem of evil, the interior way to God, the goal of human life and the meaning of history.
prerequisites: PHL200Y1/PHL205H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Medieval Christian philosophy requires sustained engagement with demanding primary texts and abstract theological concepts unfamiliar to most undeclared students.

name: The Rationalists
description: Central philosophical problems in philosophers such as Descartes, Spinoza, Leibniz, and their contemporaries.
prerequisites: PHL210Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Dense historical texts and abstract metaphysics demand sustained philosophical maturity beyond introductory courses.

name: The Empiricists
description: Central philosophical problems in philosophers such as Locke, Berkeley, Hume, and their contemporaries.
prerequisites: PHL210Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Dense primary texts and abstract philosophical argumentation demand sustained interpretive effort unfamiliar to most undeclared students.

name: Topics in 17th and 18th Century Philosophy
description: Central philosophical problems arising in the early modern period.
prerequisites: PHL210Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Assumes PHL210Y foundation but early modern texts demand sustained close reading and abstract analytical maturity rarely developed before upper years.

name: Kant
description: A systematic study of The Critique of Pure Reason.
prerequisites: PHL210Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Kant's Critique demands sustained philosophical maturity and dense conceptual engagement rare in introductory philosophy.

name: Hegel
description: An examination of Hegel's project of absolute knowing, its philosophical assumptions, and its implications for history, science and experience.
prerequisites: PHL210Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Hegel demands sustained engagement with notoriously dense philosophical texts; prior philosophy foundation expected.

name: Marx and Marxism
description: An examination of some of the leading themes in the philosophy of Karl Marx. Developments of Marxist philosophy by later thinkers, and critics of Marxism, may also be considered.
prerequisites: 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Marx's dense philosophical texts and political economy concepts demand sustained engagement; no introductory foundation assumed.

name: Phenomenology
description: Phenomenology is a method used in the analysis of human awareness and subjectivity. It has been applied in the social sciences, in the humanities, and in philosophy. Texts studied are from Husserl and later practitioners, e.g., Heidegger, Sartre, Merleau-Ponty, Gurwitsch, and Ricoeur.
prerequisites: PHL210Y1/PHL217H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Dense continental philosophy texts demand sustained close reading and philosophical maturity absent in most undeclared students.

name: Heidegger
description: Some work from the 1920s (either Being and Time or contemporary lectures) and selections from Heidegger's later work on poetry, technology, and history are studied. Heidegger's position within phenomenology and within the broader history of thought is charted.
prerequisites: PHL217H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Dense continental philosophy texts demand sustained conceptual effort and close reading even for prepared students.

name: Social and Cultural Theory
description: A study of philosophical approaches to understanding various aspects of contemporary culture and/or society. Topics may include theories of modernity, capitalism and consumerism, architecture and design, cultural pluralism, globalization, media and internet.
prerequisites: 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract theory and dense philosophical texts require sustained engagement, but topic breadth allows conceptual entry points without deep prior knowledge.

name: Early Analytic Philosophy
description: An examination of some of the classic texts of early analytic philosophy, concentrating on the work of Frege, Russell, and Wittgenstein.
prerequisites: PHL245H1/(MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1 and one of PHL232H1/PHL233H1/PHL240H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Dense primary texts require sustained close reading; symbolic logic and conceptual abstraction demand disciplinary maturity beyond typical entry level.

name: Metaphysics and Epistemology in Sanskrit Philosophy
description: This course covers topics in Sanskrit philosophy, with a special focus on metaphysics and epistemology (and related issues in philosophy of language and logic). Students will investigate specific primary texts (in translation) and will learn how to recognize and analyze the philosophical arguments they contain. Example texts and ideas include: the Upaniṣads, the Mahābhārata, the Buddhist dialogues, the Nyāya- and Vaiśeṣika-sūtra, the works of Dignāga, and others.
prerequisites: 7.5 credits (in any field) with at least 1.5 credits in Philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: First encounter with non-Western philosophy tradition requires sustained conceptual effort; translation-based primary texts demand careful analytical reading.

name: Topics in 20th Century Continental Philosophy
description: Selected topics and themes in continental philosophy of the 20th century, drawing on such approaches as hermeneutics, phenomenology, critical theory, structuralism, deconstruction, feminism, psychoanalysis, and post-colonial theory. Authors and texts will vary, but may include such authors as: Adorno, Arendt, Benjamin, Bloch, Deleuze, Derrida, Fanon, Foucault, Freud, Gadamer, Habermas, Heidegger, Irigaray, Kristeva, Lacan, Levi-Strauss, Levinas, Merleau-Ponty.
prerequisites: 8.0 credits, including PHL217H1 and an additional 1.5 PHL credits
difficulty_reasoning: Dense, abstract Continental philosophy texts demand sustained close reading and prior philosophical literacy that most undeclared students lack.

name: Contemporary Continental Philosophy
description: A discussion of selected problems and debates in recent Continental philosophy. Authors and texts will vary, but may include such authors as: Giorgio Agamben, Alain Badiou, Étienne Balibar, Judith Butler, Jacques Derrida, Catherine Malabou, Achille Mbembe, Quentin Meillassoux, Jean-Luc Nancy, Jacques Rancière, Gayatri Spivak, Slavoj Žižek, Sylvia Wynter.
prerequisites: 8.0 credits, including PHL217H1 and an additional 1.5 PHL credits
difficulty_reasoning: Dense, abstract Continental texts require sustained engagement; most students lack framework to parse philosopher-specific jargon and arguments.

name: Metaphysics
description: Historical and systematic approaches to topics in metaphysics, such as the nature of reality, substance and existence, necessity and possibility, causality, universals and particulars.
prerequisites: 7.5 credits with at least 1.5 PHL credits, including (PHL245H1/(MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1), (PHL232H1/PHL233H1/PHL240H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract philosophical reasoning about fundamental concepts demands sustained analytical maturity beyond typical first-year exposure.

name: Epistemology
description: Historical and systematic approaches to topics in the theory of knowledge, such as truth, belief, justification, perception, a priori knowledge, certitude, skepticism, other minds.
prerequisites: PHL232H1/PHL233H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Requires solid philosophical foundations from prerequisites; abstract reasoning about knowledge, truth, and skepticism demands sustained conceptual maturity.

name: Puzzles and Paradoxes
description: Time travel, truth, infinity, rational decision making: each of these topics gives rise to philosophical puzzles and paradoxes. In this class we'll consider a variety of such paradoxes. Using logic and other philosophical tools, we'll show how these paradoxes can lead to deep and important philosophical conclusions.
prerequisites: 7.5 credits, with at least 1.5 credits in philosophy, PHL245H1/​ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Paradox puzzles are conceptually engaging but require careful logical reasoning without deep technical background from most students.

name: Mind and Language in Chinese Thought
description: Issues in the philosophy of mind and language played a crucial role in the philosophical discourse of classical China. This course will guide students in reconstructing this role and exploring its philosophical significance by interpreting and critically evaluating selected early Chinese philosophical texts that treat mind, language, and interrelated aspects of psychology. Topics to be discussed include the nature and functions of names and speech; the role of “correcting names”; semantic theory and argumentation; perception and knowledge; the role of language in knowledge and action; and the ontological grounds of linguistic distinctions. Texts to be discussed include the Analects, Guǎnzǐ, Mòzǐ, Mèngzǐ, Dàodéjīng, Xúnzǐ, Zhuāngzǐ, and Annals of Mr. Lǚ.
prerequisites: 7.5 credits, with at least 1.5 PHL credits including PHL237H1
difficulty_reasoning: Dense classical texts in unfamiliar philosophical tradition require sustained interpretive effort and significant translation context.

name: Jewish Philosophy
description: A selection of texts and issues in Jewish philosophy, for example, Maimonides' Guide of the Perplexed, Buber's The Prophetic Faith, prophecy and revelation, Divine Command and morality, creation and eternity, the historical dimension of Jewish thought.
prerequisites: 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Dense primary texts and unfamiliar philosophical traditions require sustained engagement, but course assumes no prior Jewish studies background.

name: Issues in Philosophy of Mind
description: Typical issues include: the mind-brain identity theory; intentionality and the mental; personal identity.
prerequisites: One of COG250Y1/PHL240H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced philosophical concepts require sustained abstract reasoning; prerequisite courses provide essential foundation but concepts remain genuinely demanding.

name: Freedom, Responsibility, and Human Action
description: Human action, and the nature of freedom and responsibility in the light of contemporary knowledge concerning the causation of behaviour.
prerequisites: PHL210Y1/PHL232H1/PHL233H1/PHL240H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced philosophical argument and contemporary empirical psychology require sustained conceptual engagement; prerequisites ensure readiness.

name: Minds and Machines
description: An investigation of philosophical questions raised by contemporary cognitive science and artificial intelligence (AI). Possible topics to be addressed: whether machines could think and be conscious; the computational theory of mind; embodied cognition and the extended mind hypothesis; the “singularity”; and moral and political implications of machine learning research.
prerequisites: 8.0 credits, including COG250Y1 or 1.5 credits in PHL
difficulty_reasoning: Philosophy course covering AI and cognition requires sustained conceptual engagement but no technical background, with moderate weekly reading and essay assignments.

name: Philosophy of Emotions
description: A survey of philosophical topics related to the emotions, from a range of philosophical perspectives. Questions to be considered may include the following: What exactly is an emotion? Are emotions feelings? What emotions are there, and how are they shaped by culture and society? How are emotions related to reason, the brain and the body? What role do -- and should -- the emotions play in decision-making? Can an emotion be morally right or wrong, and what makes it so?
prerequisites: PHL240H1/PHL244H1/PHL342H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Requires sustained philosophical argument analysis and essay writing; no technical skills needed but demands consistent engagement.

name: Intermediate Logic
description: A continuation of PHL245H1, requiring no other prior knowledge of philosophy or mathematics. First-order logic, including basic metalogical results such as soundness and completeness. An introduction to basic set theory and metalogic. Topics may include the Loewenheim-Skolem theorems for first-order logic, Goedel’s incompleteness theorems.
prerequisites: PHL245H1/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1 and a full course in PHL/CSC/MAT
difficulty_reasoning: Formal logic and metalogical proofs demand abstract mathematical thinking unfamiliar to most undeclared students encountering these concepts first.

name: Modal Logic and Philosophical Applications of Logic
description: Formal study of the concepts of necessity and possibility, modal, propositional and quantificational logic, possible-worlds semantics, and the metaphysics of modality. Other topics may include counterfactuals, truth, vagueness, epistemic logic, temporal logic, or non-classical logic.
prerequisites: PHL245H1/(MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1 and a full course in PHL/CSC/MAT
difficulty_reasoning: Modal logic requires mastering unfamiliar formal systems and abstract metaphysical concepts with minimal intuitive footing for undeclared students.

name: Philosophy of Mathematics
description: Platonism versus nominalism, the relation between logic and mathematics, implications of Gödel's theorem, formalism and intuitionism.
prerequisites: PHL245H1/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1 and a full course in PHL/CSC/MAT
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract philosophical foundations and Gödel's theorem demand sustained conceptual effort without typical math or logic prerequisites.

name: Philosophy of Natural Science
description: The structure and methods of science: explanation, methodology, realism and instrumentalism.
prerequisites: 7.5 credits which include one of the following combinations: (1) PHL255H1 and an additional 1.0 PHL credit or (2) 1.5 PHL credits and HPS250H1
difficulty_reasoning: Requires sustained engagement with abstract philosophical concepts about science, but no prior science specialization needed.

name: Philosophy of Physics
description: Introduction to philosophical issues which arise in modern physics, especially in relativity and quantum mechanics. This course will be accessible to students without a significant background in physics, but with an interest in the philosophical challenges that modern physics poses.
prerequisites: One full course in MAT/PHY; two full courses are recommended.
difficulty_reasoning: Philosophy-first framing makes quantum and relativity accessible without deep physics; typical 300-level reading and essay workload.

name: Philosophy of Biology
description: Philosophical issues in the foundations of biology, e.g., the nature of life, evolutionary theory; controversies about natural selection; competing mechanisms, units of selection; the place of teleology in biology; biological puzzles about sex and sexual reproduction; the problem of species; genetics and reductionism; sociobiology; natural and artificial life.
prerequisites: 7.5 credits which include one of the following: (1) PHL255H1 and an additional 1.0 PHL credit, (2) 1.5 PHL credits and HPS250H1, or (3) 1.5 credits in BCH/ BIO/ CSB/ EEB/ HMB/ IMM/ LMP/ MGY/ NFS/ PHC/ PCL/ PSL/ PSY.
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract philosophical concepts applied to biology require sustained engagement but no specialist background assumed; readings are substantial and dense.

name: Philosophy of History
description: Typical questions include: Has history any meaning? Can there be general theories of history? How are the findings of historians related to the theories of metaphysics and of science? Is history deterministic? Must the historian make value judgements? Is history science or an art? Are there historical forces or spirits of an epoch?
prerequisites: 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract philosophical questions require sustained conceptual thinking, but no technical prerequisites beyond careful reading and essay writing.

name: Topics in Political Philosophy
description: A focused examination of a selected issue in political philosophy.
prerequisites: PHL265H1/POL200Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level philosophy requires sustained analytical writing and close engagement with complex primary texts for first-time exposure.

name: Philosophy of Feminism
description: Selected issues and topics in the philosophy of feminism.
prerequisites: PHL265H1/PHL268H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level philosophy requires strong analytical reading and essay skills; prerequisite foundation makes it manageable but demanding.

name: Issues in Philosophy of Law
description: Major issues in philosophy of law, such as legal positivism and its critics, law and liberalism, feminist critiques of law, punishment and responsibility.
prerequisites: PHL271H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level philosophy requires strong critical thinking and abstract reasoning; manageable for engaged students with prior PHL271 foundation.

name: Issues in Environmental Ethics
description: An intermediate-level examination of key issues in environmental philosophy, such as the ethics of animal welfare, duties to future generations, deep ecology, ecofeminism, sustainable development and international justice.
prerequisites: PHL273H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate philosophy course requiring sustained engagement with unfamiliar concepts but manageable with consistent effort and strong preparation.

name: Ethics
description: An intermediate-level study of selected issues in moral philosophy, or of influential contemporary or historical works in ethical theory.
prerequisites: PHL275H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate philosophy course requiring sustained engagement with abstract ethical arguments and prior PHL275 foundation.

name: Topics in Moral Philosophy
description: A focused examination of a selected issue in moral philosophy.
prerequisites: PHL275H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Requires PHL275 foundation and strong analytical writing skills; abstract argumentation demands sustained engagement.

name: Ethics: Death and Dying
description: An intermediate-level study of moral and legal problems, including the philosophical significance of death, the high-tech prolongation of life, definition and determination of death, suicide, active and passive euthanasia, the withholding of treatment, palliative care and the control of pain, living wills; recent judicial decisions.
prerequisites: PHL281H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate philosophy course requiring sustained engagement with abstract ethical concepts and legal case analysis, but no prior specialist knowledge assumed.

name: Ethics and Mental Health
description: An intermediate-level study of moral and legal problems, including the concepts of mental health and illness, mental competence, dangerousness and psychiatric confidentiality, mental institutionalization, involuntary treatment and behaviour control, controversial therapies; legal issues: the Mental Health Act, involuntary commitment, the insanity defence.
prerequisites: PHL281H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate philosophy course requiring engagement with unfamiliar ethical and legal concepts but offering self-contained frameworks without deep prior background needed.

name: Ethics, Genetics and Reproduction
description: An intermediate-level study of moral and legal problems, including the ontological and moral status of the human embryo and fetus; human newborn, carrier and prenatal genetic screening for genetic defect, genetic therapy; the reproductive technologies (e.g., artificial insemination, in vitro fertilization); recent legislative proposals and judicial decisions.
prerequisites: PHL281H1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate philosophy course requiring engagement with unfamiliar ethical frameworks and biomedical concepts without deep prior science background.

name: Issues in Aesthetics
description: Selected topics in the philosophy of art. Such issues as the following are discussed: whether different arts require different aesthetic principles; relations between art and language; the adequacy of traditional aesthetics to recent developments in the arts; art as an institution.
prerequisites: 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level philosophy requiring sustained analytical reading and essay writing, but no technical prerequisites beyond prior philosophy exposure.

name: Literature and Philosophy
description: An examination of the interplays and tensions between literature and philosophy. Possible themes include: the 'literary' expression of philosophical ideas; the ancient 'quarrel of the poets and philosophers'; the relation of form to content in philosophical writing, and the immense variety of philosophical genres (e.g. aphorism, essai, confession, treatise, dialogue, manifesto, meditation, etc.); the philosophical content and significance of certain 'literary' works and forms; and philosophical problems regarding translation, adaptation, and interpretation. Topics and texts will vary according to instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical and literary texts require careful reading and interpretation, but no specialized background assumed beyond one prior philosophy course.

name: Markets and Morals
description: A study of the standards that can be used to judge the performance of economic systems, e.g., efficiency, fairness, maximization, along with the different institutional mechanisms that can be used to organize economic activity, e.g., markets or hierarchies, public or private ownership.
prerequisites: One of PHL265H1/PHL275H1/POL200Y1, 7.5 courses (in any field) with at least 1.5 in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Philosophy course requiring careful argument analysis and ethical reasoning, manageable with consistent engagement and prior coursework foundation.

name: Seminar in Ancient/Medieval Philosophy
description: Advanced discussion of the principal figures and themes in ancient and/or medieval philosophy.
prerequisites: PHL200Y1/ PHL205H1/ PHL206H1 and 4.0 credits in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar demands strong philosophical foundation and sustained close reading of demanding classical texts with minimal scaffolding.

name: Seminar in Early Modern Philosophy
description: Advanced discussion of the principal figures and themes in the philosophy of the 17th and/or 18th centuries.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar demands sustained philosophical argumentation and deep engagement with complex historical texts unfamiliar to most students.

name: Seminar in 19th-Century Philosophy
description: Advanced discussion of some principal figures and themes in 19th century philosophy.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar assumes solid philosophical foundation from prerequisites; dense primary texts and high-level argumentation require sustained intellectual maturity.

name: Seminar in Epistemology
description: Typical problems include the nature of knowledge and belief; perception; theories of truth and necessity; skepticism.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level philosophy seminar demands sustained abstract reasoning and specialized argumentative skills beyond typical first-year foundation.

name: Seminar in Philosophy of Mind
description: Advanced study of a problem in the philosophy of mind.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar demands philosophical maturity and sustained engagement with difficult primary texts and arguments.

name: Seminar in Metaphysics
description: Typical problems include causality and determinism; ontological categories; mind and body; the objectivity of space and time.
prerequisites: 4.0 credits in Philosophy including (one of PHL245H1/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1) and (PHL232H1/PHL233H1/PHL240H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Seminar demands sustained engagement with abstract metaphysical reasoning and assumes solid prior philosophy foundation from prerequisites.

name: Seminar in Ethics
description: Advanced discussion of issues in moral philosophy, including issues of applied ethics.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar requires strong philosophical foundation from PHL275H1; demanding discussion participation and essay writing expected.

name: Seminar in Philosophy
description: Topics vary but bridge two or more areas or traditions of philosophy.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar demands philosophical maturity and sustained critical writing; prerequisite breadth essential for navigating varied topics.

name: New Books Seminar
description: Advanced study of key philosophical works published within the last five years.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar demanding sustained engagement with recent philosophical texts and substantial written analysis throughout the semester.

name: Seminar in Continental Philosophy
description: Advanced study of recent philosophical discussions within the continental tradition.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar requiring deep continental philosophy knowledge built on four year foundation courses you likely lack.

name: Seminar in Political Philosophy
description: Advanced study of some topic in social or political philosophy.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar demands mature philosophical thinking and substantial prior philosophy foundation; average student will struggle without deeper disciplinary context.

name: Seminar in Applied Ethics
description: Advanced study of some topic in an area of applied ethics, including bioethics, environmental ethics, and so on.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar assumes solid philosophy foundation and requires close textual analysis and original argumentation throughout.

name: Seminar in Philosophy of Law
description: Advanced discussion of issues in the philosophy of law.
prerequisites: PHL265H1/PHL271H1, one of PHL365H1/PHL370H1/PHL375H1, 4.0 credits in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar requiring sustained philosophical writing and deep engagement with specialized legal and meta-ethical theory.

name: Clinical Bioethics
description: Advanced study of topics in bioethics, taught in conjunction with clinical bioethicists associated with the health care organization partners of the University of Toronto Joint Centre for Bioethics.
prerequisites: PHL281H1, 4.0 credits in philosophy. Limited to students enrolled in the Bioethics Specialist or Bioethics Major programs.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar requiring substantial philosophy foundation, clinical case analysis, and sustained engagement with specialized bioethical frameworks.

name: Seminar in Decision Theory
description: Decision theory studies what one ought to do when some more or less desirable outcomes of one’s actions depend on external facts about which one is uncertain. Uncertainty is characterized in terms of probabilities, desires are characterized in terms of utilities, and together they determine the expected utility of one’s actions. This course introduces these notions and their mathematical representations, as well as critically reflects on philosophical questions such as whether one should always take the action that maximize one’s expected utility, whether uncertainty is subjective or objective, and why uncertainty does, or ought to, obey the laws of probability.
difficulty_reasoning: Mathematical formalism and philosophical abstraction require sustained engagement; probability and utility theory are unfamiliar to most undeclared students.

name: Seminar in Philosophy of Language
description: Advanced study of some topic in the philosophy of language.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar requiring substantial philosophical maturity, close textual analysis, and sustained independent research on specialized topics.

name: Seminar in Philosophy of Science
description: Advanced study of some area or problem in the philosophy of science.
prerequisites: One of PHL355H1, PHL357H1 or HPS250H1, 4.0 credits in philosophy
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar requires sustained engagement with primary philosophical texts and rigorous argumentative writing at upper-year level.

name: Advanced Topics in Philosophy: Socrates Project
description: Socrates Project Seminar. The Socrates Project Seminar allows exceptional senior undergraduate students to become teaching assistants for a 1st year PHL course. In addition to grading and leading tutorials, Socrates Project students enroll in PHL489Y1 as a condition of their employment. Students are expected to apply for the Socrates Project in March prior to the start of the Fall/Winter term. Admission to the Socrates Project is limited to students enrolled in a Philosophy or Bioethics program. A minimum of 80% in PHL courses is required. Information about the Socrates Project including the application form can be found at https://philosophy.utoronto.ca/st-george/undergraduate-at-st-george/socrates-project-undergraduate-tas/
prerequisites: Permission of the instructor and Department
difficulty_reasoning: TA role demands simultaneous advanced seminar engagement and teaching responsibility; dual workload pressure is substantial.

name: Individual Studies
description: Individual Studies. Not eligible for CR/NCR option. Students must complete an Individual Study application form (found on the Department of Philosophy website) in consultation with their proposed faculty supervisor. The application form must be submitted to the Undergraduate Administrator by the first day of classes.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on chosen topic and supervisor expectations. Workload mirrors upper-year independent research with sustained weekly output.

name: Individual Studies
description: Individual Studies. Not eligible for CR/NCR option. Students must complete an Individual Study application form (found on the Department of Philosophy website) in consultation with their proposed faculty supervisor. The application form must be submitted to the Undergraduate Administrator by the first day of classes.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on chosen topic and supervisor expectations. Workload mirrors a directed research project with sustained independent work.

name: Individual Studies
description: Individual Studies. Not eligible for CR/NCR option. Students must complete an Individual Study application form (found on the Department of Philosophy website) in consultation with their proposed faculty supervisor. The application form must be submitted to the Undergraduate Administrator by the first day of classes.
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research project with faculty supervision demands self-directed work and sustained scholarly output throughout semester.

name: Individual Studies
description: Individual Studies. Not eligible for CR/NCR option. Students must complete an Individual Study application form (found on the Department of Philosophy website) in consultation with their proposed faculty supervisor. The application form must be submitted to the Undergraduate Administrator by the first day of classes.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on chosen topic and supervisor expectations; workload highly variable but typically substantial for independent research.

name: Individual Studies
description: Individual Studies. Not eligible for CR/NCR option. Students must complete an Individual Study application form (found on the Department of Philosophy website) in consultation with their proposed faculty supervisor. The application form must be submitted to the Undergraduate Administrator by the first day of classes.
difficulty_reasoning: Self-directed independent research with faculty mentorship demands disciplinary maturity and sustained initiative uncommon for undeclared students.

name: Individual Studies
description: Individual Studies. Not eligible for CR/NCR option. Students must complete an Individual Study application form (found on the Department of Philosophy website) in consultation with their proposed faculty supervisor. The application form must be submitted to the Undergraduate Administrator by the first day of classes.
