difficulty_reasoning: Entirely self-directed independent research with faculty supervision demands high maturity and sustained output without structured course scaffolding.

name: Introduction to Public Health
description: This course introduces students to basic Canadian public health-related concepts, significant aspects of the historical development of public health in Canada, and a selection of topics illustrating the broad and evolving nature of this field. In addition, it helps students explore the contributions that various disciplines/fields offer to public health in Canada.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory survey with no prerequisites; emphasizes Canadian public health history and concepts accessible to any curious student.

name: Introduction to Social Determinants of Health
description: A multidisciplinary approach to understanding non-medical factors that influence health, health equity, and primary health care. Themes include critical discussion of the measurements of health outcomes and the socioeconomic and political factors that affect health inequities, including neglected and marginalized populations in Canada and globally.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory social science with accessible readings on real-world health issues requires engagement but no specialist background.

name: Health Policy in Canada
description: Introduces students to health policy in Canada, with a particular focus on the social determinants of health, and on how to improve health through policy advocacy. Examines the policy making process in the Canadian context, and the development of health policy in Canada. Explores current debates about health issues.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites required, introduces policy concepts through Canadian context, relies on readings and discussion rather than technical skills.

name: Introduction to Quantitative Methods in Public Health
description: This course offers students in Public Health a basic understanding of the principles of quantitative research methods in health research, including research design, data collection, and analyses. Considerations such as validity, generalizability, and representativeness of data will be addressed. Topics may include experimental, comparative, and epidemiological approaches.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory quantitative content with no prerequisites, but statistics and research design require active engagement and practice.

name: Introduction to Qualitative Methods in Public Health
description: This course offers Public Health students a basic understanding of the principles of qualitative health research - which are critical to contextualizing the health practices, experiences, and outcomes amongst diverse populations. Qualitative research methods will examine research design, data collection, and interpretation in using various methodological approaches such as interviews, focus groups, observation, and case studies.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory qualitative methods course with no prerequisites; conceptual foundations are self-contained and accessible without specialist background.

name: Perspectives in Health, Gender, Ethnicity and Race
description: This course will examine historical and contemporary health disparities with respect to gender, ethnicity, and race. Other intersectional issues such as Colonialism, class, sexuality, and the urban/rural divide will also be considered as they to social determinants of health. Attention will be focused on examples of disparities that perpetuate and exacerbate current epidemiological challenges for underrepresented populations in Canada and globally.
prerequisites: EEB225H1/ GGR207H1/ HST250H1/ PHS250H1/ STA220H1/ STA288H1 or other equivalent
difficulty_reasoning: Content is conceptually accessible but requires sustained critical engagement with unfamiliar social science frameworks and policy examples.

name: Health, Nutrition and Food Security
description: This course examines the antecedents (for example: social structure, environments, human development and behaviour) that underlie nutrition-mediated aspects to human health and disease. This will include review and analysis of seminal, primary, and current research as well as contemporary issues surrounding nutritional literacy and deficits, food insecurity and access, as these relate to morbidity and pre-mature mortality. The course will also examine existing public health practices for health promotion and access, and population-level disease prevention and interventions.
prerequisites: EEB225H1/ GGR207H1/ HST250H1/ PHS250H1/ STA220H1/ STA288H1 or other equivalent
difficulty_reasoning: Public health concepts are accessible without prior expertise; research analysis and policy application require consistent engagement typical of 300-level courses.

name: Special Topics in Public Health I
description: The specific content of this course will vary from one year to another, depending on the availability of particular health researchers and scholars. Specific course information will be available on the Public Health website at www.uc.utoronto.ca/programs-study-academic-programs-public-health-health-studies.
difficulty_reasoning: Content varies yearly; likely introductory public health overview with standard readings and assignments manageable for undeclared students.

name: Aging and Health
description: Provides an understanding of the interface between health and aging, and how to maintain wellness in a rapidly aging population. Examines theories of aging, the meaning of health in old age, myths about the health of Canada’s older people, and emerging national and international responses to aging populations.
difficulty_reasoning: Social science survey requiring prerequisite knowledge but no specialist background; manageable weekly readings and essays.

name: Special Topics in Public Health II
description: The specific content of this course will vary from one year to another, depending on the availability of particular health researchers and scholars. Specific course information will be available on the Public Health website at www.uc.utoronto.ca/programs-study-academic-programs-public-health-health-studies.
difficulty_reasoning: Content varies yearly; assume intermediate public health topics requiring basic research literacy and engagement without specialist prerequisites.

name: Critical Health Policy
description: A critical, in-depth exploration of contemporary health and social issues. Political, social and economic forces at play in Canadian society are examined in relation to specific health issues and policies, in order to understand general societal and system dynamics of evolution and change, and to identify implications for reform efforts.
difficulty_reasoning: Policy analysis requires critical thinking and synthesis, but no technical background needed beyond prerequisite foundation.

name: Population Health
description: Extends students´ understanding of population-based strategies of health promotion in Canada. Topics include: variations in health status as affected by population patterns, class, gender, ethnicity, employment, and family composition; major causes of morbidity and mortality; the concept of "community health", opportunities and constraints facing public policy.
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on prerequisite health foundation; mixes policy concepts with accessible social science rather than technical rigor.

name: Health Research & Practice
description: For students enrolled in the Public Health or Health Studies programs. Students engage in career-planning, presentation, and workshop activities that will prepare them for independent research and practicum projects as well as potential graduate-school applications. Includes, but not limited to, sessions on topic selection and project development, finding a supervisor, ethics and professionalism.
difficulty_reasoning: Workshop and career-planning focus requires no specialist knowledge, just engagement with practical professional development materials.

name: Epidemiology
description: Introduces students to the principles and methods of epidemiology. Emphasis on descriptive methods and study design. Computational techniques, measurement problems, and issues that surround the drawing of inferences from area-level or other aggregate data will be discussed.
difficulty_reasoning: Foundational epidemiology course builds on statistics prerequisite; computational and study design concepts require consistent engagement.

name: Public Health Practicum
description: For students enrolled in the Public Health or Health Studies programs. Individual field placement with a health-related institution or organization, in which the student applies theory and skills to specific projects and/or tasks. Culminates in an oral and written report. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: HST350H1/ PHS350H1. Students who do not meet the prerequisites are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Field placement demands real-world application and sustained output, but mentorship and hands-on learning reduce theoretical difficulty.

name: Special Topics in Advanced Public Health II
description: The specific content of this course will vary from one year to another, depending on the availability of particular health researchers and scholars. Specific course information will be available on the Public Health website at www.uc.utoronto.ca/programs-study-academic-programs-public-health-health-studies.
difficulty_reasoning: Content varies yearly; assumes foundational public health literacy. Typical upper-level seminar with moderate independent engagement.

name: Case Studies in Health Policy
description: Analyses the formation and implementation of health policy through the use of case studies. Each case deals with a substantive health policy issue. Using case studies, students will learn to understand and analyze the processes by which public policies are formed, and perform comparisons of policy alternatives.
difficulty_reasoning: Case study analysis requires policy literacy and critical thinking, but no advanced technical skills or deep disciplinary background are needed.

name: Political Economy of Health
description: Examines the production and distribution of health and illness within and across populations. A political economy approach is used to better understand the fundamental origins of health inequalities, the political action that is required to tackle them, and the obstacles that hinder the possibilities for such action.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires foundation from prerequisite and engagement with social theory, but conceptually accessible without specialist background.

name: Public Health Policy Engagement and Action
description: This course emphasizes an understanding of population health research and historical evidence in the development of health policies. We explore whether and how policies are evidence-informed, sustainable, and equitable. Knowledge translation and social justice frameworks will be examined to understand bottlenecks or obstacles for translating evidence into community engagement and action. Students will engage in case study analyses to understand the role of public engagement and action (e.g. private sectors, communities, government, non-profits, etc.) in the evolution of programs and policies.
exclusions: HST407H1 (Public Health Policy Engagement and Action), offered in Fall 2022, 2023, and 2024; HST432H1
difficulty_reasoning: Policy analysis and case studies are accessible without specialist background, but consistent weekly engagement with evidence synthesis demands effort.

name: Health & Pharmaceuticals
description: A critical examination of drug development, including the role of health professionals and the pharmaceutical industry in researching, testing, marketing, licensing, and evaluating pharmaceutical drugs. Topics include the differing needs for drug development, evaluation and dispensing in lower income countries, and potential conflicts of interest in drug development.
difficulty_reasoning: Policy-focused course on familiar health topics requires critical thinking but assumes no specialized science background.

name: Undergraduate Research Project in Public Health
description: Final culminating project for students in the Health Studies Specialist. Students will prepare an undergraduate thesis by the end of the course under the supervision of a University of Toronto faculty member. Students must submit an Independent Research Course Proposal form confirming a faculty supervisor has agreed to participate and describing the content of the project by the end of August. This form is available on the University College Public Health Program website. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: (HST250H1/ PHS250H1), STA220H1, (HST350Y1/ PHS350Y1) and an Independent Research Course Proposal form
difficulty_reasoning: Year-long independent thesis requires sustained research, writing, and faculty collaboration with minimal structured guidance throughout.

name: Independent Research Project in Public Health
description: This two-semester course designation will permit students to gain academic credit for Public Health students to pursue independently, or to participate in an ongoing health research project, under the supervision of a University of Toronto faculty member. Students must submit an Independent Research Course Proposal form confirming a faculty supervisor has agreed to participate and describing the content of the project by the end of August. This form is available on the University College Public Health Program website. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: HST209H1/ PHS209H1, HST250H1/ PHS250H1, and an Independent Research Course Proposal form
difficulty_reasoning: Self-directed research demands sustained independent work, critical thinking, and faculty collaboration without structured course scaffolding.

name: The Nature of Global Health
description: Explores the historical, ideological, economic, political, and organizational factors that shape the theory and practice of international health (IH). Students will be encouraged to critically examine some of the central tensions embedded in this field.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level health/policy course requiring critical thinking about complex systems, but no technical prerequisites and essay-based assessment.

name: Independent Research in Public Health
description: This one-semester course will permit students to gain academic credit for Public Health students to pursue independently, or to participate in an ongoing health research project, under the supervision of a University of Toronto faculty member. Students must submit an Independent Research Course Proposal form confirming a faculty supervisor has agreed to participate and describing the content of the project at least two weeks prior to the first day of term. This form is available on the University College Public Health Program website. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: HST209H1/ PHS209H1, HST250H1/ PHS250H1, and an Independent Research Course Proposal form
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research requires self-direction and disciplinary maturity; supervision helps but students drive project scope and execution.

name: The Magic of Physics
description: This course provides a survey of Physics, including both Classical and Modern Physics. It is designed for non-scientists, and assumes no background in either science or mathematics. The approach to the course is broad rather than deep. We will concentrate on the concepts underlying such fascinating topics as planetary motion, chaos, the nature of light, time travel, black holes, matter waves, Schrodinger's cat, quarks, and climate change. We will uncover the wonders of the classical and the quantum worlds courtesy of Galileo, Newton, Maxwell, Einstein, Heisenberg and many others. (PHY100H1 is primarily intended as a Breadth Requirement course for students in the Humanities and Social Sciences with no university-level background in physics. Any student with university-level credit in physics, including students with secondary school transfer credits in physics is ineligible to take this course).
exclusions: Any PHY course taken previously or concurrently (except PHY196H1/ PHY197H1/ PHY198H1/ PHY199H1/ PHY202H1/ PHY205H1/ PHY207H1)
difficulty_reasoning: Explicitly designed for non-scientists with no prerequisites; conceptual breadth over mathematical depth makes it highly accessible.

name: Introduction to Physics I
description: A first university physics course primarily for students not intending to pursue a Specialist or Major program in Physical or Mathematical Sciences. Topics include: classical kinematics & dynamics, momentum, energy, force, friction, work, power, angular momentum, oscillations, waves, sound. Lab kit fees of approximately $53 may apply.
corequisites: MAT130H1/ MAT135H1/ MAT148H1/ MAT137Y1/ MAT158H1/ MAT157Y1 recommended
recommended_preparation: MCV4U Calculus & Vectors/ MHF4U Functions & Calculus, SPH4U Physics
difficulty_reasoning: Weekly problem sets and lab work demand consistent effort; concepts are introductory but require mathematical practice.

name: Introduction to Physics II
description: The second university physics course primarily for students not intending to pursue a Specialist or Major program in Physical or Mathematical Sciences. Topics include: electricity, magnetism, light, optics, special relativity. Lab kit fees of approximately $53 may apply.
corequisites: MAT136H1/ MAT149H1/ MAT137Y1/ MAT159H1/ MAT157Y recommended
difficulty_reasoning: Electricity and magnetism require abstract conceptual thinking; weekly problem sets and labs demand sustained effort.

name: Foundations of Physics I
description: The first physics course in many of the Specialist and Major Programs in Physical Sciences. It provides an introduction to the concepts, approaches and tools the physicist uses to describe the physical world while laying the foundation for classical and modern mechanics. Topics include: mathematics of physics, energy, momentum, conservation laws, kinematics, dynamics, and special relativity.
prerequisites: MCV4U Calculus & Vectors / MCB4U Functions & Calculus; SPH4U Physics
corequisites: MAT130H1/ MAT135H1/ MAT148H1/ MAT137Y1/ MAT158H1/ MAT157Y1 (MAT148H1 recommended)
difficulty_reasoning: Calculus-heavy problem sets and abstract conceptual reasoning demanding sustained effort weekly from undeclared students.

name: Foundations of Physics II
description: The concept of fields will be introduced and discussed in the context of gravity and electricity. Topics include rotational motion, oscillations, waves, electricity and magnetism.
corequisites: MAT136H1/ MAT149H1/ MAT137Y1/ MAT159H1/ MAT157Y1 (MAT149H1 recommended)
difficulty_reasoning: Heavy weekly problem sets and new abstract concepts like fields require sustained effort and mathematical maturity beyond first-year basics.

name: Emergence in Nature
description: The universe is not a rigid clockwork, but neither is it formless and random. Instead, it is filled with highly organized, evolved structures that have somehow emerged from simple rules of physics. Examples range from the structure of galaxies to the pattern of ripples on windblown sand, to biological and even social processes. These phenomena exist in spite of the universal tendency towards disorder. How is this possible? Self-organization challenges the usual reductionistic scientific method, and begs the question of whether we can ever really understand or predict truly complex systems. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: No prior experience with physical science will be required, but familiarity with Grade 10 mathematics will be assumed.
difficulty_reasoning: Conceptual first-year survey requiring curiosity and engagement, not math or physics prerequisites.

name: Modern Physics for the Curious
description: Have you wondered about the origin and workings of the natural world around us? Have you found physical science interesting but inaccessible because it was too full of math and jargon? Have you felt a pull to become more science-literate? If so, this seminar course is for you. Ideas on the menu may include: space and time, relativity, determinism & randomness, particle physics, quantum physics, quantum information, and the fate of the Universe. Students from diverse academic backgrounds are warmly welcomed. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: No prior experience with physical science will be required, but familiarity with Grade 10 mathematics will be assumed.
difficulty_reasoning: Conceptual seminar explicitly designed for curious non-specialists; minimal math required and no problem sets expected.

name: Physics at the Cutting Edge
description: A limited enrollment seminar course for First Year Science students interested in current research in Physics. Students will meet active researchers studying the universe from the centre of the earth to the edge of the cosmos. Topics may range from string theory to experimental biological physics, from climate change to quantum computing, from superconductivity to earthquakes. The course may involve both individual and group work, essays and oral presentations. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Seminar format reduces conceptual barrier but essay and presentation assignments create consistent weekly preparation demands.

name: Dark Matter and Dark Energy are the New Black
description: It is now 90 years since astronomers found the first evidence for a form of matter that wasn't part of the stars in our galaxies, but rather is "dark" and has a gravitational attraction to ordinary matter. Other lines of evidence lead us to believe that there is six times more dark matter than the ordinary matter we are familiar with. Despite this, we have no credible, direct evidence for what this dark matter might be. It is one of the biggest puzzles in particle physics and cosmology. In the last decade, we have also discovered that something else is going on – the universe appears to be filled with "dark energy" that causes the expansion of our universe to speed up instead of slowdown. We will discuss what we know about the hypotheses of dark matter and dark energy, and the debates about what might really be going on. Are we seeing science in crisis, with a revolution just around the corner, or is this just the "normal science" talked about by Kuhn and other philosophers of science? Participants will be expected to participate in seminar-style discussions, as well as take the lead on at least one topic of discussion. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: No prior experience with physical science will be required, but familiarity with Grade 10 mathematics will be assumed.
difficulty_reasoning: Seminar format with no math or prior physics required; reading and discussion-based rather than problem-solving intensive.

name: The Physics of Science Fiction and Gaming
description: The physics of time travel, teleportation, levitation, invisibility, special effects, and other physics related topics found in literature, film, and gaming. The course will analyze the realism of physical phenomena in these media, and consider the impact of these concepts on science and society. (PHY202H1 is primarily intended as a Breadth Requirement course for students in the Humanities and Social Sciences with no university-level background in physics. Any student with university-level credit in physics, including students with secondary school transfer credits in physics is ineligible to take this course).
exclusions: Any PHY course taken previously or concurrently (except PHY196H1/ PHY197H1/ PHY198H1/ PHY199H1/ PHY100H1/ PHY205H1/ PHY207H1).
difficulty_reasoning: Conceptual breadth course using familiar media as entry point to physics, no math prerequisites or problem sets required.

name: The Physics of Everyday Life
description: An introduction to the physics of everyday life. This conceptual course looks at everyday objects to learn about the basis for our modern technological world. Topics may include anything from automobiles to weather. (PHY205H1 is primarily intended as a Breadth Requirement course for students in the Humanities and Social Sciences with no university-level background in physics. Any student with university-level credit in physics, including students with secondary school transfer credits in physics is ineligible to take this course).
exclusions: Any PHY course taken previously or concurrently (except PHY196H1/ PHY197H1/ PHY198H1/ PHY199H1/ PHY100H1/ PHY202H1/ PHY207H1).
difficulty_reasoning: Conceptual physics course with no prerequisites designed for non-science majors encountering physics for the first time.

name: The Physics of Music
description: An online course intended to provide non-science students with a basic understanding of the science behind sound and music. Topics include oscillations, waves, human hearing and perception of music, musical scales, musical instruments, recording and storing sound digitally, producing sound and broadcasting. Lectures will be delivered via the web and mandatory tutorials will require live webinar participation. The final exam will require attendance on the St. George campus. (PHY207H1 is primarily intended as a Breadth Requirement course for students with no university-level background in physics. Any student with university-level credit in physics, including students with secondary school transfer credits in physics is ineligible to take this course).
exclusions: Any PHY course taken previously or concurrently (except PHY196H1/ PHY197H1/ PHY198H1/ PHY199H1/ PHY100H1/ PHY202H1/ PHY205H1).
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory physics concepts presented intuitively through music; no math background required, self-contained material.

name: Practical Physics I
description: Develops the core practical experimental and computational skills necessary to do physics. Students tackle simple physics questions involving mathematical models, computational simulations and solutions, experimental measurements, data and uncertainty analysis.
prerequisites: PHY131H1/ PHY151H1, PHY132H1/ PHY152H1, (MAT130H1/ MAT135H1, MAT136H1)/ (MAT148H1, MAT149H1)/ MAT137Y1/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Hands-on experiments and computational work require sustained effort; data analysis and coding are new to most undeclared students.

name: Physics of Living Systems
description: An introductory course for students interested in understanding the physical phenomena occurring in biological systems and the applications of physics in life sciences. Topics may include physical processes inside living cells and systems, medical physics and imaging.
prerequisites: PHY131H1/ PHY151H1, PHY132H1/ PHY152H1, (MAT130H1/ MAT135H1, MAT136H1)/ (MAT148H1, MAT149H1)/ MAT137Y1/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Applies familiar physics concepts to biology; manageable for students with PHY131/132 foundation but requires engagement with unfamiliar applications.

name: Electricity and Magnetism
description: An introductory course in Electromagnetism. Topics include: Point charges, Coulomb’s law, electrostatic field and potential, Gauss's Law, conductors, electrostatic energy, magnetostatics, Ampere's Law, Biot-Savart Law, the Lorentz Force Law, Faraday’s Law, Maxwell's equations in free space.
prerequisites: PHY131H1/ PHY151H1, PHY132H1/ PHY152H1, (MAT130H1/ MAT135H1, MAT136H1)/ (MAT148H1, MAT149H1)/ MAT137Y1/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1
recommended_preparation: If students use MAT235H1 to satisfy the Corequisite, enrolling and completing MAT236H1 as well is strongly recommended.
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract vector calculus concepts and mathematical derivations demand sustained effort; weekly problem sets are time-intensive for most students.

name: Thermal Physics
description: The quantum statistical basis of macroscopic systems; definition of entropy in terms of the number of accessible states of a many particle system leading to simple expressions for absolute temperature, the canonical distribution, and the laws of thermodynamics. Specific effects of quantum statistics at high densities and low temperatures.
prerequisites: PHY131H1/ PHY151H1, PHY132H1/ PHY152H1, (MAT130H1/ MAT135H1, MAT136H1)/ (MAT148H1, MAT149H1)/ MAT137Y1/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Requires mastery of statistical mechanics and advanced math with no assumed background in either subject matter.

name: Classical Mechanics
description: The course analyzes the linear, nonlinear and chaotic behaviour of classical mechanical systems such as harmonic oscillators, rotating bodies, and central field systems. The course will develop the analytical and numerical tools to solve such systems and determine their basic properties.
prerequisites: PHY131H1/ PHY151H1, PHY132H1/ PHY152H1, (MAT130H1/ MAT135H1, MAT136H1)/ (MAT148H1, MAT149H1)/ MAT137Y1)/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract concepts (Lagrangian mechanics, chaos theory) and computational problem-solving demand sustained mathematical maturity beyond typical first-year foundation.

name: Introduction to Quantum Physics
description: Failures of classical physics; the Quantum revolution; Stern-Gerlach effect; harmonic oscillator; uncertainty principle; interference packets; scattering and tunneling in one-dimension.
prerequisites: PHY131H1/ PHY151H1, PHY132H1/ PHY152H1, (MAT130H1/ MAT135H1, MAT136H1)/ (MAT148H1, MAT149H1)/ MAT137Y1/ (MAT158H1, MAT159H1)/ MAT157Y1
corequisites: MAT235H1/ MAT235Y1/ MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1, (MAT223H1/ MAT240H1 recommended)
difficulty_reasoning: Quantum mechanics demands conceptual abstraction entirely foreign to classical intuition; weekly problem sets and derivations are mathematically dense.

name: Quantum Information
description: Introduction to quantum computing; Quantum states of multi-particle systems and Entanglement; Quantum Algorithms; Quantum Information Processing Technologies; Quantum error correction.
prerequisites: PHY131H1/ PHY151H1, PHY132H1/ PHY152H1, MAT223H1/ MAT240H1
difficulty_reasoning: Quantum mechanics demands abstract mathematical maturity and conceptual leaps rare in first year, despite clear prerequisites.

name: Research Opportunity Program
description: Credit course for supervised participation in faculty research project. Details at https://www.artsci.utoronto.ca/current/academics/research-opportunities/research-opportunities-program. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Research project intensity and supervisor expectations vary widely, but sustained independent lab work typically demands 10–12 weekly hours.

name: Practical Physics II
description: A modular practical course that further develops the core experimental and computational skills necessary to do physics. Modules include: experimental skills building, computational tools in data and uncertainty analysis, and independent experimental projects.
prerequisites: PHY224H1, PHY231H1/ PHY250H1/ PHY252H1/ PHY254H1/ PHY256H1
difficulty_reasoning: Lab-based course demands hands-on experimental design, data analysis, and technical report writing weekly.

name: Introduction to Biological Physics
description: A course for students interested in a deeper understanding of physical phenomena occurring in biological systems. Thermodynamics, diffusion, entropic forces, fluids, biological applications.
prerequisites: PHY252H1/ CHM222H1, PHY231H1/ PHY250H1/ PHY254H1/ PHY256H1
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on prior physics and chemistry; conceptual material is accessible but problem-solving requires solid quantitative foundation.

name: Special Topics in Physics
description: Content in any given year varies by instructor. Students must meet the prerequisites set by the department (see the Physics website for details in May).
prerequisites: To be specified annually on the course website, as stated in the calendar description. This is a 300-level course and will typically have 200-level MAT and PHY prerequisites.
corequisites: To be specified annually (if any) on the course website, as stated in the calendar description.
difficulty_reasoning: Content varies yearly; 300-level physics demands strong math foundation and upper-year maturity most undeclared students lack.

name: Electromagnetic Theory
description: This course builds upon the knowledge and tools developed in PHY250H1. Topics include: solving Poisson and Laplace equations via method of images and separation of variables, multipole expansion for electrostatics, atomic dipoles and polarizability, polarization in dielectrics, multipole expansion in magnetostatics, magnetic dipoles, magnetization in matter, Maxwell’s equations in matter, conservation laws in electrodynamics, and electromagnetic waves.
prerequisites: MAT223H1/ MAT240H1, MAT244H1/ MAT267H1, (MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1 / MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1, PHY250H1
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced mathematical techniques and abstract field concepts require substantial prior physics and multivariable calculus mastery.

name: Advanced Classical Mechanics
description: Symmetry and conservation laws, stability and instability, generalized coordinates, Hamilton's principle, Hamilton's equations, phase space, Liouville's theorem, canonical transformations, Poisson brackets, Noether's theorem.
prerequisites: MAT223H1/ MAT240H1, MAT244H1/ MAT267H1,(MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1 / MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1, PHY254H1
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract mathematical formalism with no intuitive anchors demands sustained disciplinary maturity beyond typical second-year preparation.

name: Quantum Mechanics I
description: The general structure of wave mechanics; eigenfunctions and eigenvalues; operators; orbital angular momentum; spherical harmonics; central potential; separation of variables; hydrogen atom; Dirac notation; operator methods; harmonic oscillator and spin.
prerequisites: MAT223H1/ MAT240H1,(MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1 / MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1, PHY250H1, PHY256H1/ (CHM222H1, CHM223H1), (PHY256H1 recommended)
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract formalism with heavy math foundation required; weekly problem sets demand sustained engagement and conceptual struggle.

name: Nuclear and Particle Physics
description: The subatomic particles; nuclei, baryons and mesons, quarks, leptons and bosons; the structure of nuclei and hadronic matter; symmetries and conservation laws; fundamental forces and interactions, electromagnetic, weak, and strong; a selection of other topics: CP violation, nuclear models, standard model, proton decay, supergravity, nuclear and particle astrophysics. This course is not a prerequisite for any PHY400-level course.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires mastery of quantum mechanics and relativity; abstract mathematical framework unfamiliar to most undeclared students encountering particle physics.

name: Quantum Materials: from Atoms to Crystals
description: This course covers the most important iconic quantum systems, from the hydrogen atom through to solid state systems, focusing on how quantum mechanics is applied and determines physical properties of atoms, molecules, and crystals. It begins with the hydrogen atom, including orbital and spin angular momentum, spin-orbit coupling, and effects of the magnetic field, and then extends to systems of two identical particles: bosons vs. fermions and the helium atom with two electrons. Other topics include spin singlets and triplets, entanglement, perturbation theory, the effects of electron-electron interactions and diatomic molecules. For crystals, the course covers Fermi gases, Fermi surfaces, crystal structure, the reciprocal lattice, the nearly-free electron model, energy bands, and topology using low-dimensional models.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced quantum theory applied to extended systems requires deep mathematical maturity and significant cumulative physics background.

name: Understanding Neural Networks using Physics
description: Study of neural network architectures and algorithms from the perspective of physics, using both analytical and numerical tools. Topics will include classification and regression with fully-connected networks; physics-inspired optimizers and activation functions; generative models such as normalizing flows and diffusion models; residual networks and transformers.
prerequisites: MAT223H1/ MAT240H1, MAT235Y1/ MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1, PHY224H1, PHY252H1, PHY254H1
difficulty_reasoning: Requires fluent multivariable calculus, physics intuition, and machine learning concepts simultaneously without specialist grounding.

name: Supervised Study in Physics
description: An individual study program chosen by the student with the advice of, and under the direction of, a staff member. A student may take advantage of this course either to specialize further in a field of interest or to explore interdisciplinary fields not available in the regular syllabus. Consult the department web pages for some possible topics. This course may also be available in the summer. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Consult the Physics Associate Chair (Undergraduate Studies)
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends heavily on chosen topic; workload is self-directed but requires sustained independent research and writing.

name: Supervised Study in Physics
description: An individual study program chosen by the student with the advice of, and under the direction of, a staff member. A student may take advantage of this course either to specialize further in a field of interest or to explore interdisciplinary fields not available in the regular syllabus. Consult the department web site for some possible topics. This course may also be available in the summer. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Consult the Physics Associate Chair (Undergraduate Studies)
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on chosen topic; workload driven by independent research, writing, and frequent supervisor meetings.

name: Undergraduate Research Project
description: An individual experimental or theoretical research project undertaken with the advice of, and under the direction of, a staff member. A student may take advantage of this course either to specialize further in a field of interest or to explore independent research. Consult the department web site for some possible topics. This course may also be available in the summer. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Consult the Physics Associate Chair (Undergraduate Studies)
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research demands self-direction, experimental or theoretical depth, and sustained output that most undeclared students lack prior experience managing.

name: Undergraduate Research Project
description: An individual experimental or theoretical research project undertaken with the advice of, and under the direction of, a staff member. A student may take advantage of this course either to specialize further in a field of interest or to explore independent research. Consult the department web site for some possible topics. This course may also be available in the summer. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Consult the Physics Associate Chair (Undergraduate Studies)
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research demands sustained self-direction, original problem-solving, and frequent advisor interaction without structured course scaffolding.

name: Introductory Optics
description: An introduction to the physics of light. Topics covered include: electromagnetic waves and propagation of light; the Huygens and Fermat principles; geometrical optics and optical instruments; interference of waves and diffraction; polarization; introduction to photons, lasers, and optical fibers.
prerequisites: PHY250H1, PHY224H1, (MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1 / MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Wave physics and mathematical optics demand strong calculus foundation and sustained problem-solving; most undeclared students lack confident background.

name: Physics of Climate
description: This course provides an introduction to climate physics and the earth-atmosphere-ocean system. Topics include solar and terrestrial radiation; global energy balance; radiation laws; radiative transfer; atmospheric structure; convection; the meridional structure of the atmosphere; the general circulation of the atmosphere; the ocean and its circulation; and climate variability.
prerequisites: PHY250H1/ PHY252H1, (MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1/ MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced mathematics and physics prerequisites required; conceptually demanding radiative transfer and atmospheric dynamics material unfamiliar to undeclared students.

name: Electronics Lab
description: Electrical circuits, networks and devices are all-pervasive in the modern world. This laboratory course is an introduction to the world of electronics. Students will learn the joys and perils of electronics, by designing, constructing and debugging circuits and devices. The course will cover topics ranging from filters and operational amplifiers to micro-controllers, and will introduce students to concepts such as impedance, transfer functions, feedback and noise.
prerequisites: PHY224H1, PHY250H1. Note that PHY405H1 may be taken in third or fourth year.
difficulty_reasoning: Hands-on lab with circuit design, debugging, and unfamiliar concepts requires sustained effort and prior physics foundation.

name: Computational Physics
description: This is an introduction to scientific computing in physics, using Python. Students will be introduced to computational techniques used in a range of physics research areas. By considering select physics topics, students will learn basic computational methods for function analysis (computing integrals and derivatives; finding roots and extrema), resolution of linear and non-linear equations, eigenvalue problems, Fourier analysis, ODEs, PDEs and Monte Carlo techniques. As the course progresses, students will develop their skills at debugging, solution visualization, computational efficiency and accuracy.
prerequisites: PHY224H1, PHY250H1/ PHY252H1/ PHY254H1/ PHY256H1, (MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1/ MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1
corequisites: Any 300/ 400-level lecture course in Physics (PHY/ JPE)
difficulty_reasoning: First coding experience combined with advanced physics concepts requires sustained effort and debugging practice throughout semester.

name: Time Series Analysis
description: The analysis of digital sequences; filters; the Fourier Transform; windows; truncation effects; aliasing; auto and cross-correlation; stochastic processes, power spectra; least squares filtering; application to real data series and experimental design.
prerequisites: PHY224H1/ ESS245H1, PHY250H1/ PHY252H1/ PHY254H1/ PHY256H1, (MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1 / MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1.
corequisites: Any 300/ 400-level lecture course in Physics (PHY/ JPE)
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced signal processing with rigorous math and coding demands real disciplinary maturity most undeclared students lack.

name: Advanced Physics Laboratory
description: Experiments in this course are designed to form a bridge to current experimental research. A wide range of exciting experiments relevant to modern research in physics is available. The laboratory is normally open from 9 a.m. - 4 p.m., Monday to Friday.
prerequisites: PHY250H1, PHY256H1, PHY324H1, PHY350H1/ PHY354H1/ PHY356H1
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced experimental design and research-level lab work demand mastery of prior physics courses and sustained technical output.

name: Advanced Practical Physics I
description: This course is a continuation of PHY424H1, but students have more freedom to progressively focus on specific areas of physics, do extended experiments, projects, or computational modules.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced experimental physics requiring sustained lab work, technical writing, and independent project execution throughout semester.

name: Advanced Practical Physics II
description: This course is a continuation of PHY426H1, but students have more freedom to progressively focus on specific areas of physics, do extended experiments, projects, or computational modules.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced experimental physics demands hands-on technical skill, independent problem-solving, and sustained lab work outside class time.

name: Topics in Biological Physics
description: An introduction to the physical phenomena involved in the biological processes of living cells and complex systems. Models based on physical principles applied to cellular processes will be developed. Biological computational modeling will be introduced.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced physics and biology integration demands strong math foundation from prerequisites; computational modeling adds technical depth.

name: Relativistic Electrodynamics
description: An introduction to relativistic electrodynamics. Topics include: special relativity, four-vectors and tensors, relativistic dynamics from the Principle of Stationary Action and Maxwell's equations in Lorentz covariant form. Noether's theorem for fields and the energy-momentum tensor. Fields of moving charges and electromagnetic radiation: retarded potential, Lienard-Wiechert potentials, multipole expansion, radiation reaction.
difficulty_reasoning: Relativistic formalism, tensor calculus, and Lagrangian mechanics demand sustained mathematical maturity rarely developed before upper-year physics.

name: Statistical Mechanics
description: Classical and quantum statistical mechanics of noninteracting systems; the statistical basis of thermodynamics; ensembles, partition function; thermodynamic equilibrium; stability and fluctuations; formulation of quantum statistics; theory of simple gases; ideal Bose and Fermi systems.
difficulty_reasoning: Abstract mathematical formalism and quantum concepts demand sustained mastery; weekly problem sets are conceptually dense and time-consuming.

name: Continuum Mechanics
description: The theory of continuous matter, including solid mechanics with a strong focus on fluid mechanics. Topics include the continuum approximation, dimensional analysis, stress, strain, the Euler and Navier-Stokes equations, vorticity, waves, instabilities, convection and turbulence.
prerequisites: PHY254H1, (MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1/ MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1, APM346H1/ APM351Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced mathematical techniques and fluid dynamics require strong multivariable calculus foundation; steep conceptual jump from introductory physics.

name: Quantum Mechanics II
description: Quantum dynamics in Heisenberg and Schrödinger pictures; WKB approximation; variational method; time-independent perturbation theory; spin; addition of angular momentum; time-dependent perturbation theory; scattering.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced mathematical techniques (WKB, perturbation theory, scattering) require sustained mastery of prior quantum foundations.

name: Nonlinear Physics
description: The theory of nonlinear dynamical systems with applications to many areas of physics. Topics include stability, bifurcations, chaos, universality, maps, strange attractors and fractals. Geometric, analytical and computational methods will be developed.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced mathematical concepts and computational methods require strong PHY354 foundation; typical student finds chaos theory and bifurcations conceptually challenging.

name: Supervised Study in Physics
description: An individual study program chosen by the student with the advice of, and under the direction of, a staff member. A student may take advantage of this course either to specialize further in a field of interest or to explore interdisciplinary fields not available in the regular syllabus. Consult the department web pages for some possible topics. This course may also be available in the summer. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Consult the Physics Associate Chair (Undergraduate Studies).
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research project with faculty supervision demands self-directed learning, advanced physics knowledge, and sustained output.

name: Supervised Study in Physics
description: An individual study program chosen by the student with the advice of, and under the direction of, a staff member. A student may take advantage of this course either to specialize further in a field of interest or to explore interdisciplinary fields not available in the regular syllabus. Consult the department web pages for some possible topics. This course may also be available in the summer. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Consult the Physics Associate Chair (Undergraduate Studies).
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty and scope depend entirely on chosen topic and supervisor; most students lack background for advanced physics research without significant mentorship.

name: Undergraduate Research Project
description: An individual experimental or theoretical research project undertaken with the advice of, and under the direction of, a staff member. A student may take advantage of this course either to specialize further in a field of interest or to explore independent research. Consult the department web site for some possible topics. This course may also be available in the summer. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Consult the Physics Associate Chair (Undergraduate Studies)
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research demands self-direction, experimental troubleshooting, and sustained output across a full semester without structured scaffolding.

name: Undergraduate Research Project
description: An individual experimental or theoretical research project undertaken with the advice of, and under the direction of, a faculty member. A student may take advantage of this course either to specialize further in a field of interest or to explore independent research. Consult the department web site for possible topics. This course may also be available in the summer. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Consult the Physics Associate Chair (Undergraduate Studies)
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research demands sustained self-direction, problem-solving, and faculty collaboration without structured course support or scaffolding.

name: Relativity Theory I
description: Basis of Einstein's theory: differential geometry, tensor analysis, gravitational physics leading to General Relativity. Theory starting from solutions of Schwarzschild, Kerr, etc.
difficulty_reasoning: Differential geometry and tensor calculus are abstract, unfamiliar tools requiring sustained mathematical maturity beyond typical first-year exposure.

name: Relativity Theory II
description: Applications of General Relativity to Astrophysics and Cosmology. Introduction to black holes, large-scale structure of the universe.
difficulty_reasoning: Assumes mastery of tensor calculus and special relativity from PHY483; applications require heavy mathematical maturity and sustained problem-solving.

name: Laser Physics
description: This course covers a broad range of advanced topics in classical optics, with the laser as a unifying theme. Topics include atom-photon interactions (absorption, radiation, and stimulated emission), how a laser works (gain, pumping, rate equation models, threshold, and gain clamping), optical resonators (their spectrum, finesse, stability, and transverse modes), propagation of Gaussian beams and paraxial rays, and the statistics of optical fields (spatial and temporal coherence). Time permitting, pulse propagation and pulsed lasers will be discussed.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced quantum and classical optics concepts require strong math foundation and sustained theoretical engagement throughout semester.

name: Condensed Matter Physics
description: Introduction to foundational concepts of condensed matter physics in the solid state. Main topics to be covered: crystal structure, reciprocal lattice, x-ray diffraction, crystal binding, lattice vibrations, phonons and electrons in solids, Fermi surfaces, energy bands, semiconductors and magnetism. Special topics to be surveyed: superconductivity and nanoelectronic transport.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced quantum concepts and mathematical formalism demand strong prior physics foundation. Weekly problem sets are computational and conceptually demanding.

name: Introduction to High Energy Physics
description: This course introduces the basics of fundamental particles and the strong, weak and electromagnetic forces that govern their interactions in the Standard Model of particle physics. Topics include relativistic kinematics, conservation laws, particle decays and scattering processes, the Higgs boson and electroweak symmetry breaking, and prospects for Physics beyond the Standard Model.
difficulty_reasoning: Relativistic mechanics and quantum field theory fundamentals require sustained mathematical maturity beyond typical first-year foundation.

name: Current Interpretations of Quantum Mechanics
description: Different interpretations of quantum mechanics are presented and discussed, comparing and contrasting the various approaches to understanding the formalism of the theory. We begin with “textbook quantum mechanics” and then discuss the Copenhagen view, operationalist quantum mechanics, hidden variable theories, Bohm-de Broglie theory, consistent histories, relational quantum mechanics, relative state approaches (many minds and many worlds), QBism, the interactional interpretation, and collapse theories.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires mastery of quantum formalism from PHY456 plus engagement with abstract philosophical frameworks simultaneously.

name: Advanced Atmospheric Physics
description: A preparatory course for research in experimental and theoretical atmospheric physics. Content will vary from year to year. Themes may include techniques for remote sensing of the Earth's atmosphere and surface; theoretical atmosphere-ocean dynamics; the physics of clouds, precipitation, and convection in the Earth's atmosphere.
prerequisites: PHY224H1, PHY250H1/ PHY252H1/ PHY254H1, (MAT235H1, MAT236H1)/ MAT235Y1/ MAT237Y1/ MAT257Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Specialized upper-year physics requiring strong multivariable calculus and prior physics courses; research-level atmospheric content demands sustained conceptual depth.

name: The Real World of Politics: An Introduction
description: This course introduces students to compelling issues of contemporary politics through the lens of classic and important texts in political science. The course covers the politics of climate change, Indigenous rights, elections and electoral systems, terrorism, social movements and political activism, voting, democracy, and power.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory politics survey with no prerequisites; weekly readings and essays are standard undergraduate work, manageable with consistent effort.

name: Contemporary Challenges to Democracy: Democracy in the Social Media Age
description: This course examines the latest evidence-based research on the effects of social media on democracy. We cover surveillance capitalism, privacy, disinformation, and the often-overlooked ecological implications of data consumption. We also examine targeted digital espionage against civil society, Citizen Lab research reports, and explore solutions and alternatives to social media.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites needed; contemporary topics are accessible but require sustained engagement with research papers and critical analysis.

name: What Went Wrong? A Post Mortem of Political Disasters, Catastrophic Policy Failures, and Epic Marches of Folly
description: Why do individuals, groups, and societies make repeated, and often easily predictable mistakes? Why do they persist in courses of action that produce disastrous results? Why is it that in the political world good intentions are insufficient to ensure good results? This course employs the political science analytical toolkit to answer these questions and examines an array avoidable disasters, from the local to the international.
exclusions: POL486H1 (Topics in International Politics I: What Went Wrong? A Post Mortem of Political Disasters, Policy Failures and Marches of Folly), offered in Winter 2019 and Winter 2020; POL487H1 (Topics in International Politics II: What Went Wrong? A Post Mortem of Political Disasters, Catastrophic Policy Failures), offered in Winter 2018
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; case-study format is intuitive; moderate weekly readings and occasional analytical essays required.

name: Might and Right:  Power and Justice in International Relations
description: This course explores the relationship between justice, power, and interests in the works of prominent ancient, modern, and contemporary thinkers. It adopts a problem-driven approach and engages with current controversies in international relations in light of insights from classic books. Special attention is paid to the prospects of a just world order, the causes and justifications of war, the construction of images of citizens and enemies, and the nature of duties to outsiders.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites required; classic texts are accessible but demand sustained engagement and critical thinking throughout the semester.

name: First-Year Foundation Seminar: Political Homes
description: Homes are core to our identities, shaping our beliefs, our social networks, and how we interact with the world. In turn, politics determines who has a home and who is homeless, whose home increases in value and whose mortgage gets foreclosed. Through careful consideration of evidence, we examine these processes. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: First-year seminar with accessible topic; no specialist background needed. Weekly readings and occasional reflective writing.

name: First-Year Foundation Seminar: Beyond Voting: Introduction to Citizen Political Participation
description: This course introduces students to citizen political participation in democracies, with a focus on Canadian case studies. It examines theories of political participation and introduces students to a range of methods by which citizens engage in political life including social movements, political party membership, deliberative mini-publics and more. This course examines debates about the appropriate role for citizens in governance and considers barriers to equity in political participation in Canada. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: First-year seminar with no prerequisites; weekly readings and discussion-based format require modest engagement, not specialized background.

name: First-Year Foundation Seminar: Politics and the Arts
description: A study of political ideas as found in literature, plays, art works and film. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: First-year seminar with no prerequisites; engaging texts require close reading but no specialist background needed.

name: First-Year Foundation Seminar: The China Challenge
description: China’s meteoric rise to great power status has triggered an intense international debate over its global implications. While many analysts see Beijing’s rise as posing a threat to global political and economic stability, Chinese leaders have argued forcefully that China’s efforts to regain its historic preeminence will result in a ‘win-win” outcome for all states. This course will assess the merits of these contending positions through an historical examination of China’s 20th century renaissance. The course will begin by tracing the long period of imperial decline in the 19th century, culminating in China’s revolutionary rebirth as a Marxist state in 1949. A major focus will be on the Mao-era legacy of revolutionary diplomacy and the foreign policy consequences of its later transformation into a market-authoritarian powerhouse. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: First-year seminar assumes no prior knowledge; historical narrative and geopolitical debate are accessible with consistent reading engagement.

name: Political Theory: Visions of the Just/Good Society
description: A selective presentation of critical encounters between philosophy and politics, dedicated to the quest for articulation and founding of the just/good society. Among the theorists examined are Plato, Aristotle, Machiavelli, Hobbes and Locke.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical texts require close reading and engagement, but no prior background expected; manageable with consistent effort.

name: Politics of Development
description: This course offers an introduction to the history and politics of economic and political development, starting with the Industrial Revolution and then turning to a critical analysis of the politics of economic growth, international trade, debt, state intervention, protectionism, and neo-liberalism in the global periphery, including Africa, Asia, and Latin America.
prerequisites: 4.0 credits, or 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory politics course with accessible historical narrative and contemporary case studies, manageable reading load for engaged first-year student.

name: Introduction to International Relations
description: This introductory course examines some key themes and issues in global politics, including interstate war, human rights, international institutions, and the evolution of the global order.
prerequisites: 4.0 credits, or 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory survey with no prerequisites assumes no prior knowledge; weekly readings and periodic essays are standard for first-year humanities.

name: Understanding War
description: General introduction to the study of war, covering basic concepts and theories and surveying a selection of key topics and debates. Sessions revolve around a few essential readings, which must be completed before class and will serve as a basis for various in-class and in-tutorial activities including presentations, case studies, simulations, and games.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Essential readings dominate each week, but concepts are accessible to first-year students without prior political theory background.

name: Canadian Government
description: An introduction to the study of Canadian government. Topics include institutions of governance: the constitution, machinery of government, charter of rights and freedom, and the electoral system.
prerequisites: 4.0 credits, or 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory political science requiring only civic literacy; regular readings and essays are manageable with consistent effort.

name: State, Society and Power in Comparative Perspective
description: This course is designed to introduce students to major issues and challenges that shape states, determine how they are governed, and how they change. The course helps to explain major events such as state transformation, democratization, authoritarian rule, civil conflict and social mobilization.
prerequisites: 4.0 credits, or 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory comparative politics course requiring regular readings and analytical thinking but no prior expertise assumed.

name: Immigration, Multiculturalism, and Citizenship in Canada
description: This course deals with three inter-related themes – immigration, multiculturalism, and citizenship – by focusing on a single unifying question: What does it mean to “belong” to the Canadian political community? Who belongs, on what terms, and to what ends? A range of materials – normative, empirical, historical, and contemporary – will be used.
prerequisites: 4.0 credits, or 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Politics course accessible to any student curious about Canada; weekly readings and essays manageable with consistent effort.

name: Introduction to Quantitative Reasoning I (formerly POL 322H1)
description: Introduces the foundation of quantitative empirical research methods - increasingly popular and important part of political science research and public policy debates - to enable students to interpret and evaluate the study results that employ these methods. Topics include quantitative study of politics and various empirical research designs.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory quantitative methods with accessible math, but requires sustained engagement with unfamiliar research design concepts.

name: Globalization and Development: Issues and Challenges
description: This course examines how globalization creates opportunities and challenges to development in the Global South. Key issues considered include globalization and dynamics of inequality amongst and within nations, human rights and democratic struggles, environmental sustainability and justice, gender and racialized patterns of inequality, trade, foreign aid and poverty alleviation.
prerequisites: 4.0 credits, or 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Accessible conceptual survey requiring sustained reading and essay writing but no specialized prior knowledge or technical skills.

name: Canada in Comparative Perspective
description: This course introduces students to aspects of Canadian political life by comparing them with those that prevail in other advanced democracies. Themes covered will include the Canadian constitution, federalism, parties and elections, political culture and social and economic institutions and policies.
prerequisites: 4.0 credits, or 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory comparative politics course requiring regular readings and essay writing but no specialist background assumed.

name: Introduction to Quantitative Reasoning II
description: Building up on POL222H1, students will continue to develop theoretical foundations of quantitative empirical research, such as probability theory, statistical inference, and linear regression analysis. They will also learn the basic use of statistical software and become able to conduct basic data analysis by the end of the semester.
difficulty_reasoning: Probability and regression demand sustained math engagement; software labs add weekly implementation pressure for non-specialists.

name: Colonial Legacies and Post-Independence African Politics
description: This course highlights the critical roles of pre-colonial and colonial histories in shaping contemporary political and economic developments in Africa. It covers the emergence of colonial states, the central legacies of colonial rule, and the impact of colonialism in shaping process of state and nation building from independence to the present.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Requires sustained engagement with unfamiliar historical and political concepts, but lectures and readings are narratively accessible without specialist background.

name: Politics and Society in 20th Century China
description: This course examines China’s efforts to reconstitute its political institutions following the collapse of the imperial system in 1911. Particular attention is paid to the clash between competing conceptions of political development and the rival political movements and forces that espoused them. The search for an effective and stable political order is a major focus of the course.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; history-focused with accessible primary sources; standard 300-level reading and essay load.

name: Women in Western Political Thought
description: Examines contemporary feminist perspectives in political theory as responses to the limitations of western tradition of modern political theory.
difficulty_reasoning: Reading-heavy theory course builds on prerequisite foundations; essays and seminars demand sustained engagement but manageable for prepared students.

name: Introduction to Latin American Politics and Societies
description: This course introduces students to histories and concepts necessary to understand developments in Latin American politics. Substantive issues will include the changing face of state sovereignty; human rights and social movements; the legacies of (neo)colonialism and indigenous resistance; neoliberalism, 21st century socialism, and beyond.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory political science course requiring sustained reading and essay writing but no specialist prerequisites or technical skills.

name: Contemporary African Politics: Dynamics and Challenges
description: This course explores main drivers of political organization and change in contemporary Africa, focusing on how national, regional, and international factors shape institutions, patterns of participation and political change. It considers major scholarly debates in the study of African politics and political economy and develops analytical skills for comparative study of this diverse continent.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Assumes prior POL foundation; requires sustained reading and essay writing but no specialized technical or theoretical background.

name: Canadian Foreign Policy
description: The literature, competing theories, basic interests and values, and instruments of Canadian foreign policy, as they have developed and performed under successive Liberal, Progressive Conservative and Conservative party governments since 1945 and especially from 2015 to 2021.
recommended_preparation: Courses in International Relations and/or Canadian government would be useful.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level politics course requiring sustained reading and essay writing, but no specialized technical skills or advanced prerequisites beyond POL208/209.

name: Public Opinion and Voting
description: This course introduces students to the attitudes and behaviours of the mass public in Canada and other western democracies and how they shape elections and policy making. Themes include political participation, the mass media, and the nature of social, psychological, and economic, and elite-driven forces on public opinion and voting.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires POL222 foundation but uses familiar real-world examples; weekly readings and essays demand consistent engagement without specialist expertise.

name: American Civil Liberties
description: This course offers a survey of American constitutional law in the area of civil liberties. Topics to be covered include: fundamental rights; freedoms of speech, press, and assembly; religious liberties; rights to privacy and autonomy; and due process and equal protection of the laws; sexual and familial rights.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
exclusions: POL379H1 (Civil Liberties in the United States), offered in Fall 2022, 2023, and 2024.
difficulty_reasoning: Survey course with accessible legal concepts but requires sustained reading and essay writing on unfamiliar constitutional topics.

name: Contemporary Canadian Federalism
description: Constitutional, political, administrative, and financial aspects of federal-provincial relations, regionalism, and cultural dualism.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-year political science course requiring solid grasp of Canadian institutions and policy; consistent reading and essay writing throughout semester.

name: Elections and Voting
description: This course aims to introduce students to the essential elements for understanding the electoral process. The course will first present an introduction to the origin and objectives of elections. It will then address electoral procedures and their impact on electoral participation, party systems and the determinants of vote choice. While the overwhelming focus will be on the Canadian case, the course often draws upon examples and readings from other countries/institutional contexts to highlight important differences and similarities.
prerequisites: POL214H1/ POL214Y5/ POL215H5/ POL216H5/ POL224H1/ POLB50Y3
exclusions: POL382H1 (Elections), offered in Fall 2023, and (Elections and Voting), offered in Winter 2025
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory political science concepts with Canadian focus; regular readings and essays manageable with consistent effort.

name: Modern Political Thought: Freedom and Equality
description: An exploration of ideas of freedom in the rise of the modern age from the 18th and 19th centuries, from the Age of Enlightenment to the Age of Democratic Revolutions. Thinkers studied include Rousseau, Burke, and Wollstonecraft.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical texts require sustained close reading, but course assumes no prior philosophy background and provides foundational scaffolding.

name: Modern Political Thought: Progress Through History
description: An exploration of modern political thought from the 19th to the beginning of the 20th centuries. Themes include the idea of progress through history and its implications for politics, as explored by thinkers such as J.S. Mill, Marx, Hegel, and Nietzsche.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense primary texts and abstract political philosophy require sustained engagement, but no specialized background assumed beyond prerequisite foundation.

name: European Union: Politics, Institutions and Society
description: This course provides an in-depth understanding of the history, institutions, laws, and policies of the European Union (EU). We will debate alternative explanations for the political development of the EU, unearth the events and actors that have shaped European integration, and place the EU in a broader comparative perspective. We will also grapple with key social and political debates facing the EU today, such as debates concerning the democratic legitimacy of the EU, immigration and the EU’s handling of the refugee crisis, the rise of Euroskepticism and Brexit, the rule of law crisis and autocratization of some EU member states, and how the war in Ukraine has impacted the trajectory of European integration.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses or EUR200Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Policy course requires sustained reading and writing but no technical skills. Builds naturally on first-year political science foundation.

name: Contemporary Latin American Politics
description: This course focuses on 21st century Latin American Politics. Specific attention will be paid to the ‘Pink Tide’ of left of centre governments and their aftermaths; enduring legacies of resource extraction driven development models, and Latin America’s position in shifting geopolitical contexts.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Regional politics course with accessible contemporary focus, manageable reading load, and standard 300-level essay structure.

name: The Politics of U.S. Foreign Policy
description: This course explores the making of foreign policy in the U.S. through a detailed examination of the institutions of the U.S. government involved, as well as the forces acting upon them to shape policy. After exploring theoretical approaches to the subject, it examines the evolution of the constitutional context within which U.S. foreign policy has been articulated, the bureaucracies involved in shaping policy, the impact of elections, groups and the mass media.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires POL prerequisite foundation; reading-heavy but no technical skills; typical 300-level political science course.

name: U.S. Foreign Policy in a Complex World
description: This course explores the foreign policy of the U.S. through a series of regional and thematic case studies. It begins with a historical review of U.S. foreign policy in the evolution of the U.S. as a major global power, prior to WWII. Among the case studies of U.S. foreign policy included are international organization and law, terrorism, environment (climate), Latin America, Europe, Middle East, China/East Asia, Africa and Russia.
difficulty_reasoning: Survey-style case study course requires regional knowledge and synthesis but no specialized background. Manageable reading load.

name: Politics and Government in South Asia
description: This course introduces students to politics in South Asia in the period after independence from colonial rule. It focuses on India, Pakistan, Sri Lanka and Bangladesh. The themes discussed in the course are important both to South Asia as well as to a general study of politics in developing countries.
difficulty_reasoning: Regional politics course with no specialist prerequisites assumes general literacy in politics and history, manageable with consistent engagement.

name: Experiences of Conflict
description: The course reviews selected novels that deal with personal and collective experiences of conflict. It focuses on representations of how conflict is experienced. It gives students a practical understanding of the human dimension of selected major conflicts and explores possibilities for personal and social resistance to injustice and violence. Special attention is paid to questions of identity formation and moral choice in contexts of war and nationalism.
difficulty_reasoning: Novel-based humanities course requires sustained close reading and essay writing but assumes no prior political theory background.

name: The Rise of Global China
description: This course will take students through important domestic institutions and events in China that shape its current political landscape. Students will be prompted to think about the implications of domestic political factors for China’s rise as a global power. The course examines major China’s adventurism abroad, such as the Belt-and-Road Initiative, and what increasing footprints of Chinese state and private firms overseas mean for international politics.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Requires regional knowledge and policy literacy but no specialized background. Weekly readings and essays are standard 300-level political science work.

name: Using Data to Understand Politics and Society
description: Students will learn the research tools and methods for causal inference with political and social data, including panel and temporal design, difference-in-difference, linear regression, regression discontinuity design, as well as text and network analysis, spatial statistics, and time-series analysis. This course draws from topics in the study of international relations, economics, political behavior, and statistics.
exclusions: POL304H1 (Using Data to Understand Politics and Society), offered in Fall 2021, 2022, and Winter 2021, 2024, ECO372H1, STA302H1
difficulty_reasoning: Heavy quantitative and methodological content spanning regression, causal inference, and specialized statistical techniques requires sustained technical engagement.

name: Global Politics and Global Political Thought
