description: This course will examine three pressing contemporary topics (examples include: environmental justice and the natural world; race and identity politics; and international and cosmopolitan visions) through the theoretical and analytical lenses provided by the political thought of non-Western cultural traditions and from particular marginalized identity perspectives.
exclusions: POL381H1 (Topics in Political Theory: Global Politics and Global Political Thought) taken in Winter 2020 (LEC0101), Winter 2021 (LEC0101), Winter 2022 (LEC0101), Winter 2023 (LEC0201)
difficulty_reasoning: Theory-heavy readings from unfamiliar non-Western traditions require sustained engagement; contemporary topics provide accessible entry points.

name: The Canadian Constitution
description: The moral foundations, historical events, political forces and legal ideas that have shaped the Canadian constitution; the roots, legacies, and judicial interpretation of the Constitution Act 1867, the Constitution Act 1982, and in particular the Charter of Rights and Freedoms; the constitutional framework of federalism; the politics of constitutional change; multiculturalism, 'rights talk', and the judicialization of politics.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires sustained engagement with legal and historical texts; no math or coding, but conceptual density and prerequisite gatekeeping assume some political literacy.

name: International Law: Foundations
description: This course will introduce students to the primary sources of international law (treaties and customary international law) and the legal attributes of the core actors in the international system, including states, international organizations and individuals. Related topics will include governance of territory and the seas.
difficulty_reasoning: Core legal concepts are new but self-contained; regular readings and essays require sustained engagement without requiring specialist background.

name: International Law: Operation of the International Legal Order
description: This course will expose students to the operation of international legal order with respect to the use of armed force, the law of armed conflict, and the protection of human rights. Students will also be introduced to how dispute settlement works between states.
recommended_preparation: It is highly recommended that students take POL340H1 (International Law: Foundations) before taking this course.
difficulty_reasoning: Legal concepts and case studies are accessible but require sustained reading comprehension and careful analytical thinking throughout term.

name: Social Movements in Canada
description: This course introduces the major theoretical debates in the study of social movements through a series of Canadian case studies. Students will consider the role of protest in agenda setting, how power shapes actors’ protest tactics, and the relationship between protest and other forms of political engagement.
difficulty_reasoning: Prerequisite ensures foundational political science knowledge; case-study format and theoretical frameworks are accessible with consistent engagement.

name: U.S. Government and Politics: Constitutional Structure and Development
description: This course examines the constitutional foundations of American politics—the separation of powers, federalism, and rights. Major themes include the historical origins of the American constitution, the transformation of American constitutionalism from the Civil War to the New Deal, and the struggle over the meaning of American constitutionalism in the 21st century.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
recommended_preparation: POL214H1/POL224H1/POL200Y1 and/or a course in U.S. history
difficulty_reasoning: Historical and conceptual material is manageable with consistent reading; constitutional law requires careful attention but no math or technical skills.

name: Politics of East Central Europe
description: This course examines political change in East Central Europe from the Middle Ages to the present. How are democracies created and why do they collapse? Did Eastern and Western Europe diverge politically centuries ago, or is the idea of a longstanding east-west divide largely an artefact of Cold War geopolitics?
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA
exclusions: POL377H1 (Topic: The Making of Modern Democracy: From the Middle Ages to the European Union) offered in Fall 2022 and 2023.
recommended_preparation: Some prior knowledge of the broad outlines of European history.
difficulty_reasoning: Historical content requires steady reading and synthesis; no technical prerequisites, but depth of regional context demands genuine engagement.

name: Introduction to Qualitative Research Methods
description: This course introduces students to qualitative research methods in political science (e.g., interviews, participant observation, case studies). It provides opportunities to acquire hands-on experience with these methods and prepares students to carry out their own research projects. Note: POL352H1 cannot be used as a substitute for POL222H1 for POL major/specialist program, or POL232H1 for the POL specialist program.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Hands-on methods course builds on prior POL knowledge. Practical assignments and research projects demand sustained engagement but remain introductory to qualitative work.

name: Canadian Political Parties and Elections
description: The evolution and setting of Canada's federal party system and Canadian elections. Topics include historical and theoretical perspectives, the Liberal, Conservative and New Democratic parties, third parties, leadership selection and local nominations, the representation of women and minorities in Parliament, electoral systems and election rules, campaigns, and voter behaviour.
prerequisites: POL214H1/ POL214Y1/ POL215H5/ POL216H5/ POL224H1/ POL224Y1/ POLB50Y3
difficulty_reasoning: Content is accessible to first-time learners; prerequisites ensure foundational political knowledge; consistent reading and essay assignments create steady workload.

name: Topics in South Asian Politics
description: Selected issues in South Asian politics. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level politics course requiring sustained engagement with unfamiliar regional context and analytical frameworks; standard essay and reading load.

name: Conflicts, Minority Rights and Para-States in Europe
description: This course examines a number of unresolved issues in Europe that are largely shaped by real and perceived shortcomings in minority rights. After a section on Roma Rights in Central Europe, our focus turns to the origins and outcomes of largely separatist wars in Azerbaijan, Cyprus, Georgia, Moldova and Ukraine and the peace agreements that followed.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
recommended_preparation: Students will be expected to be completely familiar with the historical context of the region we study.
difficulty_reasoning: Regional conflict case studies are accessible without deep background; weekly readings and essays demand consistent engagement but are manageable.

name: Enlarging Europe: The European Union and Its Applicants
description: European integration remains one of the most important and successful political experiments in recent history. This course looks at the historical impetus for European integration after the Second World War and to the recent trends in this process, as well as its future prospects as the Union’s borders enlarge and it confronts new challenges.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
recommended_preparation: Interest in European History and Politics.
difficulty_reasoning: Historical and political survey requiring regular reading and analytical essays, no specialist background assumed or needed.

name: Topics in Latin American Politics
description: Selected issues in Latin American politics. Content and instructor vary from year to year.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level politics course with no specialist background assumed, moderate reading and essay-based assessment typical of 300-level humanities.

name: Global Political Economy
description: This course focuses on key aspects and ideas of world economic order, such as those underlying policies governing trade, capital flows, migration, and development. It also provides an orientation to understanding and explaining underlying political dynamics and their implications.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses; (ECO101H1, ECO102H1)/ ECO105Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Requires economics foundation and conceptual political analysis but no advanced math. Moderate reading and essay workload.

name: Topics in Comparative Politics I
description: An introduction to the field of comparative politics. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory comparative politics course requiring regular readings and critical thinking, but no specialist background assumed or needed.

name: Topics in Comparative Politics II
description: An introduction to the field of comparative politics. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level politics course requiring sustained engagement with unfamiliar comparative frameworks, but content-driven rather than technical.

name: Topics in Comparative Politics III
description: An introduction to the field of comparative politics. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-year intro to comparative politics builds on first-year foundations with accessible case studies and moderate weekly readings.

name: Topics in International Politics
description: Content in any given year depends on instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level politics course demands prior POL foundation and sustained engagement with complex international topics, but remains accessible with consistent effort.

name: Topics in Political Theory
description: A detailed examination of particular authors or topics in political theory. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level theory course demands close reading and interpretation skills but builds directly on foundational POL200 prerequisite.

name: Topics in Canadian Politics
description: Examines in depth enduring and emerging issues in Canadian politics. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level politics course assumes prior POL foundation; demands sustained reading, essay writing, and critical analysis of Canadian political issues.

name: Global Environmental Governance from the Ground Up
description: This course focuses on non-state actors in global environmental governance, considering the motivations, actions, and strategies of non-governmental organizations, grassroots communities, and corporations. The course uses analytic tools from international relations and comparative politics to understand patterns of environmental protest, resistance, and change over time.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses at the 200+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on introductory political science foundation; case-study approach and IR concepts are accessible with consistent engagement.

name: Issues in Contemporary Greece
description: This course is designed to comprehensively explore the theoretical, conceptual and empirical dimensions through the political history of the Greek state from the 19th c. to the present, and, to provide students with the critical skills to follow, understand and systematically analyze contemporary Greek politics. The class will alternate between highlights of Greek political history, theoretical foundations of major themes in Comparative Politics, and their empirical application to the politics of the Modern Greek state.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Historical and political content is accessible without background; regular readings and essays manageable with consistent engagement.

name: U.S. Government and Politics
description: Who rules the United State of America? This course will investigate this question by examining how power is attained and how power is exercised in American elections, the legislative process, the bureaucracy, and the federal courts. Particular attention will be paid to the role of national interest groups, regional economic interests, and new modes of political mobilization.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
recommended_preparation: POL214H1/ POL224H1/ POL200Y1 and/or a course in U.S. history
difficulty_reasoning: Politics content is conceptually accessible; prerequisite ensures foundational knowledge; typical 300-level essay and reading demands.

name: Politics in Europe
description: This course applies the basic concepts in comparative politics to the political systems of Europe. We will cover theories of transitions to democracy, formation and development of the nation-state, political institutions and their effects, parties and party systems and elections and electoral behaviour. We will use these theories to gain a better understanding of politics in Europe. We will also address some of the major challenges that Europe and the EU have recently faced such as the eurozone crisis, Brexit, the rise of populism and extreme right parties and the challenges of immigration and incorporation of minorities. The goal is for students to become familiar with the politics and governments of contemporary Europe through the lens of current and classic themes in comparative politics.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses, or EUR200Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Comparative politics framework is accessible to first-year students; European context requires reading but no specialist background assumed.

name: Politics and Government of Southeast Asia
description: This course provides an overview of political regimes in Southeast Asia, as well as some of the main issues that shape its political life. It includes legacies of colonial rule, nationalist struggles, democratization, ethnic and secessionist conflict, as well as social movement.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Regional overview course with accessible concepts; weekly readings and essays manageable with consistent engagement.

name: Research Participation
description: Credit course for supervised participation in a faculty research project. Offered only when a faculty member is willing and available to supervise. Interested faculty review plans with the Undergraduate Director, and then make the opportunity known to students as appropriate. Check with Undergraduate Office for more details and faculty proposal form. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Available to students in their third year of study (who have completed at least 9.0 credits)
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on the specific research project supervisor and field; workload scales with project scope and mentor expectations.

name: Research Participation
description: Credit course for supervised participation in a faculty research project. Offered only when a faculty member is willing and available to supervise. Interested faculty review plans with the Undergraduate Director, and then make the opportunity known to students as appropriate. Check with Undergraduate Office for more details and faculty proposal form. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Available to students in their third year of study (who have completed at least 9.0 credits)
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on faculty project scope; workload scales with research demands and supervision intensity.

name: The Military Instrument of Foreign Policy: Concepts and Approaches
description: In light of endemic international threats and conflicts, the seminar analyses the use of the military instrument of foreign policy. We meld theoretical and pragmatic approaches. Among the subjects covered are civil-military relations, the development of nuclear weapons, deterrence and nuclear deterrence, arms control and war termination strategies.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar demanding mature theoretical grasp of security studies and policy analysis without assumed background.

name: Topics in Comparative Politics III
description: Selected issues in comparative politics. Vary from year to year.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar with substantial reading and essay writing demands familiarity with political analysis but no exotic prerequisites.

name: Human Rights and International Relations
description: Human rights have become dominant in international politics since the end of World War II. The process of creating and implementing human rights is political. We explore historical, philosophical, and empirical explanations of the roots, effects, and implications of human rights today through a variety of topics.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level political science requires strong reading comprehension and essay writing, but assumes no specialist background beyond prerequisite courses.

name: Experiments in Political Science: Foundation
description: Experiments are a central methodology in political science. Scholars from every subfield regularly turn to experiments. Practitioners rely on experimental evidence in evaluating social programs, policies, institutions, and information provision. The design, implementation, and analysis of experiments raise a variety of distinct epistemological and methodological challenges. This is particularly true in political science due to the breadth of the discipline, the varying contexts in which experiments are implemented (e.g., laboratory, survey, field), and the distinct methods employed (e.g., psychological or economic approaches to experimentation). This seminar will review the challenges to experimentation, discuss how to implement experiments, and survey prominent applications.
exclusions: POL440H1 (Topic: Experimental Thinking: Foundation) offered in Fall 2025, POL478H1 (Topic: Experiments in Political Science) offered in Winter 2025
difficulty_reasoning: Methodological foundations require careful engagement with research design and statistics, but seminar format and prerequisite POL232H1 provide sufficient scaffolding.

name: Politics of North-South Relations
description: This course explores the complex relations between the developed world and Global South in historical and contemporary settings. It engages critical scholarship within International Politics and International Political Economy to examine salient factors in North-South relations such as dependency and interdependence, trade, development aid, global governance architecture, and South-South cooperation.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level political science requires sustained engagement with complex theories and contemporary case studies but remains accessible with consistent effort.

name: Human Security and Intra-state Conflicts in the Global South
description: What are the underlying causes of insecurity and instability, and what factors support or undermine attainment of durable peace after episodes of violent conflict in the Global South? This course explores these questions by focusing on identity-based conflicts and through comparative case studies and theoretical perspectives from political science and related disciplines.
prerequisites: POL201H1/ POL201Y1/ POL208H1/ POL208Y1/ POL209H5/ POLB80H3/ POLB90H3
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level politics course requiring sustained engagement with theory and case studies, but no specialized background assumed beyond prerequisites.

name: Globalization, Democracy and Growth
description: This course introduces students to recent work in the rational choice institutionalist tradition in comparative political economy. It focuses on the link between institutions and economic policy, starting with an examination of the effect on economic outcomes of specific democratic institutions, such as the separation of powers or delegation to unelected officials like central bankers. In so doing it also asks whether globalization has altered the relationship between democratic institutions and economic policy choices. Most of the readings rely on either formal models or quantitative empirics; students will therefore also acquire proficiency in the interpretation of such work.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
exclusions: POL443H1 (Globalization, Democracy and Growth), offered in Winter 2017, 2018, 2019, 2024, and 2025
difficulty_reasoning: Formal models and quantitative empirics require sustained analytical effort unfamiliar to undeclared students without economics or statistics background.

name: Maimonides and His Modern Interpreters
description: The course offers an introduction to the seminal work of Jewish philosophy, 'The Guide of the Perplexed' by Moses Maimonides. We will delve into some of the basic themes of Jewish philosophical theology and religion as they are treated by Maimonides.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense philosophical text requires sustained engagement with unfamiliar concepts; no prior background assumed but considerable intellectual maturity needed.

name: State and Development in Historical Perspective
description: What is the state’s role in economic development? What caused the Industrial Revolution, and why was Britain at its forefront? This course examines a variety of competing, and complementary, explanations for historical (and present-day) variation in patterns of economic development, focusing on the state’s role in each.
exclusions: POL443H1 (Topic: State and Development in Historical Perspective) offered in Winter 2023 and 2024.
recommended_preparation: Some preparation in qualitative or quantitative research methods (or both).
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level political economy requires synthesizing historical and theoretical material; manageable with strong reading discipline and prior POL coursework.

name: Democracy and Dictatorship
description: The course provides an in-depth introduction to theories of the origins of democracy and dictatorship. In the first part of the course, we examine and compare theories rooted in economic development, voluntarism, institutional design, and historical institutionalism. The latter half of the course applies these different approaches to debates over the origins of Nazi rule in Germany in the 1930s, military dictatorship in Chile in the 1970s, and non-democratic rule in contemporary Russia.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level theory requires sustained engagement with unfamiliar frameworks, but case studies provide concrete anchors for understanding.

name: American Political Development
description: This course explores both the substance and the theory of APD, using the second as a lens through which the first can be analyzed and interpreted. We begin by examining the methodologies, mechanisms, and patterns of APD, before turning to synoptic accounts of political development and then working through broad eras of political development. Throughout we will focus on questions of race, democracy, and inclusion/exclusion that both animate the American past and structure its present.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
exclusions: POL492H1 (American Political Development), offered in Winter 2022, 2023, 2024, and 2025.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level political science requires sustained analytical reading and essay writing, but no specialized prerequisite knowledge beyond intro POL courses.

name: Federalism and Diversity in Canada (and Beyond)
description: Canada as a key case in comparative federalism studies, with a particular focus on the management of diversity and conflict. Federal theory is applied to analyze federal institutions and dynamics in Canada (and other cases). Topics include the distribution of power, the judiciary’s role and group representation.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level political science requires sustained engagement with theory and case analysis, but prerequisites ensure adequate foundation for an average student.

name: Dynamics of Political Change in Contemporary China
description: This course will explore processes of political change in post-Mao China through an examination of selected systemic challenges and regime responses. The primary focus will be on processes of institutional development and decay as the Party-State regime evolves from hard to soft authoritarianism.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites assumed; conceptual engagement with institutional change and authoritarianism is accessible but requires sustained analytical effort.

name: Business and Politics: Power in a Global World
description: This seminar course examines the political power of business from an international and comparative perspective. Topics include the role of public authority in governing business behavior, the formation of business interests, corporate lobbying, structural and ideational business power, corporate social responsibility, and transnational private governance.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requires strong reading comprehension and critical analysis of unfamiliar political economy concepts, but no specialized prerequisites beyond stated POL courses.

name: Topics in Comparative Politics I
description: Selected issues in comparative politics. Vary from year to year.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requiring solid prior political science foundation and sustained analytical reading, but not research-intensive.

name: Topics in Comparative Politics II
description: Selected issues in comparative politics. Vary from year to year.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requiring sustained engagement with diverse comparative cases and analytical writing, but content-driven rather than technical.

name: Political Economy of Development (formerly POL447Y1)
description: The course explores the rise, evolution, and performance of the dominant neoliberal approach to development and poverty reduction. It also assesses the feasibility and efficacy of alternative development strategies. Case studies are drawn from Latin America, Asia, and Africa.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level political science with substantial reading and conceptual density, but no technical prerequisites or math required beyond typical undergraduate ability.

name: Topics in International Politics III
description: For advanced students of international relations. Various topics are taken up each year, the content of which depends on the instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced upper-year seminar requiring strong prior IR foundation, specialized reading, and substantive analytical writing on complex topics.

name: Ethics and International Relations
description: The course aims to explore the requirements of justice and fairness in international affairs. It is common to theorize international relations in terms of interests and power. But even the most cursory look at what important actors actually do in their international interactions reveals that they use normative language all the time. This has not gone unnoticed, with investigations of ethics in the international arena multiplying in recent years. Drawing on readings from political philosophy, legal theory, and normative international relations theory, the course will take up practical ethical dilemmas encountered in world affairs. The main focus of the course will be on institutions. Examples will be drawn from the issue areas of trade, health, and the environment, among others.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level philosophy and theory course demanding sustained critical engagement with abstract normative concepts and primary texts.

name: Politics and Policy Analysis
description: Major theories of public policy-making and related approaches to policy analysis are examined from the perspective of political science. Key contributions to the theoretical literature pertaining to leading models are read and discussed. Models of public policy-making are successively applied to analysis of cases of Canadian and comparative policy development.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-year policy theory requires sustained engagement with dense academic literature and comparative analysis skills.

name: Postmodern and Contemporary Thought
description: Study of postmodern and contemporary themes. Beginning with political economy, then the effect of technology on politics, a discussion of Western colonialism leading to a revised concept of social relations. Social contract theory will be analyzed through a natural contract as well as examination of individualism and the posthuman.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense theoretical reading and unfamiliar philosophical frameworks demand sustained engagement without prior disciplinary foundation.

name: Topics in Public Policy
description: The course is designed for advanced students with serious interests in the public policy field. Specific topics covered will vary from year to year.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced upper-year course requiring strong policy foundation and independent research; prerequisites are gatekeeping indicators.

name: Topics in Middle East Politics
description: This class undertakes an in-depth examination of various themes relating to politics and society in the Middle East and North Africa. Past topics include "Contesting Authoritarianisms in the Middle East" and Protest Politics in the Middle East."
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level politics course demanding sustained engagement with complex regional themes and analytical writing, but no technical prerequisites.

name: Data Visualization for Political Science
description: This course provides the fundamental computational tools for managing, analyzing, and presenting political data in R. The material will enable students to become proficient enough to actively implement the methods and tools in their own research and professional lives.
exclusions: POL 478H1 (Data Management and Visualization for Political Scientists), offered in Fall 2022 and 2023, and Winter 2025
difficulty_reasoning: First coding exposure with unfamiliar syntax demands sustained practice; weekly assignments and projects typical of 300-level courses.

name: Topics in Political Thought I
description: A seminar on a central problem in political thought. It proceeds through the reading of a small number of major texts. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar requiring close reading of dense philosophical texts and sustained critical engagement unfamiliar to most undeclared students.

name: Topics in Political Thought II
description: A seminar on a central problem in political thought. It proceeds through the reading of a small number of major texts. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Seminar demands close reading of difficult primary texts and sustained analytical writing without subject-matter scaffolding.

name: Topics in International Politics I
description: For advanced students of international relations. Various topics are taken up each year, the content of which depends on the instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar requiring strong prior IR foundation and sustained analytical writing on specialized topics.

name: Topics in International Politics II
description: For advanced students of international relations. Various topics are taken up each year, the content of which depends on the instructor.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced seminar assumes strong IR foundation from prerequisites; topic-driven format demands sustained engagement and sophisticated analysis.

name: Topics in Canadian Politics I
description: Examines in depth enduring and emerging issues in Canadian politics. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
prerequisites: POL214H1/ POL214Y1/ POL215H5/ POL216H5/ POL224H1/ POL224Y1/ POLB50Y3; 1.0 other POL/ JPI credit in Canadian politics. See the Department's website http://politics.utoronto.ca/undergraduate/courses/fallwinter-timetable/ for POL courses by area group.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar on specialized Canadian politics topics requires strong foundational knowledge from prerequisites and sustained analytical engagement.

name: Topics in Canadian Politics II
description: Examines in depth enduring and emerging issues in Canadian politics. Content in any given year depends on instructor.
prerequisites: POL214H1/ POL214Y1/ POL215H5/ POL216H5/ POL224H1/ POL224Y1/ POLB50Y3; 1.0 other POL/ JPI credit in Canadian politics. See the Department's website http://politics.utoronto.ca/undergraduate/courses/fallwinter-timetable/ for POL courses by area group.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requiring strong foundational Canadian politics knowledge and sustained analytical writing throughout semester.

name: Topics in Comparative Politics IV
description: Selected issues in comparative politics. Vary from year to year.
prerequisites: 2.0 credits in POL/ JPA/ JPF/ JPI/ JPR/ JPS/ JRA courses
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requires solid political science foundation and sustained critical engagement with specialized topics.

name: Independent Studies
description: Open only when a Political Science full-time faculty member is willing and available to supervise. Students must find an appropriate supervisor in the Department of Political Science and obtain the approval of the Director of Undergraduate Studies before enrolling. Obtain details and an application form from the Department Undergraduate Office. Application forms can also be downloaded from the Department's website at www.politics.utoronto.ca. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on chosen topic and supervisor expertise; workload is self-directed, making consistency and independence crucial for typical students.

name: Independent Studies
description: Open only when a Political Science full-time faculty member is willing and available to supervise. Students must find an appropriate supervisor in the Department of Political Science and obtain the approval of the Director of Undergraduate Studies before enrolling. Obtain details and an application form from the Department Undergraduate Office. Application forms can also be downloaded from the Department's website at www.politics.utoronto.ca. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on chosen research topic and supervisor expectations; workload is high due to sustained independent research output.

name: Senior Thesis and Thesis Seminar
description: A 40 to 60-page (15,000 to 20,000 word) research paper (75% of the final mark) written under the supervision of a political science faculty member and a companion thesis seminar (25% of final mark). The seminar provides a forum for students to periodically present and discuss their on-going research and to examine issues and approaches related to the structure, organization and presentation of the thesis. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: 14.0 credits, 3.0 GPA in Political Science courses, supervisor's approval, an approved thesis proposal.
difficulty_reasoning: Full independent research thesis demands sustained disciplinary maturity and self-direction rare before upper-year study.

name: Portuguese for Beginners I
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: Introductory course to Portuguese for students with no previous knowledge of this language or advanced proficiency in Spanish. Students develop basic knowledge and reading, listening, speaking and writing skills, within a communicative approach. Students are introduced to cultural aspects of the Portuguese-speaking world. Taught in English and Portuguese.
exclusions: PRT100Y1, PRT102H1, PRT120Y1, PRT120H1, PRT201H1, PRT202H1, PRT205H1, PRT219Y1, PRT220Y1, PRT301H1, PRT302H1, PRT320Y1, PRT420H1, PRT420Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Language courses demand consistent weekly practice and memorization, but beginner material is inherently self-contained and approachable.

name: Portuguese for Beginners II
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: A continuation of introductory Portuguese for non-native speakers. Students will expand their basic knowledge of cultural aspects of the Portuguese-speaking world and their reading, listening, speaking and writing skills in the target language. Taught in English and Portuguese.
exclusions: PRT100Y1, PRT120Y1, PRT120H1, PRT201H1, PRT202H1, PRT205H1, PRT219Y1, PRT220Y1, PRT301H1, PRT302H1, PRT320Y1, PRT420H1, PRT420Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Second language learning requires consistent daily practice and homework, but beginner material is intrinsically accessible and non-technical.

name: Portuguese for Spanish Speakers
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: Introduction to the Portuguese language for speakers of Spanish. Development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills with an emphasis on communication, and an overview of basic grammatical structures and building of vocabulary. Presentation of cultural aspects of the Portuguese-speaking world.
prerequisites: Native or advanced knowledge of Spanish, SPA320Y1/ SPA302H1 for non-native speakers of Spanish; no previous knowledge of Portuguese.
exclusions: PRT100Y1, PRT120Y1, PRT201H1, PRT202H1, PRT205H1, PRT219Y1, PRT219H1, PRT220Y1, PRT301H1, PRT302H1, PRT320Y1, PRT420Y1, PRT420H1
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory language course with Spanish foundation eases comprehension; regular speaking and writing practice requires consistent weekly effort.

name: Intermediate Portuguese I
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: This course expands the linguistic repertoire of learners beyond basic structures by offering a review of grammar at an intermediate level. Introduction to authentic cultural materials from the Portuguese-speaking world with practice designed to build vocabulary, and to improve oral and written expression. Taught in Portuguese with occasional English.
prerequisites: PRT100Y1/ PRT102H1/ PRT120Y1/ PRT120H1 or placement test
exclusions: PRT202H1, PRT205H1, PRT219Y1, PRT219H1, PRT220Y1, PRT301H1, PRT302H1, PRT320Y1, PRT420Y1, PRT420H1
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate grammar and authentic cultural materials demand consistent engagement, but manageable for students with foundational Portuguese from prerequisites.

name: Intermediate Portuguese II
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: This course continues to expand the linguistic repertoire of learners to a high intermediate level, expanding the use of authentic cultural materials from the Portuguese-speaking world and promoting increased development of vocabulary, and oral and written expression. Taught in Portuguese with occasional English.
exclusions: PRT205H1, PRT219Y1, PRT219H1, PRT220Y1, PRT301H1, PRT302H1, PRT320Y1, PRT420Y1, PRT420H1
difficulty_reasoning: Continuous language practice and authentic material demand consistent engagement, but intermediate proficiency builds naturally on prerequisite foundation.

name: Introduction to the Literatures of the Portuguese-Speaking World
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: Students expand their reading, writing, and critical skills through the study of a representative selection of short stories and poetry from Portugal, Brazil, and Lusophone Africa. Required for a program degree. Taught in Portuguese.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced language proficiency and literary analysis required; consistent reading and essay writing across multiple traditions.

name: Advanced Portuguese I
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: Intensive practice in written and oral Portuguese for the student with advanced proficiency in the language. Selective review of grammar with emphasis on complex language structures. Taught in Portuguese.
prerequisites: PRT202H1/ PRT205H1/ PRT219Y1/ PRT219H1/ PRT220Y1 or placement test
difficulty_reasoning: Taught entirely in Portuguese with advanced grammar and complex structures demands fluency our average student lacks.

name: Advanced Portuguese II
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: This course continues to improve written and oral proficiency for the student with advanced proficiency in Portuguese, offering a selective review of grammar with emphasis on the complex sentence. Taught in Portuguese.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced grammar and complex sentence analysis taught entirely in Portuguese demands sustained immersion and disciplinary maturity.

name: Portuguese Language and Society
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: Explores how factors such as ethnicity, gender, social class and age affect the use of Portuguese language and determine its characteristics. The course overviews mostly the aspects of Brazilian and European Portuguese. (Offered in alternate years.)
prerequisites: PRT202H1/ PRT205H1/ PRT219Y1/ PRT219H1/ PRT220Y1/ PRT120Y1 or Portuguese placement test.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level linguistics requires solid Portuguese foundation from prerequisites; sociolinguistic concepts demand engagement but are accessible.

name: Topics in Portuguese Studies
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: Exploration of cultural topics relevant to the Portuguese-speaking world. Examples include: Brazilian Popular Music, Soccer and Society, Luso-Afro-Brazilian Environmental Narratives, and Lusophone Women Writers. Students can enroll in this course multiple times, as long as the topic of the course is different. For any questions, please reach out to the Associate Chairs of Undergraduate Studies or Portuguese Studies. Taught in Portuguese.
prerequisites: PRT258H1, PRT120H1/ PRT205H1/ PRT219Y1/ PRT219H1 or Portuguese placement test
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced Portuguese language proficiency required; specialized cultural content demands sustained engagement and discussion.

name: Contemporary Brazilian Cinema
department: Dept. of Italian Spanish Portuguese & Latin American Studies
description: This course covers aesthetic, cultural, and social aspects of contemporary Brazilian cinema. The course examines both works of fiction and documentaries from emerging voices as well as world renowned filmmakers, spanning from Cinema Novo to the present. Topics include: music and urban culture, violence, inequality, environmental justice, and gender and sexuality. Lecture time is divided between film screening and class discussion held in English. Students choose tutorials in Portuguese (necessary for this course to be considered towards credit in Portuguese programs) or English.
difficulty_reasoning: No prior film knowledge required; lectures screen films with discussion. Light weekly readings on assigned movies and cultural context.

name: Biomedical Research at the Cutting Edge
description: Explore the thought processes, logic, motivation, techniques, analysis and impact of recent high-profile publications to gain insight into the enterprise of science. Outstanding scientists present recent high-impact papers, and students will examine the research in depth, focusing on the underlying questions, experimental approach, results and significance. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: SBI4U and SCH4U (Grade 12 University Preparation Biology and Chemistry); permission of Department
difficulty_reasoning: Requires strong high school science foundation and comfort reading primary research papers, but content is guided and structured.

name: Basic Human Physiology
description: A survey course covering all organ systems intended for students who are not proceeding further in Physiology.
exclusions: Any 300-level PSL course taken previously or concurrently
difficulty_reasoning: Survey format with no prerequisites makes concepts accessible, but breadth of organ systems requires consistent memorization and integration effort.

name: Introduction to Physiologic Adaptations of Marine Mammals
description: Systems approach to physiology of marine mammals in their aquatic environment. Highlights unique features of cardiovascular, respiratory, renal, urinary, and reproductive systems. Introduces relevant physiology, and makes comparisons to human condition and disease.
prerequisites: (BIO120H1, BIO130H1), CHM136H1/CHM151Y1. Students who do not have all of the required prerequisites are encouraged to contact the course coordinator to seek a waiver.
difficulty_reasoning: Specialized physiology content requires sustained engagement with unfamiliar systems and comparisons, but is self-contained and introductory within its domain.

name: Research Opportunity Program
description: Credit course for supervised participation in faculty research project. Details at https://www.artsci.utoronto.ca/current/academics/research-opportunities/research-opportunities-program. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on the research project assigned. Workload is steady but self-paced within supervisor expectations.

name: Human Physiology I
description: Principles of neurophysiology, endocrinology and reproductive physiology for students enrolled in Life Science programs.
recommended_preparation: BIO130H1; CHM136H1/ CHM151Y1; and 1.0 credit from any of the following: MAT135H1, MAT136H1, MAT137Y1, MAT157Y1, PHY131H1, PHY132H1, PHY151H1, PHY152H1
difficulty_reasoning: Dense memorization of unfamiliar physiological systems and mechanisms requires sustained engagement but is conceptually self-contained without heavy math.

name: Human Physiology II
description: Principles of respiratory, cardiovascular, gastrointestinal, and renal physiology for students enrolled in Life Science programs.
recommended_preparation: BIO130H1; CHM136H1/CHM151Y1; and 1.0 credit from any of the following: MAT135H1, MAT136H1, MAT137Y1, MAT157Y1, PHY131H1, PHY132H1, PHY151H1, PHY152H1
difficulty_reasoning: Systems physiology requires learning multiple organ systems with unfamiliar terminology and mechanisms but is taught systematically with clear application.

name: Topics in Cellular, Molecular and Organismic Physiology I
description: Focus on two topics: (1) The regulation of body weight/composition including the hormonal, neural and metabolic interactions between adipose tissue, the brain, the pancreas, and muscle. (2) Cardiovascular and respiratory control systems in adults and neonates in terms of feedback control systems, mechanisms, and clinical disorders.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced physiology concepts require strong foundational knowledge from prerequisites; integration across multiple organ systems demands sustained study effort.

name: Topics in Cellular, Molecular and Organismic Physiology II
description: Focus on two topics: the brain and pregnancy. Delve into the physiology of the central nervous system along with associated diseases as model systems. Learn about fetal and maternal adaptations during pregnancy and the consequences of maladaptation. The course is designed for Physiology Specialist students, although permission is granted to other students upon Departmental approval.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced neuroscience and reproductive physiology require strong biochemistry foundation and sustained integration of complex systems knowledge.

name: Mammalian Molecular Biology
description: Extend molecular biology concepts to current scientific literature in mammalian physiology. Apply these principles to disease and complex behaviours. Discuss bioethical issues raised by these techniques and their application. Benefit from three weeks in small group seminars lead by research professors focusing on current advances in mammalian molecular biology.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced molecular concepts applied to primary literature and research seminars demand sustained engagement and specialized background understanding.

name: Mammalian Physiology Laboratory
description: A laboratory course covering selected topics in physiology. An ancillary fee of $53 will be charged for lab materials.
prerequisites: BIO230H1/(BIO240H1, BIO241H1)/BIO255H1, BCH210H1/BCH242Y1, MAT100-series/PHY100-series, PSL300H1, PSL301H
difficulty_reasoning: Lab-based course demands hands-on experimental design, data analysis, and formal write-ups weekly with minimal margin for error.

name: Advanced Physiology Laboratory
description: A problem-based laboratory course focused on the integration and control of organ systems to understand body functions. Enrollment in this course is restricted to students in the Physiology Specialist and Major Programs although permission is granted to students in other programs upon approval by the Department. An ancillary fee of $53 will be charged for lab materials.
prerequisites: BIO230H1/ (BIO240H1, BIO241H1)/ BIO255H1, PSL300H1, PSL301H1, PSL372H1
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced lab course requiring mastery of organ system physiology and independent experimental design with heavy written deliverables.

name: Research Opportunity Program
description: Credit course for supervised participation in faculty research project. Details at https://www.artsci.utoronto.ca/current/academics/research-opportunities/research-opportunities-program. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on the research project's nature and supervisor expectations; workload is sustained lab or field work spread across semester.

name: Regenerative Medicine
description: Regenerative medicine promises to restore body function that has been lost due to disease, damage or age. Students will explore selected molecular, stem cell and tissue engineering developments to better understand how the body normally functions and how cells, tissues, and organs can be repaired or replaced.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-year physiology requiring mastery of PSL300/301 foundations and synthesizing complex molecular, cellular, and tissue-level concepts simultaneously.

name: Reproduction: Development and Function
description: This course provides an in-depth review of the development and function of the male and female reproductive systems. Topics include sex determination and differentiation, steroidogenesis, gametogenesis, hormonal control of the reproductive axis, the female ovulatory cycle, fertilization and implantation, infertility and assisted reproduction approaches. Joint undergraduate/graduate course - PSL420H1/LMP2100H
prerequisites: A minimum grade of 78% is required in PSL300H1 and PSL301H1. If minimum grade has not been met students are advised to contact the Department of Physiology at physiology.undergrad@utoronto.ca for approval.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced physiology content with heavy integration of biochemistry, endocrinology, and developmental biology demands strong foundational knowledge.

name: Pregnancy and Birth: From Implantation to Newborn Life
description: General overview of the integrated physiological events associated with fetal development, pregnancy and birth. The approach emphasizes physiological processes using insights gained from studies of humans, animals, cells and genes. Where appropriate the clinical consequences of aberrant development are reviewed. Joint undergraduate/graduate course - PSL421H1/PSL1421H
prerequisites: A final grade of 75% or higher in each of PSL300H1 and PSL301H1. If minimum is not reached, please contact the Department of Physiology at physiology.undergrad@utoronto.ca for approval.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced physiology requiring strong PSL300/301 foundation; complex integrated systems demand sustained study and synthesis.

name: Integrative Metabolism and its Endocrine Regulation
description: This course integrates the newest findings and experimental approaches from cellular and molecular biology into metabolic function at the tissue, organ and whole body level.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced integration of multiple organ systems and molecular mechanisms requires substantial prior physiology and biochemistry foundation.

name: Systems Neuroscience I: Neuroanatomy, Neurophysiology and Higher Cognition
description: Introduction to systems neuroscience including neuroanatomy, neuroimaging and human neurophysiology (EEG, MEG), neural support for higher cognitive functions (e.g., memory, language), network neuroscience, and computational modeling.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires mastery of neuroanatomy, physiology, and computational modeling with minimal assumed background beyond PSL300.

name: Systems Neuroscience II: Sensory and Motor
description: In depth study of the sensory and motor systems, including clinical concerns/applications, neuromodulation, and brain-computer interfaces.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level neuroscience requiring solid PSL and HMB foundation; dense mechanistic content demands sustained engagement.

name: Neuroscience: Cellular and Molecular
description: Overview of the fundamentals of cellular and molecular aspects of brain function. Course material is updated yearly to reflect the rapid evolution of ideas in Neuroscience. Joint undergraduate/graduate course - PSL445H1/PSL1445H/JNR1444Y
prerequisites: PSL300H1, PSL301H1, CJH332H1 or permission of instructor A minimum grade of 78% is required in PSL300H1, PSL301H1 and CJH332H1. If minimum grade has not been met students are advised to contact the Department of Physiology at physiology.undergrad@utoronto.ca for approval.
difficulty_reasoning: Joint undergrad/grad course demanding mastery of complex molecular mechanisms and research-level depth beyond typical 300-level prerequisite background.

name: Neural Disorders
description: Explore topics in neurological disorders and treatments to reinforce and expand your knowledge of cellular and molecular neurophysiology. Course material is updated yearly to reflect the rapid evolution of ideas in this area. Joint undergraduate/graduate course - PSL446H1/PSL1446H/JNR1444Y
prerequisites: PSL300H1, PSL301H1, CJH332H1 or permission of instructor A minimum grade of 78% is required in PSL300H1, PSL301H1 and CJH332H1. If minimum grade has not been met students are advised to contact the Department of Physiology at physiology.undergrad@utoronto.ca for approval.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced neurophysiology content builds on multiple prerequisite courses; joint undergrad/grad cohort raises expectations significantly.

name: Mechanisms of Neural and Endocrinal Secretion
description: Exocytosis and other aspects of secretion mainly in neurons and neuroendocrine cells, but also in pancreatic cells. Topics include synapse anatomy and physiology, synaptic plasma membrane and vesicle proteins, membrane fusion, genetic tools, endocrine secretion, plasticity in neurotransmitter release, diseases arising from secretion defects.
difficulty_reasoning: Highly specialized upper-year physiology requiring mastery of multiple cumulative biochemistry and cell biology foundations simultaneously.

name: Membrane Physiology
description: Biophysics and molecular biology of ion channels. Topics include equivalent circuits for cells, molecular structure of voltage-gated channels, distribution of channels, relationship between single-channel and whole-cell recording, and regulation of channel function by voltage, phosphorylation, G-proteins and metabolites. Joint undergraduate/graduate course - PSL452H1/PSL1452H
prerequisites: PSL300H1, PSL301H1 A minimum grade of 75% is required in PSL300H1 and PSL301H1. If minimum grade has not been met students are advised to contact the Department of Physiology at physiology.undergrad@utoronto.ca for approval.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced biophysics content demands strong math and physics foundations; joint undergrad/grad course reflects steep conceptual density.

name: Molecular Aspects of Cardiovascular Function
description: Heart anatomy and development, ion channels and contractile proteins involved in cardiac and smooth muscle contraction are studied. Emphasis is on regulation of electrical and contractile function of kinases, metabolism, volume and ions. Joint undergraduate/graduate course - PSL462H1/PSL1462H
prerequisites: PSL300H1, PSL301H1 A minimum grade of 75% is required in PSL300H1 and PSL301H1. If minimum grade has not been met students are advised to contact the Department of Physiology at physiology.undergrad@utoronto.ca for approval.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced molecular mechanisms and signaling pathways require substantial prior physiology foundation and cumulative problem-solving.

name: Cardiovascular Physiology
description: Development of the cardiovascular system from conception to adulthood with particular emphasis on maturational changes, age-related differences and developmental problems from cellular/molecular to whole organ/system.
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced cellular and developmental concepts build on physiology foundation; requires sustained integration of complex mechanisms across multiple scales.

name: Sleep Physiology and Chronobiology
description: This course covers the physiology underlying sleep and circadian rhythms, and their impact on important physiological processes and health. The integrative nature of the basic physiological processes is emphasized via discussions and clinical presentations. Overall, the aim is to present the full spectrum of integrative physiology from molecules and cells to understanding the sick patient.
prerequisites: PSL300H1, PSL301H1 A minimum grade of 75% is required in PSL300H1 and PSL301H1. If minimum grade has not been met students are advised to contact the Department of Physiology at physiology.undergrad@utoronto.ca for approval.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-year physiology course demanding strong foundation from prerequisites and integrative mastery of complex systems.

name: Project in Physiology
description: Laboratory research project with reading assignments leading to a final report. By special arrangement with a Physiology staff member after admission to course. PSL498Y1 is recommended for students applying to the Physiology graduate program. Enrollment in this course is restricted to students in the Physiology Specialist and Major Programs, although permission is granted to students in other programs upon approval by the Department. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: (PSL304H1, PSL305H1)/PSL303Y1, PSL372H1, PSL374H1, permission of Department
difficulty_reasoning: Year-long independent research project with advanced physiology prerequisites demands sustained lab work, data analysis, and report writing.

name: Project in Physiology
description: Laboratory research project with reading assignments leading to a final report. By special arrangement with a Physiology staff member after admission to course. PSL499H1 is recommended for students applying to the Physiology graduate program. Enrollment in this course is restricted to students in the Physiology Specialist and Major Programs, although permission is granted to students in other programs upon approval by the Department. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: (PSL304H1, PSL305H1)/PSL303Y1, PSL372H1, PSL374H1, permission of Department
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research project demands advanced physiology knowledge, experimental design maturity, and sustained lab work across full semester.

name: First-Year Learning Community
department: ASDN: Arts and Science, Office of the Dean
difficulty_reasoning: Learning community format prioritizes community building and transition support over rigorous content delivery or assessment.

name: Introductory Psychology
description: A brief introductory survey of psychology as both a biological and social science. Topics will include behavioural neuroscience, learning, perceptual, motivational, cognitive, developmental, personality, abnormal, and social psychology.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory survey with no prerequisites, broad conceptual overview, moderate weekly readings, and standard exams.

name: Statistics I
description: Fundamentals of descriptive and inferential statistics, including population and sampling distributions, simple association, probability, estimation, and hypothesis testing.
exclusions: ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ KPE291H1/ KPE391H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3
difficulty_reasoning: Math-heavy content with new concepts requires steady practice, but course supports learning through structured problem sets and clear applications.

name: Statistics II
description: Fundamentals of statistical analysis of experimental and observational data including linear models, the analysis of variance, a priori contrasts, post-hoc tests, power analysis and effect size calculations.
prerequisites: PSY201H1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3
exclusions: ECO220Y1/ SOC252H1/ STA221H1/ KPE291H1/ KPE391H1/ PSY202H5/ ECO220Y5/ STA221H5/ PSYB06H3/ PSYC08H3/ STAB27H3/ STAC32H3
difficulty_reasoning: Builds on PSY201 foundation with abstract concepts like power analysis and ANOVA requiring sustained mathematical engagement.

name: Psychological Research
description: This course provides an introduction to conceiving, designing, and conducting research in psychology. It prepares students to be both consumers and producers of scientific research, and also addresses basic issues related to the work of psychological scientists such as theory development, research ethics, and scientific writing. Students in this course will gain insight into the scientific process as a whole – its advantages, difficulties, and limitations. As such, students will be able to better evaluate the knowledge that psychological science can provide, and integrate that knowledge into a broader worldview.
corequisites: PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3
difficulty_reasoning: Introduces research methods without assuming prior expertise, but requires sustained engagement with statistical and methodological concepts throughout.

name: Psychological Strategies for Learning and Living Well
description: What does it take to thrive in university and beyond? This course explores the psychological science underlying personal growth, well-being, and academic success. Drawing on cognitive, social, and positive psychology, students examine evidence-based strategies for building habits, enhancing attention and memory, managing stress, improving sleep, and fostering motivation. In other words, students learn how to thrive (and not just survive) in university. The course emphasizes applied learning: students identify personal goals and integrate course principles into their daily lives through weekly reflections, small-group discussions, and a culminating creative project. Students also develop skills in critically evaluating popular psychology and self-help claims. By combining scientific insight with practical application, this course prepares students to thrive academically and personally, strengthen interpersonal relationships, and cultivate self-regulation and resilience.
difficulty_reasoning: Applied psychology with no technical prerequisites; weekly reflections and discussions are manageable; practical focus makes concepts immediately relatable.

name: Introduction to Developmental Psychology
description: The developmental approach to the study of behaviour with a focus on infancy through childhood. Topics include sensorimotor skills, cognition, socialization, personality, and emotional behaviour.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory psychology course covering descriptive developmental concepts with minimal quantitative or theoretical abstraction required.

name: Introduction to Social Psychology
description: Contemporary areas of research in social psychology: social perception, attitudes, inter-personal relations, and group processes.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory social psychology with relatable real-world examples; manageable readings and standard essays for a 200-level course.

name: Personality and Its Transformations
description: Theory and research in personality structure and dynamics: the interaction of cultural and biological factors in the development and expression of individual differences.
difficulty_reasoning: Requires foundational psychology knowledge from prerequisite; moderate reading and essay assignments typical of 300-level humanities.

name: Introduction to Psychopathology and Clinical Science
description: A critical survey of concepts, theories, and the state of research in the area of psychopathology and therapeutic methods.
difficulty_reasoning: Introduces clinical concepts and mental health frameworks without assuming prior psychology background. Manageable with consistent reading and engagement.

name: Introduction to Learning and Plasticity
description: Concepts, theories, and applications of classical and contemporary learning theories, including classical and operant conditioning. Current theories of the physiological and anatomical basis of learning and memory, including synaptic plasticity, the role of the hippocampus, amygdala, frontal cortex and other brain regions. Theories will be related to a practical understanding and applications such as drug addiction, phobias and other disorders.
difficulty_reasoning: Neuroscience concepts and terminology demand engagement, but prerequisite psychology foundation and applied examples make it manageable with consistent study.

name: Introduction to Cognitive Psychology
description: An introduction to research and theory on the neural and cognitive architecture of attention, memory, language, thinking and reasoning.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory cognitive psychology builds on PSY100 foundation with accessible concepts but requires consistent engagement and reading.

name: Introduction to Sensation and Perception
description: An introduction to the physiological and psychological basis of perception across the different sensory modalities in humans and lower animals, with an emphasis on vision. Exploring visual perception such as shape and objects, scenes, colour, space, and motion as well as auditory perception of simple and complex sounds, and location. Further topics may include touch, including perception of temperature, pain and body posture, the chemical senses, and cross-modal influences of the senses on one another. In-class demonstrations may supplement the lectures.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory sensory science with visual focus and demonstrations; requires engagement but no math or specialist prerequisites.

name: Behavioural Neuroscience
description: Provides students with a solid background in the biological basis of behaviour. Animal and human research topics will include functional neuroanatomy and neural signalling. Further topics may include sensory and motor control, motivational systems, hormones, and emotions, among other topics.
difficulty_reasoning: Neuroanatomy and mechanisms are novel and conceptually dense for first-timers, but prerequisite psychology foundation makes it manageable.

name: Research Opportunity Program
description: Credit course for supervised participation in faculty research project. Details at https://www.artsci.utoronto.ca/current/academics/research-opportunities/research-opportunities-program. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Difficulty depends entirely on research project complexity and supervisor expectations; workload varies by project scope and intensity.

name: Research Opportunity Program
description: Credit course for supervised participation in faculty research project. Details at https://www.artsci.utoronto.ca/current/academics/research-opportunities/research-opportunities-program. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Supervised research requires learning domain-specific methods and concepts on the job, but mentor support reduces independent struggle significantly.

name: Group Research Project
description: A full academic year intensive research project for those seeking their first hands-on research experience. Students work in small groups (3-5 students) to conduct a project under the direction of a faculty member. Students also learn foundational research skills and present their work during 2-hour lectures. Three-hour practical sessions are dedicated to group work and project coordination. Students are expected to devote an additional 5-hours per week to research activities. Each project culminates in a journal-style report and oral presentation. Students must complete an application form before the start of the academic year. The application form is available on the Department’s website at https://psych.utoronto.ca/current-program-students/undergraduate-courses, and must be submitted no later than August 1st. Students will be matched with Faculty Supervisors early in the term. Enrolment is restricted to students in the Psychology Major program. Not eligible for CR/NCR option. Note: Students are encouraged to complete both PSY202H1 and PSY203H1 (or their listed exclusions), but only one is required as a corequisite.
prerequisites: PSY100H1/ PSY100Y5/ (PSYA01H3 and PSYA02H3)/ COG250Y1, and PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3, and 1.5 credits of additional courses in PSY
corequisites: PSY202H1/ ECO220Y1/ SOC252H1/ STA221H1/ PSY202H5/ ECO220Y5/ STA221H5/ PSYB06H3/ PSYC08H3/ STAB27H3/ STAC32H3/ PSY203H1/ PSYB70H3
exclusions: PSY299H1/ PSY299Y1/ PSY309H1/ PSY399H1/ PSY399Y1/ PSY405H1/ PSY405Y1/ PSY406H1/ PSY406Y1/ ROP299H1/ ROP299Y1/ ROP399H1/ ROP399Y1
difficulty_reasoning: Year-long research project with 8 hours weekly commitment, group coordination complexity, and unfamiliar methods for first-time researchers.

name: The Treatment of Psychological Data
description: This course provides a practical yet intensive introduction to the research pipeline, with a focus on research data management and advanced statistical analysis and inference. Students learn how to find, organize, and analyze data sets in a transparent and reproducible way. Students also learn more about statistical inference, focusing on how the design and analysis of data shape the interpretation of results.
prerequisites: PSY202H1/ ECO220Y1/ SOC252H1/ STA221H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY202H5/ STA221H5/ PSYB06H3/ PSYC08H3/ STAB27H3/ STAC32H3
difficulty_reasoning: Requires solid statistics foundation and hands-on data analysis skills; weekly assignments and coding practice consume substantial time.

name: Research Specialization: Practicum
description: Research Specialists learn about the research opportunities within our department, and develop skills in the areas of critical thinking, writing, and oral presentation. Students also conduct a pre-thesis research project under the supervision of a faculty member. Students should ideally complete a PSY lab course (PSY3*9H1) during the same year this course is taken; however, permission to take the lab course in subsequent years may be granted in consultation with the Undergraduate Administrator (psy.undergrad@utoronto.ca). Enrolment is restricted to PSY Research Specialists.
prerequisites: PSY202H1/ ECO220Y1/ SOC252H1/ STA221H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY202H5/ STA221H5/ PSYB06H3/ PSYC08H3/ STAB27H3/ STAC32H3
corequisites: PSY319H1/ PSY329H1/ PSY339H1/ PSY349H1/ PSY359H1/ PSY369H1/ PSY379H1/ PSY389H1
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research project under faculty supervision demands sustained effort, critical thinking, and writing skills across the full term.

name: Social Development
description: Theory and research in social attachment, aggression, morality, imitation and identification, altruism, and parental discipline, with discussion of methodological issues.
prerequisites: PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3, and PSY210H1/ PSY210H5/ PSYB20H3
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-year psychology course requiring solid research methods foundation and engagement with empirical literature on child development.

name: Cognitive Development
description: This course examines the developmental of knowledge in fundamental domains such as spatial perception, navigation, object perception, number, language, and theory of mind. Emphasis is on current experimental findings and how they address centuries-old debates surrounding the origin and nature of human knowledge.
prerequisites: PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3, and PSY210H1/ PSY210H5/ PSYB20H3
difficulty_reasoning: Experimental findings are accessible but require sustained engagement with research papers and developmental theory throughout the semester.

name: Psychology of Aging
description: Age changes in sensory and perceptual processes, motor skill, learning, memory, and personality. Theory, methodological problems, social, cultural, and environmental influences that shape behaviour and attitudes towards and among the elderly.
prerequisites: PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3, and PSY210H1/ PSY210H5/ PSYB20H3
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory gerontology concepts with modest empirical and statistical demands; prerequisite research methods foundation makes content accessible.

name: Developmental Laboratory
description: This laboratory course provides an overview of developmental psychology methods. The class conducts an original research project, including design, data collection, data analysis, and a written report. Course capacity is limited to 15 students and priority is given to PSY Specialists and Research Specialists during the first enrolment period.
prerequisites: PSY202H1/ ECO220Y1/ SOC252H1/ STA221H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY202H5/ STA221H5/ PSYB06H3/ PSYC08H3/ STAB27H3/ STAC32H3, and PSY210H1/ PSY210H5/ PSYB20H3
difficulty_reasoning: Original research project with design, data collection, analysis, and reporting demands substantial methodological maturity and sustained output.

name: Social Psychology: Attitudes
description: An intensive study of social attitude development, description, measurement, modification, and organization.
prerequisites: PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3, and PSY220H1/ PSY220H5/ PSYB10H3/ SOC213H1
difficulty_reasoning: Intermediate psychology course requiring statistics foundation and prior PSY coursework, manageable with consistent reading and study effort.

name: Cross-Cultural Psychology
description: One of the hallmarks of human behaviour is its diversity. Some of the ways in which we differ are thought to be relatively idiosyncratic (e.g., specific aspects of personality), whereas others are fairly systematic. Cultural psychology is one area of research in human behaviour that examines systematic differences resulting from individuals’ cultural backgrounds. This course will introduce you to the consideration of cultural variation in the study of human thought and behaviour.
prerequisites: PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3, and PSY220H1/ PSY220H5/ PSYB10H3/ SOC213H1
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory psychology content with statistics prerequisite already satisfied; manageable reading and essay-based assessment typical of mid-level psychology.

name: Intergroup Relations
description: An in-depth examination of theories and research in intergroup relations; focuses on stereotyping, prejudice, discrimination, and stigma.
prerequisites: PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3, and PSY220H1/ PSY220H5/ PSYB10H3/ SOC213H1
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-year psychology course requiring solid stats and research methods foundation, moderate reading load, and conceptual engagement with social phenomena.

name: The Psychology of Good and Evil
description: How is it that we are capable of transcendent kindness—and unspeakable cruelty? How do we explain people’s strongly held opinions about abortion, gay marriage, affirmative action, eating meat, and torture? How do evolution, culture, and religion conspire to shape our moral natures? These are among the most important—and exciting—questions around, and they are the focus of this course. We will explore the modern psychological science of moral belief and action from a range of sub-disciplines, including social-personality psychology, cognitive neuroscience, and developmental psychology. We will look at research from the lab, from the community, and from the battlefield; we will discuss babies, monkeys, and psychopaths; we will debate claims about moral differences between men and women, liberals and conservatives, Christians and Muslims. We will explore issues such as prejudice and bigotry, sexuality and purity, and punishment, revenge, and forgiveness.
recommended_preparation: PSY201H1/ ECO220Y1/ EEB225H1/ GGR270H1/ IRW220H1/ POL222H1/ SOC202H1/ STA220H1/ STA238H1/ STA248H1/ STA288H1/ ECO220Y5/ PSY201H5/ STA215H5/ STA220H5/ PSYA06H3/ PSYB07H3/ STAB22H3/ STAB23H3/ STAB57H3, and PSY220H1/ PSY220H5/ PSYB10H3/ SOC213H1
difficulty_reasoning: Accessible psychology content requiring only intro-level background; moderate weekly readings and essays typical for 300-level humanities course.

name: Social Cognition
