Written application (detailed proposal, reading list and a letter of support from a Victoria College faculty member who is prepared to supervise) must be submitted for approval on behalf of Victoria College. For application procedures visit the Victoria College website. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: A minimum CGPA of 3.0, completion of 15.0 credits, and permission of the College Program Director
difficulty_reasoning: Self-designed curriculum requires independent research and writing; supervisor guidance mitigates difficulty but workload depends entirely on chosen scope.

name: Vic Capstone Research Colloquium
description: This seminar provides work-in-progress support for students pursuing full-year or half-year Individual Studies projects. In an interdisciplinary seminar, students receive training and practice in project design, professional skills, and effective communication in a variety of fields and contexts. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: A minimum CGPA of 3.0, completion of 15.0 credits, and an application are required
corequisites: Registration in an Independent / Individual Studies or Senior Essay course
exclusions: POL499Y1, RLG404H1/RLG405H1, UNI460H1, thesis seminars in other programs
difficulty_reasoning: Seminar format with peer feedback and project support reduces intellectual demand; sustained individual research project creates moderate consistent workload.

name: TDSB SEE Program Co-op
difficulty_reasoning: Co-op placement with minimal academic content; primarily experiential learning in workplace setting with light structured coursework.

name: First-Year Learning Community
department: ASDN: Arts and Science, Office of the Dean
difficulty_reasoning: Learning community courses emphasize peer support and transition skills with minimal prerequisite knowledge required or technical content.

name: Order and Disorder I: Issues and Perspectives
description: Societies require law and order, but at what point does order become oppression? How do we balance our need for freedom and society’s need for order? This interdisciplinary seminar allows students to explore these and related questions through selected readings introducing theories from sociology, political science, philosophy, and history. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Interdisciplinary seminar with no prerequisites assumes no specialist background; reading-heavy but conceptually accessible to curious first-year students.

name: Order and Disorder II: Problems and Solutions
description: This interdisciplinary seminar introduces students to some of the methods used by scholars and researchers in sociology, political science, philosophy, and history to develop, test, and debate possible solutions to the problems of social order and disorder. Topics will vary from year to year. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Interdisciplinary seminar with no prerequisites assumes no background knowledge and emphasizes discussion over technical mastery.

name: From Rayguns to Light Sabers: Science Fiction in Contemporary Culture
description: This course examines science fiction as pop culture genre, media industry, and sociocultural phenomenon, with attention to some of its key themes, its important texts, creators, and audiences, and its place in contemporary culture. We will study some works chosen by the class as well as by the instructor. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: Survey course on familiar pop culture with no prerequisites requiring no specialized knowledge or technical skills.

name: Indigenous Knowledge and Storytelling in Toronto
description: The land now known as Toronto has a 13,000+ year old history of Indigenous presence that is still unfolding. This history is inscribed in the land – it is visible in the geographical features, place names, and contemporary urban form of the city and is represented through stories (oral and written) told by diverse members of Toronto’s Indigenous community. This course engages with stories of Indigenous history and presence in Toronto through a selection of Indigenous literary works about Toronto, Indigenous guest speakers, and a series of experiential Indigenous storytelling tours of significant locations across the city. Students will be introduced to Indigenous worldviews and ways of knowing and will learn why storytelling remains a significant and culturally-appropriate means for keeping and sharing land-based Indigenous Knowledge. Students will gain a deeper appreciation of the city as a traditional Indigenous territory and will reflect on their own relationships and responsibilities within these lands. Restricted to first-year students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites or specialist knowledge required; content-driven course emphasizing storytelling, guest speakers, and experiential learning rather than technical skill-building.

name: Introduction to STEM and Women and Gender Studies
description: Exploring a new theme each year, this course looks at the intersection of STEM fields and women and gender studies, showing how critical thinking about questions of gender and sexuality, race and colonialism within a transnational and interdisciplinary feminist frame brings fresh insights to the design, analysis and social impact of important issues in the sciences.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory interdisciplinary course requiring no STEM or gender studies background, accessible through critical thinking and readings.

name: Introduction to Women and Gender Studies
description: An integrated and historical approach to social relations of gender, race, class, sexuality and disability, particularly as they relate to women's lives and struggles across different locales, including Canada.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory survey with no prerequisites; accessible frameworks and primary sources require regular reading but minimal technical analysis.

name: Texts, Theories, Histories
description: Examines modes of theories that shaped feminist thought and situates them historically and transnationally so as to emphasize the social conditions and conflicts in which ideas and politics arise, change and circulate.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense theoretical reading and essay writing demand sustained engagement but no prior expertise assumed; typical 300-level humanities course.

name: Gender in Popular Culture
description: A critical examination of institutions, representations and practices associated with contemporary popular culture, mass-produced, local and alternative.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites required; analyzes familiar popular culture through accessible critical lenses without specialized disciplinary background assumed.

name: Gender & Environmental (In)Justice
description: Using a transnational, feminist framework, this course examines material and conceptual interrelations between gendered human and non-human nature, ecological crises, political economies and environmental movements in a variety of geographical, historical and cultural contexts. Does environmental justice include social justice, or are they in conflict? What might environmental justice and activism involve?
difficulty_reasoning: Interdisciplinary humanities with accessible conceptual frameworks and no prerequisites. Weekly readings and essays are standard for engaged students.

name: Radical Relationalities
description: This course activates the ontology ‘radical relationality’ to “orient our cells toward justice” (Yupik dance maker, choreographer Emily Johnson). This course is attuned towards exploring multiple patterns and formations of radical relationality, bridging theory with praxis to conjure future ancestral practices of living and forms of relation. Through understanding that relations are processes; and constitutive of matter- portals of possibilities for (re)worldings, this course enacts patterns of change-making across scales from the micro to the earthly and planetary. We explore interspecies relationalities and intimacies through decolonial thought; land/body justice; reciprocity, modes of transformative justice and care ‘otherwise.’ We explore radical frameworks of transformative justice, organizing, allyship, and other activations of radical relational processes as justice work from multiple communities, including Queer, Indigenous, and Black (and the various intersectional relations, solidarities, and radical co-commitments these communities share).
prerequisites: Minimum of 0.5 WGS credits or permission from department
exclusions: WGS280H1 (Kinstillations and Other Radical Relationalities), offered in Winter 2025 and 2026
difficulty_reasoning: Theory-heavy course requiring engagement with unfamiliar decolonial and queer frameworks, but no technical prerequisites and accessible entry point.

name: Special Topic in Women and Gender Studies
description: An upper level seminar. Subjects of study vary from year to year.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requires disciplinary foundation from prerequisite but relies on discussion and essays rather than specialist technical skills.

name: Special Topic in Women and Gender Studies
description: An upper level seminar. Subjects of study vary from year to year.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar requires strong essay and discussion skills but topic varies yearly, making advance preparation difficult for newcomers.

name: Gendered Labour Around the World
description: This course will focus on masculinities and femininities in workplace settings, with an emphasis on service work around the world. We will discuss workers' lived experiences of gender regimes which are embedded within the dynamics of class, race and nation. The relationships between gender processes and workplace hierarchies will be explored.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
exclusions: WGS363H1 Gendered Labour Around the World
difficulty_reasoning: Accessible conceptual framework requires consistent engagement with readings and weekly reflection essays on gendered labour themes.

name: Making Knowledge in a World that Matters
description: Teaches skills in feminist approaches to making knowledge. Introduces feminist practices for doing research and navigating the politics of production and exchange. Develops skills for conveying knowledge to the wider world, such as through research papers, reports, performance, new media, art.
difficulty_reasoning: Assumes foundational gender studies knowledge from prerequisite; essay and creative projects require critical thinking but are conceptually accessible.

name: Gender Issues in the Law
description: Examines the operation of the law as it affects women, the construction and representation of women within the legal system, and the scope for feminist and intersectional analyses of law. Includes an analysis of specific legal issues such as sexuality and reproduction, equality, employment, violence and immigration.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Legal concepts and feminist theory are new for most students; manageable with sustained engagement and consistent reading.

name: The Politics of Gender and Health
description: Examines diverse traditions and normative models of health (e.g. biomedicine, social constructionist, indigenous health) in conjunction with analyses of the origin, politics, and theoretical perspectives of contemporary Women's Health Movements. Topics may include fertility, sexuality, poverty, violence, labour, aging, (dis)ability, and health care provision.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Interdisciplinary humanities course requiring close reading and critical thinking about unfamiliar frameworks and social theory.

name: Studies in Post-Colonialism
description: Examines gendered representations of race, ethnicity, class, sexuality and disability in a variety of colonial, neo-colonial, and post-colonial contexts. Topics may include the emergence of racialist, feminist, liberatory and neoconservative discourses as inscribed in literary texts, historical documents, cultural artifacts and mass media.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Heavy reading and essay-writing in unfamiliar theoretical frameworks; requires sustained engagement with complex, jargon-dense texts.

name: Utopian Visions, Activist Realities
description: Drawing on diversely situated case-studies, this course focuses on the ideals that inform struggles for social justice, and the mechanisms activists have employed to produce the change. Foci include the gendered implications of movement participation, local and transnational coalition, alternative community formation, and encounters with the state and inter/supra/transnational organizations.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Essay-heavy humanities course requiring sustained engagement with social theory and case studies but no specialized prior knowledge.

name: Gender and Violence
description: An interdisciplinary study of gendered violence in both historical and contemporary contexts including topics such as textual and visual representations; legal and theoretical analyses; structural violence; war and militarization; sexual violence; and resistance and community mobilization.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Interdisciplinary humanities course requiring sustained engagement with emotionally demanding texts but no specialist prerequisites or technical skills.

name: Feminist Studies in Sexuality
description: Sexual agency as understood and enacted by women in diverse cultural and historical contexts. An exploration of the ways in which women have theorized and experienced sexual expectations, practices and identities. This course will be offered every three years.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Theory-heavy humanities course with no assumed background; manageable with consistent engagement in readings and discussion participation.

name: Studies in Queer and Trans
description: Takes up conversations in queer and trans studies as separate and entangled fields. It explores how queer and trans people have experienced and theorized gender and sexuality.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Theory-heavy humanities course requires sustained engagement with unfamiliar concepts but no prerequisite expertise assumed.

name: Playing, Sports, Cultures
description: Why do we play? How do movement practices shape our selves and environments? What do sports — and the cultures around them — mean and do today? This course explores sports worlds through the lenses of feminist cultural studies. Through a selection of key concepts and case studies, we examine questions of representation, identity, economics, policy and regulation, and audiences and fandoms to consider what sports cultures teach us about what it means to be human. Readings will touch on a variety of fields connected to feminist cultural studies, including Black feminism, trans studies, Indigenous studies, disability studies, fan studies, and feminist political economy. It explores the local and global power relations that shape sports worlds, what’s at stake when we move our bodies in different ways, and the transformative potential of play.
prerequisites: Minimum of 0.5 WGS credits or permission from department.
exclusions: WGS331H1 (Playing, Sports, Cultures), offered in Winter 2023, 2024, and 2025
difficulty_reasoning: Accessible cultural studies approach to sports with no technical prerequisites; consistent weekly readings and essays are manageable with regular effort.

name: Feminist Graphic Novels
description: Comics aren't new, and graphic novels aren't either, but feminists have built a rich array of stories about consciousness, resistance, and coming of age in this genre that warrant scholarly attention. In this case, we will read graphic novels for their subtleties, thinking about what picture and text make possible in the exploration of emotion, interconnection, and identity. Reading about resistance to marriage in Aya of Yop City, a child's view of revolution in Persepolis, parent child reckoning in Fun Home, and loneliness in Skim will advance students' understandings of the of the power of narrative and the pictorial displacement of innocence.
prerequisites: WGS160Y1Y or WGS271Y1Y or 1.0 WGS credit
difficulty_reasoning: Close reading of graphic novels is accessible without prior theory background; moderate weekly reading and essay writing typical of 300-level humanities.

name: Translating Sexuality: Queer Migration, Queer Diasporas
description: This course examines how notions of sexuality travel as people move within and beyond national borders. It investigates how queer and trans migrants pursue different versions of belonging, solidarity, survival, and hope. Participants will study transnational archives (which may include popular culture, new media, film, literature, and performance) as they trace globalization's effects on racialized, queer, and trans communities. Major topics may include: queer of color critique; queer settler colonialism; transnational and global south sexualities; imperialism and militarism; neoliberalism and homonationalism; humanitarianism and sexual rights; queer and trans social movements; postcolonial intimacies.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Heavy conceptual reading in unfamiliar theory; no math or coding; manageable with sustained engagement and prior WGS exposure.

name: Gender and Neoliberalism
description: Reviews major feminist transnational, Marxist and Foucauldian approaches to the study of neoliberalism. Adopts a comparative, historical and global approach to the ways that gender is implicated in state restructuring, changing roles for corporations and non-governmental organizations, changing norms for personhood, sovereignty and citizenship, and changing ideas about time/space.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Dense theoretical frameworks (Marxist, Foucauldian) and interdisciplinary scope demand sustained conceptual engagement without prior context.

name: Writing the Body
description: Examines the ways in which bodies are lived and inscribed and represented through a variety of genres. Students will work through issues of corporeality and materiality in the production and reception of texts and will practice embodied writing on a personal level through in-class workshops and written assignments.
difficulty_reasoning: No prerequisites needed; conceptual topics are accessible, but writing workshops and creative assignments demand consistent effort.

name: The Politics of Girlhood
description: The course communicates the growing field of "girl studies" and provides a critical exploration of the historical, social, psychological and political definitions attached to girlhood. We will move toward a feminist understanding of how definitions of girl-child shape individual experience, historical narratives, cultural representations, political agendas and futures.
prerequisites: 0.5 credit from WGS core courses. Students who do not meet the prerequisite are encouraged to contact the department.
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level humanities course requiring close reading of theory and cultural analysis, but no technical prerequisites or prior disciplinary knowledge assumed.

name: Advanced Topics in Women and Gender Studies
description: An upper level seminar. Topics vary from year to year depending on instructor. Please consult the Women & Gender Studies Institute's website for more information.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1 and 1.0 WGS credit at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar demands disciplinary maturity, strong writing, and sustained critical engagement with specialized gender theory.

name: Advanced Topics in Women and Gender Studies
description: An upper level seminar. Topics vary from year to year depending on instructor.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1 and 1.0 WGS credit at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar demands strong WGS foundation and sustained critical engagement with evolving topics.

name: Advanced Topics in Women and Gender Studies
description: An upper level seminar. Topics vary from year to year depending on the instructor.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1 and 1.0 WGS credit at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar demands strong reading comprehension and analytical writing; topics vary, requiring students to engage deeply with specialized scholarship.

name: Advanced Topics in Women and Gender Studies
description: An upper level seminar. Topics vary from year to year depending on the instructor.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1 and 1.0 WGS credit at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level seminar demands sustained critical engagement, seminar participation, and substantial reading in specialized gender studies theory.

name: Toxic Worlds, Decolonial Futures
description: This course explores the ways environmental violence is an integral practice of settler colonialism that affects human and non-human life, disrupts Indigenous sovereignty, and enacts ongoing racism. A typical way of addressing environmental violence is to document the harm done to bodies and communities. This class asks, how might we also refuse environmental violence and enact better obligations to land/body relations? What kind of decolonial futures can be summoned in the aftermath of environmental violence? Our readings will bring Indigenous feminist approaches together with Black feminist, queer, and feminist environmental justice approaches. Participants will build upon the readings to create their own decolonial environmental justice future projects.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1 and 1.0 WGS credit at the 300+ level
exclusions: WGS463H1 (Advanced Topics in Gender Theory: Toxic Worlds, Decolonial Futures), offered in Winter 2018, Winter 2019 and Winter 2020
difficulty_reasoning: Upper-level humanities course requiring comfort with theory and social-justice frameworks, but no technical background assumed.

name: Independent Study in Women and Gender Studies Issues
description: Under supervision, students pursue topics in Women and Gender Studies not currently part of the curriculum. For students in the Women and Gender Studies Specialist or Major. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Completion of WGS160Y1, WGS260H1, and WGS360H1, a CGPA of at least 3.0 and permission of the Undergraduate Coordinator.
difficulty_reasoning: Independent study requires self-directed research and disciplinary maturity; assumes mastery of WGS theory from three prerequisites.

name: Independent Study in Women and Gender Studies
description: Under faculty supervision, students pursue topics in Women and Gender Studies that are not currently part of the curriculum. For students in the Women and Gender Studies Specialist or Major. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: Completion of WGS160Y1, WGS260H1, and WGS360H1, a CGPA of at least 3.0 and permission of the Undergraduate Coordinator.
difficulty_reasoning: Independent study requires self-directed research and sustained writing in specialized discipline; demands maturity beyond typical 300-level coursework.

name: Honours Seminar
description: Supervised undergraduate thesis project undertaken in the final year of study. Students attend a bi-weekly seminar to discuss research strategies, analytics, methods and findings. A required course for Specialist students. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1 and 1.0 WGS credit at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Undergraduate thesis demands sustained independent research, writing, and disciplinary maturity beyond typical coursework.

name: Advanced Topics in Gender Theory
description: Senior students may pursue more advanced study in feminist theory. Topics vary from year to year depending on instructor.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1 and 1.0 WGS credit at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Advanced theory course requires sustained engagement with dense feminist scholarship and strong writing skills developed through prior WGS coursework.

name: Special Topics in Gender and the Law
description: Senior students may pursue advanced study in gender and law. Topics vary from year to year.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1, WGS365H1 and an additional 0.5 WGS credit at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Senior seminar demands sustained engagement with specialized legal and gender theory requiring strong foundational WGS knowledge.

name: Community Engagement
description: The application of theoretical study to practical community experience. Advanced Women and Gender Studies students have the opportunity to apply knowledge acquired in the Women and Gender Studies curriculum through a practicum placement within a community organization. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: 2.5 WGS credits including WGS160Y1 and 1.0 WGS credit at the 300+ level
difficulty_reasoning: Practicum placement demands sustained community engagement and applied learning beyond typical coursework; requires self-direction and reflection.

name: Introduction to Academic Writing
description: This course introduces students to the strategies and practices of successful writing at the university and beyond. WRR103H1 challenges students to reflect on and cultivate their strengths as readers and writers as they enter the university. Students will develop their critical reading abilities and written communication skills through meaningful writing projects in diverse genres, including multimodal composition.
difficulty_reasoning: Introductory writing course with no prerequisites, focused on skill-building rather than subject mastery. Regular assignments but manageable scope.

name: Writing Reports
description: Students will learn the fundamentals of report writing, including how to write abstracts and conduct literature reviews as well as qualitative and quantitative research. Students also learn to communicate visually, including how to create tables, charts, and graphs with attention to purpose, audience, structure, style, skills they apply to a formal report and a poster presentation.
difficulty_reasoning: Practical writing skills course with no prerequisites; workload spreads across multiple short assignments and one major report project.

name: Theories of Rhetoric: A Brief History of Persuasion
description: When contemporary critics seek to discredit dishonest politicians, they tend to refer to their discourse as “mere rhetoric.” But there is so much more to rhetoric than deception. This course examines the history of rhetoric, the art of persuasion, from its birth in Greco-Roman antiquity to its rebirth in twentieth and twenty-first century thought. In addition to tracking the history of Western ideas about persuasion, we will bring rhetorical theories to bear on vital questions about philosophy, psychology, media, and advertising.
difficulty_reasoning: History and theory course requiring close reading and essay writing, but no specialized prerequisites or technical skills assumed.

name: Strategic Writing in Business and the Professions: Theory and Practice
description: Students learn the theory and practice of effective and ethical communication in the workplace, including business, government, and non-profit organizations. Students apply ethical reasoning models to case studies. Students have an opportunity to work directly with a community partner, helping them to solve an industry-specific problem or concern. This experiential learning enables students to work together as a team to develop relevant solutions as they strengthen their written and verbal communication skills.
difficulty_reasoning: Theory and case study analysis are accessible, but sustained written output and community project coordination demand consistent effort.

name: Writing in Business and the Professions for Rotman Commerce Students
description: Designed for and restricted to Rotman Commerce undergraduates, the course reflects the program’s learning goals, which include critical thinking, ethical reasoning, and business and professional communication (oral and written). Students apply business communication theory and ethical reasoning models to business cases. Students have an opportunity to work directly with a community partner, helping them to solve an industry-specific problem or concern. This experiential learning enables students to work together as a team to develop relevant solutions as they strengthen their written and verbal communication skills
difficulty_reasoning: Business communication theory and case analysis are accessible to any student, but community project coordination and multiple deliverables demand consistent effort.

name: Digital Rhetoric
description: This course explores the pivotal role that media plays in our culture. Beginning with U of T rhetorician Marshall McLuhan’s far-reaching ideas about media environments, WRR303H1 takes students on a journey through a wide variety of ideas about media, technology, and rhetoric. Topics include the rhetorical dimensions of social media platforms, the strengths and shortcomings of online activism, the emergence of surveillance capitalism, and the operation of persuasion in dating apps.
difficulty_reasoning: No technical prerequisites required; concepts build on general media literacy. Weekly readings and essays are standard for humanities 300-level courses.

name: Rhetoric of Health and Medicine
description: Since its inception, rhetoric has been concerned with persuasion and its relationship to human flourishing. This course brings rhetorical thought into important dialogue with health research, medical practices, and pharmaceutical advertising. Medicalized phenomena—like hypochondria, depression, sexual dysfunction, and death & dying—are all bound up with influence. A rhetorical perspective on health and wellness tracks this influence through networks of individuals, institutions, texts, media forms, genres, and narratives.
exclusions: INI308H1 (Selected Topics in Writing and Rhetoric: Rhetoric of Health and Medicine) offered in Fall 2019, and WRR308H1 (Selected Topics in Writing and Rhetoric: Rhetoric of Health and Medicine) offered in Winter 2022
difficulty_reasoning: Rhetorical analysis is teachable without prior background, but sustained close reading and essay writing demand consistent effort.

name: Stylistic Editing and Copy Editing
description: This course introduces students to professional editorial conventions at two later stages of the editorial process. Both stages require analytical skills and sentence expertise. Through stylistic editing, students learn how to improve a writer’s literary style; through copy editing, they learn how to ensure both accuracy and consistency (editorial style).
difficulty_reasoning: Practical editorial skills are teachable without prior background; consistent practice on real texts builds competence naturally.

name: Writing Literary Journalism
description: This course introduces students to works by some of the most influential literary journalists of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. Classes will be devoted to individual literary-journalistic genres: the personal essay, the profile, the polemic, the memoir, literary reportage, and cultural criticism. Students will look closely at key writers who worked across these genres: George Orwell, James Baldwin, Joan Didion, Joyce Carol Oates. Over the semester, students will develop and write a feature-length work of literary journalism in a genre of their choice, to be refined through peer workshop and instructor feedback.
exclusions: INI308H1 (Selected Topics in Writing and Rhetoric: Writing Literary Journalism), offered in Summer 2021
recommended_preparation: Experience or strong interest in writing journalism.
difficulty_reasoning: Reading literary texts is accessible, but developing original feature-length journalism requires sustained writing and revision skills.

name: Visual Rhetoric
description: This course examines how images and objects communicate with and persuade viewers. Visual rhetoric is part of the broader academic field known as rhetorical studies. This course will introduce students to the “language” of display, exploring questions such as the following: How does physical arrangement, context, and architectural space give voice to the silent object? How are fine art and decorative art objects invested with meaning? Students will be introduced to object-based learning and material culture and learn to analyze and interpret visual grammar in international exhibitions, in auction and exhibition catalogues, in reviews of exhibits, and in museum collections. Objects will include ceramics, jewellery, interiors, architecture, and fashion in the eighteenth, nineteenth, and twentieth centuries.
difficulty_reasoning: Visual analysis is approachable without prior background; moderate weekly readings and essays demand consistent engagement but no specialist knowledge.

name: Style in Creative, Professional, and Academic Writing
description: This course examines features of style and rhetoric in creative writing (fiction and non-fiction prose), professional writing (including AI-assisted writing), and academic writing (published articles, academic blogs, and research-informed social media). A series of questions will frame course themes and concepts. What are the rhetorical effects of the strategies of storytelling observable in the non-fiction prose of fiction writers? What are the unspoken rules and observable patterns in the products of those who write in the professional workplace? What is the impact of AI on the production and reliability of knowledge in the research setting and beyond?
exclusions: WRR308H1 (Style in Creative, Professional, and Academic Writing), offered in Fall 2025
difficulty_reasoning: Writing-focused course with accessible concepts but requires sustained analytical reading and multiple essay drafts throughout semester.

name: Developmental and Substantive Editing
description: This course introduces professional editorial conventions at two early stages of the editorial process. Both stages require editors to think critically and creatively as they assess content, organization, and argument. Students learn how to analyze and evaluate these elements, envision possible improvements, and explain these suggestions persuasively.
difficulty_reasoning: Practical editorial skills are teachable through examples and hands-on practice without requiring deep subject expertise or advanced prerequisites.

name: Independent Studies in Writing and Rhetoric
description: Independent research projects devised by students and supervised by the Writing and Rhetoric staff. Open only to students who are completing the Minor Program in Writing and Rhetoric Program. Applications should be submitted to the Program Director by June 1 for a Fall session course or by November 1 for a Spring session course. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit from WRR103H1/WRR104H1, WRR201H1, WRR203H1, WRR303H1, WRR305H1, WRR413H1, WRR414H1
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research requires self-direction and disciplinary maturity; students must design, execute, and defend original writing projects with minimal scaffolding.

name: Independent Studies in Writing and Rhetoric
description: Independent research projects devised by students and supervised by the Writing and Rhetoric staff. Open only to students who are completing the Minor Program in Writing and Rhetoric Program. Applications should be submitted to the Program Director by June 1 for a Fall session course or by November 1 for a Spring session course. Not eligible for CR/NCR option.
prerequisites: 1.0 credit from WRR103H1/WRR104H1, WRR201H1, WRR203H1, WRR303H1, WRR305H1, WRR413H1, WRR414H1
difficulty_reasoning: Independent research project requires self-directed investigation, disciplinary maturity, and sustained output typical of advanced undergraduate work.

name: Writing for Social Change
description: We will read and write a variety of texts focused on understanding and effecting social change. Students will be encouraged to engage with different theories of social change and an array of writing genres, ranging from journalism to critical theory to fiction. Ultimately, students will focus on one or several key social issues that they wish to write about for their final project.
difficulty_reasoning: Regular reading and writing assignments with accessible social theory; final project requires sustained effort but is self-directed.
